   With \(\phi(x)=h(x)+L\), this gives \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\), and since \(\phi\) is increasing, \(\phi(x)>x\). The span bound follows by taking a maximum on one fundamental interval and using
   \[
   h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x).
   \]

3. **Patch for PL embeddedness.**  
   Replace the compressed embeddedness paragraph by the following lemma.

   Let \(E\) be the compact quotient Sadowsky band, with seams included among the chosen rulings. In ruling coordinates on each smooth piece,
   \[
   \Psi(u,v)=((1-v)u+v h(u),vc),\qquad 0\le v\le1,
   \]
   and, because each ruling maps linearly to a straight segment,
   \[
   E(\Psi(u,v))=(1-v)b(u)+v t(u),
   \]
   where \(b(u)=E(u,0)\) and \(t(u)=E(h(u),c)\) are \(C^1\) on each piece and have matching first derivatives at seams. For partitions with \(\max |u_{i+1}-u_i|\to0\), the affine interpolation over each triangular half of the strip \([u_i,u_{i+1}]\times[0,1]\) converges to \(E\) in \(C^1\) in these ruling coordinates: finite differences of \(b,t\) converge uniformly to \(b',t'\), while the \(v\)-direction derivative is controlled by \(t(u)-b(u)\).

   Now use compact \(C^1\)-stability of embeddings. Cover the quotient surface by finitely many ruling-coordinate neighborhoods on which, after a rigid motion, \(E\) is a \(C^1\) graph over a plane and the projection to that plane is quantitatively bilipschitz. For sufficiently fine partition, the projected PL map has derivative uniformly close to the projected derivative of \(E\), hence is injective on each smaller neighborhood. Choose a neighborhood \(U\) of the diagonal in \(M\times M\) covered by these local injectivity neighborhoods. Since \(E\) is embedded and \(M\times M\setminus U\) is compact, there is \(\eta>0\) such that
   \[
   |E(p)-E(q)|>\eta\qquad ((p,q)\notin U).
   \]
   Uniform convergence \(\|F_0-E\|_\infty<\eta/3\) rules out nonlocal intersections, and local injectivity rules out intersections inside \(U\). Thus the quotient PL map is embedded, and the lifted map is quotient-injective.
parsed.problem_solved: As written, the proof gives a complete lower bound argument: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\). The upper bound is established conditional on the Sadowsky construction lemma and the PL embeddedness/stability details. After inserting the routine patches above, the proof establishes that the realized set is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), hence its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
claim_verified: After inserting the listed routine patches, the proof establishes the stronger statement that the realized numbers are exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and hence their infimum is \(\sqrt3\). As written without those patches, the lower bound is essentially complete but relies on compressed triangulation/leaf-graph details, and the upper bound is conditional on the fully justified Sadowsky-band and PL secant-embeddedness lemmas.
solution_verified:
## Claim

The realized numbers are exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Consequently the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

---

## 1. The lower bound

Fix \(\beta>0\), write \(G=G_\beta\), and suppose \(\beta\) is realized by a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) and a \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\). We prove \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

### 1.1. Basic structure of clean triangulations

Every clean triangle has exactly one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it its **boundary edge**. The other two edges join the two boundary components and will be called **transverse edges**.

**Lemma 1.** The triangulation is face-to-face: distinct edges have disjoint relative interiors; every transverse edge is incident to exactly two triangles; every boundary edge is incident to exactly one triangle.

**Proof.** Two transverse edges cannot cross transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\). At an interior crossing, each incident triangle occupies a one-sided open half-neighborhood of its edge, and two such half-neighborhoods necessarily overlap in an open sector, contradicting disjointness of triangle interiors.

If two transverse edges are collinear and overlap in more than one point, then they are the same edge, because a non-horizontal line meets the two boundary lines \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in exactly two points.

Boundary edges cannot overlap in a nontrivial interval: at an interior point of such an overlap, both incident triangles contain a small inward half-disk, again contradicting disjointness of interiors. Similarly, an endpoint of a transverse edge cannot lie in the relative interior of a boundary edge, because the triangle having the boundary edge contains a small inward half-disk there, while the triangle having the transverse edge contributes a nonempty sector inside that half-disk.

Thus intersections of triangles are unions of common faces. Now take an interior point of a transverse edge. A small disk around it is cut into two sides by the edge. At most one triangle can occupy each side, and the union condition forces one triangle on each side. Hence the edge is incident to exactly two triangles. The same local half-disk argument at an interior point of a boundary edge gives exactly one incident triangle. ∎

### 1.2. Passing to the Möbius-band quotient

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
M_\beta=[0,\beta]\times[0,1]/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y),
\]
so \(M_\beta\) is a compact Möbius band whose boundary is a circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

The realization condition implies \(f\circ G=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
Moreover, if \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), hence \(p=G^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), so \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need that the quotient triangulation is finite. First, \(f\) is injective on each triangle. Indeed, if \(f|_\tau\) had affine rank \(<2\), then arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\tau\) would satisfy \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would give \(p=G^k(q)\) with \(k\neq0\), but every nontrivial \(G^k\) moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\), impossible for \(p,q\) sufficiently close.

Hence no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in\tau\cap G^k\tau\), then \(z=G^k w\) for some \(w\in\tau\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Since \(f|_\tau\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting freeness of the \(G\)-action unless \(k=0\).

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, and every \(G\)-orbit of triangles has a representative meeting that rectangle. Therefore the induced triangulation of \(M_\beta\) has finitely many triangles.

### 1.3. Cevian lengthening

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is the boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(0\le s\le1\), define
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1.
\]
Set
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two transverse edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
So every segment from the apex of a clean triangle to a point of its boundary edge is strictly lengthened by \(f\). In particular, its image is nondegenerate.

### 1.4. The leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider the one-parameter family of segments joining the apex to the opposite boundary edge. Call these segments **leaves**. The endpoint leaves of the family are precisely the two transverse edges of the triangle.

For the finite quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\), form a graph \(\Lambda_M\) as follows. Each triangle contributes an interval parametrizing its leaves. Whenever two triangles share a transverse edge, glue the corresponding endpoints of their leaf intervals. By Lemma 1, every transverse edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\).

We claim that \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose leaf intervals belong to \(C\). The set \(U_C\) is open. Indeed, this is clear at interior points of triangles and at interior points of boundary edges. At an interior point of a transverse edge, the two incident triangles lie in the same component because their leaf intervals are glued.

It remains only to check boundary vertices. Let \(v\) be a boundary vertex. Choose a sufficiently small half-disk \(U\) around \(v\) meeting only triangles incident to \(v\). Radial projection from \(v\) sends the incident triangle sectors to a finite subdivision of a semicircle. Since the triangles cover a neighborhood of \(v\) and have disjoint interiors, every complementary sector is occupied by exactly one triangle. Consecutive sectors share a radial edge from \(v\), and every such interior radial edge is a transverse edge. Hence all triangles incident to \(v\) lie in the same component of the leaf graph. Thus \(U_C\) contains a neighborhood of each of its boundary vertices.

The complement of \(U_C\) is a union of similar sets coming from other components, hence is also open. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. A finite connected \(2\)-regular graph is a circle:
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same construction upstairs gives a locally finite leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). The same local-link argument shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The deck transformation \(G\) acts on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), and the quotient graph is \(\Lambda_M\). Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a connected covering of a circle.

It is not a finite covering. Indeed, suppose some nonzero power \(G^k\) fixed a lifted leaf. Write the leaf as the segment with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
If \(k=2n\) is even, then \(G^k\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so fixing the leaf forces \(2n\beta=0\), hence \(k=0\). If \(k=2n+1\) is odd, then \(G^k\) interchanges the two boundary components, and fixing the leaf forces simultaneously
\[
t=(2n+1)\beta,\qquad t=-(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Thus no nontrivial deck power fixes a leaf. Therefore the connected covering is infinite:
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong \mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By the cevian lengthening lemma, its image is a nondegenerate straight segment, hence determines an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line varies continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

If \(\ell\) is a lifted leaf, then \(G\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has the same image segment with the opposite orientation, because \(G\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G=f\). Therefore along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G\ell\), the oriented support line travels from some oriented line to the same unoriented line with the opposite orientation.

### 1.5. A lemma on oriented lines

Represent an oriented line in \(\mathbb R^3\) by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

**Lemma 2.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist two distinct parameters \(s_1,s_2\) for which the corresponding unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(L_s\), and extend them to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, with
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Assume for contradiction that \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then normalization gives a continuous map
\[
\Phi:S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1.
\]
On \(\delta=0\), \(\Phi\) is constant. But on \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\Phi\left(s+\frac12,\frac12\right)=-\Phi\left(s,\frac12\right).
\]
A map \(\psi:S^1\to S^1\) satisfying \(\psi(s+\frac12)=-\psi(s)\) has odd degree: after lifting to an angle function, the angle changes over a half-period by an odd multiple of \(\pi\), hence over a full period by an odd multiple of \(2\pi\). This contradicts the fact that \(\psi\) is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map at \(\delta=0\). Therefore \((a,b)\) vanishes somewhere.

At a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two oriented lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
When \(u\perp v\), the condition \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar, hence intersect. ∎

Applying Lemma 2 to the path of support lines along a fundamental interval of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), we obtain two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\in\mathbb R^3\).

### 1.6. Geometry of lifted leaves

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Its flat length is
\[
|J|_0=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

We first prove
\[
|t|<\beta.
\]
If \(t=\beta\), then the two endpoints of \(J\) are identified by \(G\), so their images coincide, contradicting the fact that \(f(J)\) has positive length. Similarly, \(t=-\beta\) is impossible.

If \(t>\beta\), translate horizontally and suppose \(J\) has endpoints \((0,0)\) and \((t,1)\). Then \(GJ\) has endpoints \((\beta,1)\) and \((t+\beta,0)\). The two segments \(J\) and \(GJ\) intersect at height
\[
y=\frac{t+\beta}{2t}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus two distinct points of \(J\) are identified by \(G\), so their images under \(f\) coincide, contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on the nondegenerate segment \(J\). The case \(t<-\beta\) is identical using \(G^{-1}\). Hence \(|t|<\beta\).

Parametrize the boundary circle of \(M_\beta\) by a coordinate modulo \(2\beta\): a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\), while a top point \((v,1)\) has coordinate \(v-\beta\). Thus the endpoints of a leaf
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1)
\]
cut the quotient boundary into two arcs of flat lengths
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t.
\]

We also record the following boundary-length fact. Since the quotient triangulation is finite, the boundary circle is subdivided into finitely many boundary edges. On each boundary edge \(e\), \(F|_e\) is affine with contraction factor \(\lambda_e<1\). Hence any nontrivial boundary arc \(\gamma\), decomposed into finitely many subsegments \(I_j\subset e_j\), satisfies
\[
\ell_F(\gamma)=\sum_j \lambda_{e_j}|I_j|
<
\sum_j |I_j|
=\ell_0(\gamma).
\]
So every nontrivial boundary arc is strictly shortened in length by \(F\).

### 1.7. The metric inequality

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the two quotient leaves whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at \(P\).

The relative interiors of distinct leaves in \(M_\beta\) are disjoint. Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. Hence at least one of the two image segments lies entirely in a closed ray from \(P\) along its support line. Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line} F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By the cevian lengthening lemma,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge 1.
\]
Therefore, writing
\[
y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T)),
\]
we have
\[
y>1.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+t,1).
\]
Replacing the lift by its \(G\)-translate changes \(t\) to \(-t\). We choose the lift so that the boundary arc \(D\) containing \(q\) has flat length
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Let \(H\) be the other boundary arc, so
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Let
\[
L=|F(T)|.
\]
By the cevian lengthening lemma,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

The boundary arc \(H\) connects the two endpoints of \(F(T)\). Therefore
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

Now the boundary arc \(D\) also connects the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and it passes through \(Q\). If \(A,C\) are the endpoints of \(F(T)\), then
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\) and \(|A-C|=L\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary contraction gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Thus no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## 2. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

### 2.1. Sadowsky bands

We use the following standard explicit construction.

**Lemma 3.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there exists an embedded \(C^{1,1}\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), developable Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), equipped with a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. In lifted form, there is a map
\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that
\[
E_\mu(X+\mu,1-Y)=E_\mu(X,Y),
\]
the quotient map is embedded, each full-width ruling maps affinely and isometrically to a straight segment, and the boundary curves are parametrized by arclength. Moreover the construction extends to a slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) ruled band.

**Proof.** Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction bends a flat rectangular strip over three circular rods of diameters \(d,d,2d\). The surface consists cyclically of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces. Each piece is an isometric image of its flat development, and adjacent pieces meet along common generators with matching tangent planes. Hence the resulting surface is developable, \(C^1\), piecewise \(C^\infty\), and in fact \(C^{1,1}\).

Let the strip length be \(\mu\), and let \(b\) be the half-width. The elementary non-overlap computation for the three-cylinder geometry gives the maximal allowed half-width
\[
b_{\max}=\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Choose
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]
Then
\[
b_{\max}>\frac12.
\]
Thus a strip of half-width \(1/2\), i.e. width \(1\), lies strictly inside the embedded range.

Because the inequality is strict, there is \(\eta>0\) such that the same construction remains embedded for half-width \(1/2+\eta\). Restricting that wider embedded band to the central width-one subband gives the desired band, with a slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) extension.

The cylinder generators and their planar continuations give a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. Since the construction is an isometric bending of a flat rectangle, the boundary curves are parametrized by arclength. Passing to the infinite lift gives the stated map \(E_\mu\) satisfying
\[
E_\mu(X+\mu,1-Y)=E_\mu(X,Y).
\]
∎

### 2.2. A PL secant approximation lemma

Let \(c,L>0\), and define
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]
on \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\).

We need a PL approximation of a ruled embedded band which remains embedded on the quotient.

**Lemma 4.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be the lift of a scaled Sadowsky band as in Lemma 3, invariant under \(H_{L,c}\). Suppose the full-width rulings are
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]
Fix \(x_0\), put
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L,
\]
and set
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exists an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\) iff \(P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\);
2. boundary edges are nonexpanded;
3. every transverse edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]
4. every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Because the rulings form a noncrossing foliation of the strip, \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is a strictly increasing homeomorphism. The map \(H_{L,c}\) sends \(R_x\) to \(R_{\phi(x)}\), where
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
The top endpoint relation gives
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L,
\]
and hence
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]

Also,
\[
|h(x)-x|<L.
\]
Indeed, if \(h(x)-x=L\), then the two endpoints of \(R_x\) are identified by \(H_{L,c}\), contradicting the positive length of the ruling image. If \(h(x)-x>L\), then \(R_x\) intersects \(H_{L,c}(R_x)\) in an interior point, producing two distinct points of the quotient with the same image under the embedded band. The case \(h(x)-x<-L\) is identical using \(H_{L,c}^{-1}\). Therefore \(\phi(x)>x\).

Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]
Choose a partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
fine enough that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]
for all \(i\), and also fine enough that all seams between planar and cylindrical pieces occur among the rulings \(R_{x_i}\). Extend the sequence by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Put
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The four vertices \(B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\) bound a clean quadrilateral \(Q_i\). The quadrilaterals \(Q_i\) tile the strip.

For \(0\le i<N\), triangulate \(Q_i\) by the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), and extend the choice \(H_{L,c}\)-invariantly. Thus the diagonal choice alternates under \(H_{L,c}\) and repeats under \(H_{L,c}^2\). This gives an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. Since \(E\) is \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant and the triangulation is \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant, \(F_0\) is \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant.

Boundary edges are nonexpanded because their images are chords of boundary arcs parametrized by arclength.

For transverse edges, first consider a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\). Since \(E\) maps rulings isometrically,
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Now consider a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(|T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le |T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&> r-\rho \\
&> d-2\rho \\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The opposite diagonal type is handled similarly using the lower boundary increment. Thus every transverse edge is shortened by at most \(\varepsilon\).

The horizontal span of a ruling edge is at most \(S\), and the horizontal span of a diagonal is at most \(S+\rho<D\). Hence every transverse edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

It remains to ensure quotient-injectivity. Because the Sadowsky band has a slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) extension, the compact quotient of the width-\(c\) band lies in the interior of a compact embedded \(C^{1,1}\) surface. Such a compact \(C^{1,1}\) embedded surface has positive reach in a neighborhood of the compact subband: locally this follows from graph charts with Lipschitz tangent planes, and globally from compact embeddedness separating non-nearby points. Hence there is a tubular neighborhood on which the nearest-point projection \(\pi\) to the wider surface is uniquely defined.

As the mesh of the partition tends to \(0\), the secant PL maps converge uniformly to \(E\). On planar pieces the approximation is exact, because \(E\) is affine there. On cylindrical pieces the approximation replaces circular arcs between nearby generators by chords; the chord sagitta is \(O(\Delta x^2)\) and the tangent-plane error is \(O(\Delta x)\). Since seams are among the partition rulings and adjacent tangent planes match, the same estimates hold uniformly across the whole quotient.

For sufficiently fine partitions, \(F_0\) lies inside the tubular neighborhood. Let \(E^+\) denote the wider embedding and define on the quotient
\[
P=(E^+)^{-1}\circ \pi\circ F_0.
\]
Then \(P\) is piecewise \(C^1\)-close to the inclusion of the original quotient band into the wider parameter band.

For sufficiently small \(C^1\)-error, \(P\) is locally injective in finitely many coordinate charts. Uniform \(C^0\)-closeness to the inclusion prevents collisions between pairs of points outside those local charts: compactness gives a positive separation for such pairs under the inclusion, and the small \(C^0\)-error preserves that separation. Hence \(P\) is injective.

If \(F_0(p)=F_0(q)\) on the quotient, then \(P(p)=P(q)\), so \(p=q\). Thus the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting back to \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) gives
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). ∎

### 2.3. Realizing every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Fix
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
Let \(E_\mu\) be the width-one, length-\(\mu\) Sadowsky band from Lemma 3. Let \(S_\mu\) be the corresponding ruling-span bound over a fundamental interval, and choose
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]
Set
\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu},\qquad L=a\beta.
\]
Then
\[
c>1,\qquad L=c\mu.
\]

Scale the Sadowsky band by the factor \(c\). This gives a width-\(c\), length-\(L\) ruled embedded band invariant under
\[
H_{L,c}(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Its ruling-span bound is \(cS_\mu\), so \(cD_\mu\) is an admissible span bound.

Choose \(a\) close enough to \(1\) so that
\[
m:=\min_{0\le u\le D_\mu}
\left[
c\sqrt{1+u^2}
-
\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1}
\right]>0.
\]
This is possible because when \(a=1\), one has \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), and therefore
\[
c\sqrt{1+u^2}>\sqrt{c^2u^2+1}
\]
for every \(u\). By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\), the minimum remains positive for all \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\).

Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]
Apply Lemma 4 to the scaled band with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]
We obtain an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H_{L,c}\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with the four properties listed in Lemma 4.

Now define the affine map
\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=A(x+\beta,1-y)
=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=H_{L,c}(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Set
\[
f=F_0\circ A,\qquad \mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0).
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on each triangle.

We verify the squeeze inequalities.

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A\) multiplies its flat length by \(a\), and \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges. Hence
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a transverse edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let the horizontal span of \(A(p)A(q)\) be \(t\). By Lemma 4,
\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cu\) with \(0\le u<D_\mu\). Then
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=\sqrt{t^2+c^2}=c\sqrt{1+u^2},
\]
while
\[
|p-q|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)^2+1}
=\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1}.
\]
Using Lemma 4 and the choice of \(\varepsilon<m\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
|f(p)-f(q)|
&=|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)| \\
&\ge |A(p)-A(q)|-\varepsilon \\
&=c\sqrt{1+u^2}-\varepsilon \\
&>\sqrt{\left(\frac{cu}{a}\right)^2+1} \\
&=|p-q|.
\end{aligned}
\]
Thus every transverse edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally, for any \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow Ap=H_{L,c}^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z \\
&\Longleftrightarrow p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H_{L,c}\).

Therefore every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

Combining the lower and upper bounds, the realized numbers are exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Hence their infimum is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]

The proof must explicitly avoid boundary issues by applying it only on an interior compact subband of a slightly wider band. ([www-sop.inria.fr](https://www-sop.inria.fr/members/Mathijs.Wintraecken/Papers/convsup.pdf))
</CITATION_AUDIT>

<MAJOR_GAPS>
- (none)
</MAJOR_GAPS>

<MINOR_GAPS>
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The leaf-graph/cutting argument in the lower bound is essentially correct but should explicitly prove that the upstairs action on the leaf graph is free before calling the quotient map a covering, and should justify the classification of the connected cover of \(S^1\).
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The metric step using \(|AQ|+|QC|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}\), and the choice of the boundary arc \(D\) through \(q\), need a short inserted proof.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 3 needs the Sadowsky construction cited/expanded into the exact form required: embedded width-one band for every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), \(C^{1,1}\) regularity, continuous full-width ruling foliation, arclength boundary, and slightly wider embedded extension.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 4’s proof of \(|h(x)-x|<L\) has an inaccurate quotient-injectivity wording; the contradiction should instead use isometry/injectivity on a single ruling.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 4’s PL embeddedness argument is too compressed. It needs a precise tubular-neighborhood/positive-reach statement and a quantitative \(C^0\)/piecewise-\(C^1\) secant-approximation argument implying the projected PL map is injective.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — In the upper-bound verification of transverse edges, “horizontal span” should be explicitly interpreted as absolute horizontal span \(t=|\Delta X|\), so \(t\ge0\).
</MINOR_GAPS>

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
**Patch 1: leaf-graph covering.**  
Move the “no nontrivial deck power fixes a lifted leaf” calculation before the sentence asserting that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is a covering. If a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x,0)\), \((x+t,1)\), then \(G^{2n}\) fixes it only if \(2n\beta=0\), and \(G^{2n+1}\) fixes it only if simultaneously \(t=(2n+1)\beta\) and \(t=-(2n+1)\beta\), impossible. Thus the \(\mathbb Z\)-action on leaves is free. Therefore the quotient map is a genuine graph covering. Since \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\), a connected cover is either finite circular or infinite linear. If it were finite, some nonzero \(G^k\) would fix a point in a finite fiber, hence a lifted leaf, contradicting freeness. So \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\).

**Patch 2: metric inequality.**  
After defining \(A,C\) as endpoints of \(F(T)\), place coordinates so \(A=(-L/2,0)\), \(C=(L/2,0)\) in a plane containing \(Q\), and write \(Q=(s,y)\), where \(y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}AC)\). Then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
=\sqrt{(s+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(s-L/2)^2+y^2}
\ge 2\sqrt{(L/2)^2+y^2}
=\sqrt{L^2+4y^2},
\]
with the minimum at \(s=0\). Also, since \(y>1\), \(Q\) is not one of the endpoints of \(F(T)\), so the boundary arc \(D\) genuinely splits at \(q\) and its image length is at least \(|A-Q|+|Q-C|\).

**Patch 3: Sadowsky-band lemma.**  
Insert the verified Sadowsky construction as follows. For \(\mu>\sqrt3\), choose
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]
Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction with midline length \(l=\mu\) and rod diameters \(d,d,2d\) has allowable half-width
\[
b_{\max}=\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}>\frac12.
\]
Choose \(\eta>0\) with \(1/2+\eta<b_{\max}\). The width-\(1+2\eta\) strip is embedded; restricting to the central width-one strip gives the required band and the wider extension. The surface is assembled from planar and cylindrical pieces meeting along common generators with matching tangent planes; bounded piecewise second derivatives and matching first jets imply \(C^{1,1}\). The cylinder generators and their planar continuations give the full-width straight ruling foliation, and because the construction is an isometric bending of the flat rectangle, boundary lines are arclength-parametrized. ([researchgate.net](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/264742939_Translation_of_Michael_Sadowsky%27s_Paper_An_Elementary_Proof_for_the_Existence_of_a_Developable_MOBIUS_Band_and_the_Attribution_of_the_Geometric_Problem_to_a_Variational_Problem))

**Patch 4: correction in Lemma 4 for \(|h(x)-x|<L\).**  
If \(h(x)-x=L\), the two endpoints of the ruling \(R_x\) are related by \(H_{L,c}\), so \(E\) sends them to the same point, contradicting that \(E|_{R_x}\) is an isometry on a positive-length segment. If \(h(x)-x>L\), then \(R_x\) and \(H_{L,c}(R_x)\) cross in the flat strip. Thus there are distinct points \(w,z\in R_x\) with \(z=H_{L,c}(w)\). Since \(E\circ H_{L,c}=E\), \(E(z)=E(w)\), again contradicting the isometry of \(E|_{R_x}\). The case \(h(x)-x<-L\) is identical using \(H_{L,c}^{-1}\). Hence \(|h(x)-x|<L\), and \(\phi(x)=h(x)+L>x\).

**Patch 5: PL secant embeddedness in Lemma 4.**  
Let \(K\) be the compact quotient of the width-\(c\) band and let \(K^+\) be the quotient of the slightly wider embedded \(C^{1,1}\) band. Since \(K\Subset \operatorname{int}K^+\), compactness and \(C^{1,1}\) embeddedness give a uniform tubular neighborhood \(U\) of \(E(K)\) in which nearest-point projection \(\pi:U\to E(K^+)\) is single-valued and Lipschitz. For sufficiently fine partitions, the PL secant map \(F_0\) lies in \(U\). Define
\[
P=(E^+)^{-1}\circ \pi\circ F_0:K\to K^+.
\]
On each planar or cylindrical piece, with seams included among the selected rulings, the secant interpolation converges uniformly to \(E\), and in the ruling/boundary directions its derivative differs from \(DE\) by \(O(\delta)\), where \(\delta\) is the maximum bottom/top boundary mesh. This follows because \(E\) is affine along rulings and \(C^{1,1}\) transversely; planar pieces are exact and cylindrical pieces have chord sagitta \(O(\delta^2)\) and tangent error \(O(\delta)\). Hence \(P\to\operatorname{id}_K\) uniformly and piecewise \(C^1\), with \(\|DP-I\|<1/2\) in finitely many parameter charts for \(\delta\) small.

In each sufficiently small convex coordinate chart, the a.e. derivative bound gives
\[
|P(u)-P(v)|\ge \tfrac12 |u-v|,
\]
so \(P\) is locally injective. For pairs outside these small charts, compactness of \(K\times K\setminus\{\text{near diagonal}\}\) gives a positive separation under the identity map; uniform \(C^0\)-closeness preserves that separation. Therefore \(P\) is globally injective. If \(F_0(p)=F_0(q)\), then \(P(p)=P(q)\), so \(p=q\) on the quotient. Lifting gives
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H_{L,c}^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

**Patch 6: absolute horizontal span.**  
In §2.3, replace “Let the horizontal span be \(t\)” by
\[
t=|\Delta X(Ap,Aq)|,\qquad 0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]
Then write \(t=cu\) with \(0\le u<D_\mu\). The subsequent inequalities are unchanged.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
claim_verified: As written, the proof gives a complete lower bound argument: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\). The upper bound is established conditional on the Sadowsky construction lemma and the PL embeddedness/stability details. After inserting the routine patches above, the proof establishes that the realized set is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), hence its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).
solution_verified:
## Theorem

A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Hence the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

Throughout, \(|\cdot|\) denotes Euclidean length in the flat strip or in \(\mathbb R^3\), according to context.

---

## 1. The lower bound

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\). We prove \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

### 1.1. A face-to-face refinement

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+s r_1\), define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2 .
\]
The two nonboundary edges are lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Thus every cevian from the opposite vertex \(a\) to the boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

We may refine \(\mathcal T\) so that it is face-to-face. Indeed, two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\), because near a transverse crossing one of the incident triangles on one edge would overlap an incident triangle on the other edge. If two nonboundary edges overlap in a nontrivial segment, then they lie on the same line; since each joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), they have the same endpoints and hence coincide.

Thus the only non-face-to-face phenomenon is that boundary vertices may subdivide boundary edges. For each triangle, insert all boundary vertices lying on its boundary edge. Local finiteness makes this a finite subdivision on each edge. Replacing the triangle by the corresponding smaller clean triangles gives a locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant face-to-face clean triangulation. The old map \(f\) is still affine on every new triangle. New boundary edges are subsegments of old boundary edges and are shortened; new nonboundary edges are cevians and are lengthened by the preceding paragraph.

Hence we assume from now on that \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face. In particular, every compact boundary arc is mapped by \(f\) to a PL path of strictly smaller length.

### 1.2. Passing to the quotient Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle
=([0,\beta]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\). Since \(f(p)=f(G_\beta p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The orbit condition in the definition of realization says exactly that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), nontrivial orbit-related points are at distance at least \(\beta\) in \(\Sigma\): even powers translate by \(2n\beta\), and odd powers translate horizontally by \((2n+1)\beta\) while reversing \(y\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some triangle, then, because it is affine, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

Consequently no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(w=G_\beta^{-k}z\in\Delta\), and \(f(z)=f(w)\). Injectivity on \(\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), impossible because \(G_\beta^k\) has no fixed point for \(k\neq0\).

Every triangle orbit has a representative meeting \([0,2\beta]\times[0,1]\), and local finiteness gives only finitely many such triangles. Therefore the quotient has a finite face-to-face clean triangulation, and \(F\) is affine and injective on every quotient triangle.

### 1.3. The circle of leaves

In each clean triangle, take the family of cevians from the opposite vertex to the boundary edge. Gluing the endpoints corresponding to common nonboundary edges gives a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), whose points parameterize the leaf segments in \(M_\beta\).

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence each connected component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

The graph is connected. If \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles corresponding to edges of \(C\). Whenever two triangles share a nonboundary edge, their intervals in \(\Lambda_M\) meet at a vertex, so they belong to the same component. Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary sides; hence all triangles in the fan belong to the same component. Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\).

The lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is similarly connected. The quotient map \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is a covering. No nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) fixes a lifted leaf: if a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1),
\]
then an even power translates both endpoints horizontally, while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
s=(2m+1)\beta,\qquad s=-(2m+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Thus \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is an infinite connected covering of a circle, so \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\).

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image is a nondegenerate straight segment, because every leaf is lengthened. Hence each leaf determines an oriented line in \(\mathbb R^3\), varying continuously with the leaf. If \(\ell\) is a lifted leaf, then \(G_\beta\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has the same image segment with the opposite orientation. Therefore along an interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\), we get a continuous path of oriented lines \(L_t\), \(0\le t\le1\), satisfying
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

### 1.4. An oriented-line lemma

We need the following elementary fact.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_t\), \(0\le t\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) with \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then two distinct lines in the family are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and \(m=p\times u\) for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous representatives \((u(t),m(t))\) and extend them by
\[
(u(t+1),m(t+1))=-(u(t),m(t)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and
\[
(a,b)(s+\delta,1-\delta)=-(a,b)(s,\delta).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a map from the cylinder \(S^1\times[0,1]\) to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. On \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\phi\left(s+\frac12,\frac12\right)=-\phi\left(s,\frac12\right),
\]
so the middle circle has odd degree. But it is homotopic in the cylinder to the constant bottom circle, contradiction. Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a point, \(a=0\) says the directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the lines are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says they are coplanar. Therefore they intersect. \(\square\)

Applying the lemma, we obtain two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at some point \(P\).

### 1.5. Boundary arc lengths of a leaf

Let \(J\) be a lifted leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Then
\[
|J|=\sqrt{1+s^2}.
\]

We claim \(|s|<\beta\). If \(s=\beta\), then the endpoints are related by \(G_\beta\), so their images are equal, contradicting that \(J\) is lengthened. The case \(s=-\beta\) is identical.

If \(s>\beta\), then \(J\) intersects \(G_\beta J\) in its relative interior. Indeed, after translating coordinates, take \(J\) from \((0,0)\) to \((s,1)\). The segment \(G_\beta J\) runs from \((s+\beta,0)\) to \((\beta,1)\), and the two segments meet at height
\[
y=\frac{s+\beta}{2s}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus two distinct points of \(J\) are \(G_\beta\)-related and have the same image, contradicting injectivity on \(J\). The case \(s<-\beta\) follows using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Hence \(|s|<\beta\).

In the quotient boundary circle, the two endpoints of \(J\) split the boundary into arcs of flat lengths
\[
\beta-s,\qquad \beta+s.
\]
Thus for any quotient leaf, if the two boundary arcs between its endpoints are called \(D\) and \(H\), then for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\),
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
and the flat length of the leaf is
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

### 1.6. The metric contradiction

Let \(T\) and \(B\) be the two quotient leaves found above, with perpendicular intersecting support lines. Their image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors, because \(F\) is embedded and distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors. Therefore at least one of them lies in a closed ray starting at \(P\). Rename the leaves so that this one is \(B\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put \(Q=F(q)\). Since the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) is lengthened, so
\[
|F(B)|>|B|\ge1.
\]
Thus
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))>1.
\]

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc between the endpoints of \(T\) that contains \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Because \(T\) is lengthened,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
The arc \(H\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\), and boundary arcs are strictly shortened, so
\[
L\le \ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

The arc \(D\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
For a point \(Q\) at distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2},
\]
with equality when the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) is the midpoint of \(AC\). Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
But \(D\) is strictly shortened, so
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}<\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\]
and hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Therefore every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

---

## 2. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

### 2.1. Sadowsky’s three-cylinder Möbius band

We use the following classical construction of Sadowsky. For a rectangle of length \(l\) and half-width \(b\), Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction gives a \(C^1\) embedded developable Möbius band made from three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces whenever
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}
\]
for some cylinder parameter \(d>0\). The construction is obtained by Euclidean motions on planar pieces and by the standard unrolling isometry on cylindrical pieces; adjacent pieces meet tangent-to-tangent along full-width generator segments. Thus the intrinsic flat metric is preserved, the surface is ruled by full-width straight generators, and strict inequality gives positive separation of nonadjacent compact pieces. This is Sadowsky’s 1930 construction; the English translation by Hinz and Fried records the three-planar/three-semicylindrical construction and the original publication data. ([ar5iv.org](https://ar5iv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

Taking \(l=\mu\), \(b=1/2\), and
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}
\]
gives, for every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), a width-one embedded path-isometric ruled Möbius band
\[
I_\mu:M_{\mu,1}\to\mathbb R^3,
\qquad
M_{\mu,1}=([0,\mu]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\mu,1-y).
\]
Let \(E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3\) be its lift. Then
\[
E_\mu\circ H_{\mu,1}=E_\mu,
\qquad
H_{\mu,1}(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y),
\]
and the lift is quotient-injective:
\[
E_\mu(P)=E_\mu(Q)
\iff
P=H_{\mu,1}^k(Q)
\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives the same construction on
\[
\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\]
with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\qquad L=\mu c.
\]

### 2.2. Ruling parametrization

Let \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\) be such a scaled lifted Sadowsky band. Its lifted ruling foliation consists of disjoint straight segments joining \(y=0\) to \(y=c\). Therefore each bottom point \((x,0)\) lies on a unique ruling whose top endpoint is \((h(x),c)\). Write
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]

If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), then the two straight segments \(R_{x_1}\) and \(R_{x_2}\) would meet, contradicting that they are leaves of a foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing. Every top boundary point belongs to a ruling, so \(h\) is onto. Thus \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is an increasing homeomorphism.

Since
\[
H(x,0)=(x+L,c),\qquad H(h(x),c)=(h(x)+L,0),
\]
the image of \(R_x\) under \(H\), reoriented bottom-to-top, is \(R_{h(x)+L}\). Hence
\[
h(h(x)+L)=x+L.
\]
Define
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
Then
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
Since \(\phi\) is increasing, this implies \(\phi(x)>x\) for all \(x\).

The horizontal spans of the rulings are uniformly bounded. Choose \(x_0\), and set
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
Because
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]
and because \(h(x+2L)=h(x)+2L\), the same bound holds for every \(x\in\mathbb R\).

### 2.3. An invariant PL approximation

We need a PL approximation compatible with the deck map.

**Lemma.** Let \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\) be a scaled Sadowsky lift with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]
and let \(S\) be the ruling-span bound above. For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
   \iff
   P=H^k(Q)
   \quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z;
   \]
2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]
4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon,
\]
and also small enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Choose \(x_0\). Take a finite partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so fine that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho.
\]
Include among the \(x_i\) all seam rulings in one quotient period. Extend by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i),
\]
so that
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The rulings \(B_iT_i\) divide the strip into clean quadrilaterals
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]

For \(i=0,\dots,N-1\), triangulate \(Q_i\) using the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Its \(H\)-image triangulates \(Q_{i+N}\) using the diagonal \(T_{i+N}B_{i+N+1}\). Extend by \(H^2\). This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. Since \(E\circ H=E\), the map \(F_0\) is \(H\)-invariant.

It remains to justify quotient-injectivity for sufficiently small \(\rho\). On each smooth Sadowsky piece, use ruling coordinates
\[
\Psi(u,v)=((1-v)u+v h(u),vc),\qquad 0\le v\le1.
\]
Because \(E\) maps each ruling linearly to a straight segment,
\[
E(\Psi(u,v))=(1-v)b(u)+v t(u),
\]
where
\[
b(u)=E(u,0),\qquad t(u)=E(h(u),c).
\]
On each piece, \(b,t,h\) are \(C^1\), and the seam rulings have been included in the partition. As \(\rho\to0\), the secant PL maps converge to \(E\) in \(C^1\) in these ruling coordinates: finite differences of \(b\) and \(t\) converge uniformly to \(b'\) and \(t'\), and the \(v\)-direction derivative is controlled by \(t(u)-b(u)\). The fact that the actual clean triangles are Euclidean triangles rather than coordinate triangles does not affect the conclusion, because the affine coordinates differ from the ruling coordinates by a \(C^1\) error tending uniformly to \(0\).

Now use compact \(C^1\)-stability of embeddings. On the compact quotient Möbius band, cover the Sadowsky surface by finitely many ruling-coordinate neighborhoods, narrow in the ruling parameter and full in the ruling direction, such that in each neighborhood \(E\) is a \(C^1\) graph over some plane and the projection to that plane is quantitatively bilipschitz. Shrink these neighborhoods to a smaller cover. For sufficiently small \(\rho\), every PL face meeting a smaller neighborhood lies in the corresponding larger one, and its image under \(F_0\) is also a graph over the same plane. The projected PL map has derivative uniformly close to that of the projected Sadowsky map, hence is locally injective.

For nonlocal intersections, choose a neighborhood \(U\) of the diagonal in \(M\times M\) such that all pairs in \(U\) lie in one of the local injectivity neighborhoods. Since the quotient Sadowsky map is embedded and \(M\times M\setminus U\) is compact, there is \(\eta>0\) such that
\[
|E(p)-E(q)|>\eta
\qquad ((p,q)\notin U).
\]
Uniform convergence \(\|F_0-E\|_\infty<\eta/3\) rules out intersections outside \(U\), and local injectivity rules out intersections inside \(U\). Thus the quotient PL map is embedded, which is equivalent to quotient-injectivity of the lift \(F_0\).

The edge estimates are straightforward. Boundary edges are chords of boundary arcs of the path-isometric Sadowsky surface, so their image lengths are at most their flat lengths.

For a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\),
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|,
\]
because rulings map isometrically to straight segments.

For a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(|T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_{i+1})-E(T_i)|\le |T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho.
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&> r-\rho\\
&> d-2\rho\\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The opposite diagonals \(T_iB_{i+1}\) are handled identically.

Finally, ruling edges have horizontal span at most \(S\), and each diagonal has horizontal span \(<S+\rho<D\). \(\square\)

### 2.4. Pulling back by an anisotropic affine map

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
Start with the width-one Sadowsky band of length \(\mu\). Let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound, and choose \(D_\mu>S_\mu\).

Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}.
\]
Set
\[
c=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}.
\]
Then \(c>1\), and if
\[
L=\mu c,
\]
then
\[
L=a\beta.
\]

Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\). It now has width \(c\), length \(L=a\beta\), and ruling-span bound \(cS_\mu\). Apply the PL approximation lemma with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]

For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)
=
\sqrt{c^2s^2+c^2}
-
\sqrt{\frac{c^2s^2}{a^2}+1}.
\]
At \(a=1\), we have \(c=\beta/\mu>1\), so
\[
\Delta_1(s)>0
\]
for every \(s\in[0,D_\mu]\). By compactness and continuity, for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m,
\]
and apply the PL approximation lemma to the scaled Sadowsky band with this \(\varepsilon\).

Define the anisotropic affine map
\[
\mathcal A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
\mathcal A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Because \(L=a\beta\),
\[
\mathcal A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
(ax+L,c-cy)
=
H(\mathcal A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H\).

Let
\[
\mathcal T=\mathcal A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ\mathcal A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

We verify the squeeze inequalities.

If \(pq\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\), then \(\mathcal A(p)\mathcal A(q)\) has length \(a|p-q|\). Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
So boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(t\) be the horizontal span of \(\mathcal A(p)\mathcal A(q)\). By the PL lemma,
\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), with \(0\le s\le D_\mu\). Then
\[
|\mathcal A(p)-\mathcal A(q)|
=
\sqrt{t^2+c^2}
=
\sqrt{c^2s^2+c^2},
\]
while
\[
|p-q|
=
\sqrt{\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)^2+1}
=
\sqrt{\frac{c^2s^2}{a^2}+1}.
\]
The PL approximation lemma gives
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(\mathcal A p)-F_0(\mathcal A q)|
\ge
\sqrt{c^2s^2+c^2}-\varepsilon.
\]
Since \(\varepsilon<m\le \Delta_a(s)\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
>
\sqrt{\frac{c^2s^2}{a^2}+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

Finally, because \(\mathcal A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H\) and \(F_0\) is quotient-injective,
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff F_0(\mathcal A p)=F_0(\mathcal A q)\\
&\iff \mathcal A p=H^k(\mathcal A q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), and every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. Therefore the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is
\[
\sqrt3.\qedhere
\]

   **Sadowsky construction lemma.** For every \(\mu>\sqrt3\) there exists a \(C^1\) embedded developable Möbius band
   \[
   I_\mu:M_{\mu,1}=([0,\mu]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\mu,1-y)\to\mathbb R^3
   \]
   which is path-isometric for the flat metric, is made from finitely many planar and cylindrical pieces, and is ruled by full-width straight generators joining the two boundary components of the lifted rectangle. Its lift \(E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3\) satisfies
   \[
   E_\mu(P)=E_\mu(Q)\iff P=H_{\mu,1}^k(Q),\qquad H_{\mu,1}(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y).
   \]

   **Justification.** Sadowsky’s construction gives a band of length \(l\), half-width \(b\), and cylinder parameter \(d>0\) whenever
   \[
   b\le \frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
   \]
   Taking \(l=\mu\), \(b=1/2\), and sufficiently small
   \[
   0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}
   \]
   gives strict inequality. The planar pieces are moved by Euclidean motions and the cylindrical pieces by standard unrolling isometries, so the intrinsic metric is preserved. Adjacent pieces meet along common generator segments with matching tangent planes. Strict inequality means the chosen half-width is below the first-overlap width; since only finitely many nonadjacent compact pieces are involved, disjointness gives a positive separation. Embeddedness of the quotient gives quotient-injectivity of the lift.

2. **Replace the \(C^1\)-stability paragraph in the PL approximation lemma.** The sentence claiming \(C^1\)-convergence of the full-width ruled PL interpolation should be deleted. Use this argument instead.

   On each Sadowsky piece choose ruling coordinates
   \[
   \Psi(u,v)=((1-v)u+v h(u),vc),\qquad 0\le v\le1,
   \]
   so that
   \[
   E(\Psi(u,v))=(1-v)b(u)+v t(u),
   \]
   with \(b,t,h\) \(C^1\) on each piece. Include all seam rulings in the partition.

   For a cell \(u\in[u_i,u_{i+1}]\), the PL map \(F_0\) is the two-triangle vertex interpolation of the four points
   \[
   b_i=b(u_i),\quad b_{i+1}=b(u_{i+1}),\quad t_i=t(u_i),\quad t_{i+1}=t(u_{i+1}).
   \]
   Since \(b,t\), and the ruling vector \(t-b\), are uniformly continuous on the compact quotient, the interpolation error satisfies
   \[
   \sup_{\Psi([u_i,u_{i+1}]\times[0,1])}|F_0-E|
   \le C\,\omega(|u_{i+1}-u_i|),
   \]
   where \(\omega(\rho)\to0\) as \(\rho\to0\). Hence \(F_0\to E\) uniformly on the quotient.

   For local injectivity, cover the compact Sadowsky band by finitely many neighborhoods \(W_j\), each narrow in the ruling parameter and full in the ruling direction, such that \(E(W_j)\) is a graph over some plane \(\Pi_j\). This is possible because each piece is planar or cylindrical and adjacent pieces meet \(C^1\)-tangent along seam rulings. For sufficiently fine partition, every PL cell meeting a smaller \(W_j'\Subset W_j\) lies in \(W_j\). The projection \(\pi_j\circ F_0\) maps each such PL triangle affinely onto a nondegenerate triangle in \(\Pi_j\), and the projected triangles tile a planar polygon without overlap. Thus \(F_0\) is a graph over that planar polygon and is locally injective on \(W_j'\).

   For nonlocal intersections, choose a neighborhood \(U\) of the diagonal in \(M\times M\) such that every pair in \(U\) lies in one of the local graph neighborhoods. Since \(E\) is embedded and \(M\times M\setminus U\) is compact, there is \(\eta>0\) with
   \[
   |E(p)-E(q)|\ge\eta\qquad ((p,q)\notin U).
   \]
   Taking the partition fine enough that \(\|F_0-E\|_\infty<\eta/3\) rules out intersections outside \(U\), while the graph argument rules them out inside \(U\). Therefore the quotient PL map is embedded, equivalently the lift is quotient-injective.

   - Local finiteness implies every compact boundary arc meets finitely many boundary edges, so strict shortening of each boundary subedge implies strict shortening of the whole arc.

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
