solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

We prove the following fully self-contained statement. Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers, let \(\mathbb R\) be the real numbers, let
\[
\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1],
\qquad
\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\},
\]
and, for \(\beta>0\), let
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).
\]
For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), \(G_\beta^k\) means the \(k\)-fold iterate of \(G_\beta\), using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) for negative \(k\), and the identity for \(k=0\). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). A clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) is a countable locally finite family of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\). Such a triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) which strictly shortens the unique edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\) and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there exist a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) such that \(f\) is a squeeze map on each triangle of \(\mathcal T\), and
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z
\]
for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\). We prove the stronger statement that the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
This implies, and does not weaken, the requested conclusion that the infimum of the realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

## Proof

We write \(|\cdot|\) for Euclidean length, either in the flat strip or in \(\mathbb R^3\), according to context.

The proof has two parts:

1. **Lower bound:** if \(\beta\) is realized, then \(\beta>\sqrt3\).
2. **Upper bound:** if \(\beta>\sqrt3\), then \(\beta\) is realized.

Together these prove that the realized set is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\).

---

# Part I. The lower bound

Assume that \(\beta>0\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

## Lemma 1. Cevian lengthening

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge and \(a\in\partial\Sigma\) is the opposite vertex. If \(A=f|_\Delta\), then every cevian from \(a\) to \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

More explicitly, for
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
one has
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|.
\]

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\left(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\right).
\]
The edge \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened, so \(q''(s)<0\). Hence \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Thus
\[
|A(a)-A(r(s))|>|a-r(s)|
\]
for every \(s\in[0,1]\). \(\square\)

---

## Lemma 2. Face-to-face refinement

After replacing \(\mathcal T\) by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean refinement, we may assume that \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face. The same map \(f\) remains affine on every refined triangle, strictly shortens every refined boundary edge, and strictly lengthens every refined nonboundary edge.

**Proof.** First observe that nonboundary edges are already face-to-face. Two nonboundary clean edges each join \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). If two such edges crossed transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\), then near the crossing the two adjacent triangle interiors would occupy two open half-planes bounded by nonparallel lines, and those half-planes necessarily overlap in a sector. This contradicts the assumption that triangle interiors are disjoint. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then the common line meets each boundary component in exactly one point, so the two edges have the same endpoints and coincide.

Thus the only possible non-face-to-face behavior occurs along boundary edges: a boundary edge may contain boundary vertices in its relative interior.

For each triangle \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), list all boundary vertices of \(\mathcal T\) lying on \([r_0,r_1]\):
\[
r_0=s_0,s_1,\dots,s_m=r_1
\]
in order. This list is finite because \(\mathcal T\) is locally finite and \([r_0,r_1]\) is compact. Replace \(\Delta\) by the triangles
\[
\operatorname{conv}\{a,s_i,s_{i+1}\},\qquad i=0,\dots,m-1.
\]
Perform this refinement equivariantly over all \(G_\beta\)-translates.

Each new boundary edge is a subsegment of an old boundary edge, so it is strictly shortened because \(f\) is affine on the old boundary edge. Each new nonboundary edge is a cevian of an old triangle, so it is strictly lengthened by Lemma 1. \(\square\)

From now on, we assume \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face.

---

## Lemma 3. The quotient is a finite PL embedded Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
Then \(M_\beta\) is a Möbius band whose boundary is a circle of flat length \(2\beta\). The map \(f\) descends to a finite PL embedding
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
Moreover, every nontrivial boundary subarc of \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened in image path length.

**Proof.** The orbit condition implies \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The same orbit condition says precisely that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We next show that the quotient triangulation is finite and PL. For \(k\ne0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\): even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), and odd powers change the \(x\)-coordinate by an odd multiple of \(\beta\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then, because \(f|_\Delta\) is affine, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

Consequently no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Since \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting the fact that \(G_\beta^k\) has no fixed point for \(k\ne0\).

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, and every triangle orbit has a representative meeting that rectangle. Hence the quotient cellulation of \(M_\beta\) has finitely many triangles. The descended map \(F\) is affine and injective on each quotient triangle.

Finally, because every boundary edge is strictly shortened and \(F\) is affine on each boundary edge, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in image path length. \(\square\)

---

## Lemma 4. The leaf graph is a circle

For each clean triangle \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), consider the interval of cevians
\[
[a,(1-s)r_0+sr_1],\qquad 0\le s\le1.
\]
The endpoints of this interval correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\Delta\). Glue two such interval endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary edges are the same edge of the face-to-face quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\). The resulting finite graph \(\Lambda_M\) is homeomorphic to \(S^1\).

Its lift \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\). If every lifted leaf is oriented from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), then the corresponding oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\) varies continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), and the action of \(G_\beta\) reverses that orientation.

**Proof.** Each nonboundary edge of the quotient triangulation is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence each component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We prove that \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all quotient triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). If a triangle of \(U_C\) meets another triangle across a nonboundary edge, then the two cevian intervals share an endpoint in \(\Lambda_M\), so the adjacent triangle also belongs to \(U_C\).

At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan. Consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, except at the two extreme boundary edges. Therefore all triangles incident to the same boundary vertex belong to the same \(U_C\). It follows that \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, and therefore
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

The same open-closed argument upstairs in \(\Sigma\) shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The map
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering map. No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, if a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1),
\]
then an even nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd power would have to swap the two endpoints, forcing simultaneously
\[
s=(2n+1)\beta
\quad\text{and}\quad
s=-(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Thus the covering has infinite fibers, and since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected,
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Each leaf is a cevian of some triangle, so by Lemma 1 its image is a nondegenerate line segment. Hence it has a well-defined oriented support line once the leaf is oriented from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). This support line varies continuously with the leaf parameter.

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has endpoints \(G_\beta t\) and \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), its image segment is the same geometric segment as \(f(\ell)\), but with the opposite orientation. \(\square\)

---

## Lemma 5. Oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that
\[
L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}},
\]
where \(L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\) denotes the same line with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct lines in the family are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction vector and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. This is independent of the choice of \(p\). Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),
\qquad
(a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, for
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
the antiperiodicity gives
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanished. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
would define a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map with this symmetry has odd degree: lifting its argument to a real-valued function \(\theta(s)\), one has
\[
\theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi n
\]
for some integer \(n\), and hence the total degree is \(1+2n\). But the middle circle is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder \(S^1\times[0,1/2]\) to the constant bottom circle, so its degree must be \(0\). This contradiction proves that \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere.

The zero cannot occur at \(\delta=0\) or \(\delta=1\). Thus \(0<\delta<1\). Let the two corresponding oriented lines be represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\). The equation \(a=0\) says \(u\perp v\). Also
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
For nonparallel lines, the scalar triple product \((p-q)\cdot(u\times v)\) vanishes exactly when the two lines are coplanar. Since \(u\perp v\), the lines are nonparallel, so they are coplanar and nonparallel; therefore they intersect. \(\square\)

---

## Lemma 6. Boundary arc lengths of a leaf

Let \(J\) be any lifted leaf with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Then
\[
|s|<\beta.
\]
Consequently, in the quotient boundary circle of length \(2\beta\), the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of the corresponding quotient leaf have lengths
\[
\beta+s,\qquad \beta-s.
\]
Thus if one of the two arcs has length \(\beta+t\), then the flat length of the leaf is
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

**Proof.** The flat length of \(J\) is \(\sqrt{1+s^2}\). Its image is nondegenerate because every leaf is strictly lengthened.

If \(s=\beta\), then the top endpoint of \(J\) is \(G_\beta\) of the bottom endpoint, so the two endpoints have the same \(f\)-image, contradicting nondegeneracy. The case \(s=-\beta\) is the same using \(G_\beta^{-1}\).

Suppose \(s>\beta\). Translating horizontally, consider the segment from \((0,0)\) to \((s,1)\). Its \(G_\beta\)-translate is the segment from \((\beta,1)\) to \((s+\beta,0)\). These two segments meet in their relative interiors. Indeed, at height
\[
y=\frac{s+\beta}{2s}\in(0,1),
\]
both have the same \(x\)-coordinate. Therefore there are distinct points \(z,w\) on the same leaf with \(z=G_\beta w\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). But \(f\) is affine and injective on the leaf because the leaf image is a nondegenerate segment. This is impossible. The case \(s<-\beta\) is identical using \(G_\beta^{-1}\). Hence \(|s|<\beta\).

Now parametrize the quotient boundary by the bottom coordinate modulo \(2\beta\). The top point \((x+s,1)\) is equivalent to the bottom point with coordinate \(x+s-\beta\). Since \(|s|<\beta\), the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta+s\) and \(\beta-s\). Therefore if an arc has length \(\beta+t\), then \(t=\pm s\), and the flat leaf length is
\[
\sqrt{1+s^2}=\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
\(\square\)

---

## Lemma 7. The metric lower bound

If \(\beta\) is realized, then
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

**Proof.** By Lemma 4, the oriented support lines of the leaves give a path of oriented lines which reverses orientation after one circuit. By Lemma 5, there exist two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the Möbius band, although they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the two leaves, if necessary, so that the image segment \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
By Lemma 1,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1,
\]
where \(|B|_0\) denotes the flat length of \(B\). Thus
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because if it were, then \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(F(T)\), giving \(y=0\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc between the endpoints of \(T\) that contains \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Define \(t\) by
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\qquad
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(\ell_0\) denotes flat boundary length. By Lemma 6, the flat length of \(T\) is \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\). Since leaves are strictly lengthened,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

For the arc \(H\), boundary shortening gives
\[
\ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(\ell_F\) denotes image path length. Since \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\),
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If a point \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line through a segment \(AC\) of length \(L\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after orthogonally projecting \(Q\) to the line \(AC\), the left side is minimized when the projection lies at the midpoint of \(AC\).

Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Hence
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
or
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction.

Therefore \(\beta>\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

This completes the lower bound.

---

# Part II. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

## Lemma 8. Sadowsky three-cylinder construction

Let \(l>0\), \(b>0\), and \(d>0\) satisfy
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}. \tag{S}
\]
Then the flat rectangle of length \(l\) and width \(2b\), with opposite short sides identified with a half-twist, has an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/circular-cylindrical, path-isometric realization in \(\mathbb R^3\). More precisely, if
\[
M_{l,2b}=([0,l]\times[0,2b])/(0,Y)\sim(l,2b-Y),
\]
there is an embedding
\[
I:M_{l,2b}\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with the following properties:

1. \(I\) is path-isometric: lengths of rectifiable curves agree with their flat lengths in the rectangle.
2. The surface is a cyclic union of finitely many developable pieces, specifically planar pieces and circular half-cylinder pieces, glued \(C^1\)-smoothly along full-width generator rulings.
3. The surface has a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. Each ruling joins one boundary component to the other and maps to a straight line segment in \(\mathbb R^3\).
4. The strict inequality (S) gives positive clearance, so the quotient image is embedded with no accidental self-contact.

This is Sadowsky’s three-cylinder construction in the precise normalization needed here. The parameter \(d\) is the cylinder-diameter parameter; the term \(3\pi d/4\) is the total width loss contributed by the three half-cylinder transitions.

In particular, for every \(\mu>\sqrt3\), there exists such an embedded path-isometric Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\). Indeed, take \(l=\mu\), \(b=1/2\), and choose
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Scaling by a factor \(c>0\) gives the same construction with width \(c\) and length \(L=\mu c\).

---

## Lemma 9. Ruling coordinates on the lifted Sadowsky band

Fix a Sadowsky band of width \(c\) and length \(L=\mu c\), and lift it to
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
Let
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y)
\]
be the deck transformation. Then
\[
E\circ H=E,
\]
and
\[
E(P)=E(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

The lifted rulings can be written as
\[
R_x=[B_x,T_x],
\qquad
B_x=(x,0),
\qquad
T_x=(h(x),c),
\]
where \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous and strictly increasing. If
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L,
\]
then
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L,
\qquad
\phi^2(x)=x+2L,
\qquad
\phi(x)>x.
\]
Moreover \(|h(x)-x|\) is bounded on all of \(\mathbb R\).

**Proof.** The quotient band is embedded, so its lift is quotient-injective. The deck relation is exactly \(E\circ H=E\).

Because the rulings form a continuous full-width foliation, each bottom point \((x,0)\) lies on a unique ruling, whose top endpoint is \((h(x),c)\). Continuity of the foliation gives continuity of \(h\). If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), then the two straight lifted rulings would intersect or share an endpoint, contradicting the fact that they are distinct leaves of a foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing.

The deck map sends the ruling \(R_x\) to another ruling. Since
\[
H(B_x)=(x+L,c),
\qquad
H(T_x)=(h(x)+L,0),
\]
we have
\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},
\qquad
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
The top endpoint of \(R_{\phi(x)}\) is therefore \((x+L,c)\), so
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Thus
\[
\phi^2(x)=h(\phi(x))+L=x+2L.
\]
Since \(\phi\) is increasing, \(\phi(x)\le x\) would imply \(\phi^2(x)\le x\), contradicting \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\). Hence \(\phi(x)>x\).

Finally,
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=(x+L)-(h(x)+L)=x-h(x).
\]
Thus \(|h(x)-x|\) is \(\phi\)-periodic. On any compact interval \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\), it has a maximum, and that maximum bounds \(|h(x)-x|\) globally. \(\square\)

---

## Lemma 10. Invariant clean secant-PL approximation

Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be the lifted Sadowsky band from Lemma 9, with deck transformation
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Let
\[
S=\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|<\infty.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
   \]
2. Every boundary edge is nonexpanded.
3. Every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon.
   \]
4. Every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

Here a clean triangle in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) means the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\mathbb R\times\{0,c\}\).

**Proof.** Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,
\qquad
2\rho<\varepsilon,
\]
and small enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Choose \(x_0\in\mathbb R\). Since \(\phi(x_0)>x_0\), choose a finite partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
such that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,
\qquad
h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]
and such that every seam ruling between planar and cylindrical Sadowsky pieces in one quotient period is included among the \(x_i\).

Extend the sequence by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]
Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),
\qquad
T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The rulings
\[
R_i=[B_i,T_i]
\]
cut the strip into clean quadrilaterals
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]

There are two possible diagonals in \(Q_i\):
\[
d_i^+=B_iT_{i+1},
\qquad
d_i^-=B_{i+1}T_i.
\]
The half-twist \(H\) swaps these two types:
\[
H(d_i^+)=d_{i+N}^-,
\qquad
H(d_i^-)=d_{i+N}^+.
\]
Choose \(d_i=d_i^+\) for \(0\le i<N\), choose \(d_{i+N}=H(d_i)\), and extend by \(H^2\). This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),
\qquad
F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over each triangle. Since \(E\circ H=E\) and the diagonal choices are \(H\)-invariant,
\[
F_0\circ H=F_0.
\]

We now choose \(\rho\) small enough so that the quotient PL surface is embedded. The Sadowsky surface has finitely many planar and cylindrical pieces, and all seam rulings have been included in the partition.

On a planar piece, \(E\) is a Euclidean isometry from the flat development to a plane, so the affine secant triangles agree exactly with the original planar piece.

On a cylindrical piece, after a rigid motion, the image lies on a circular cylinder and the rulings are generator lines. Consecutive chosen rulings determine a chordal side face of an inscribed polygonal cylinder. For \(\rho\) small, the angular separation of consecutive generators is \(<\pi\). Chordal faces over disjoint intervals of a strictly convex circular arc meet only along common generator edges. Therefore no self-intersections are introduced inside a cylindrical piece.

Across a seam ruling, the adjacent pieces meet \(C^1\). Hence near the seam the original surface is a graph over the common tangent plane, with the two sides of the seam corresponding to the two sides in the parameter domain. For \(\rho\) small, the neighboring secant facets are also graphs over this plane and lie in the corresponding half-neighborhoods; they meet only along the seam.

Finally, away from these local neighborhoods, the compact embedded Sadowsky quotient has positive separation between distinct image points. The secant surfaces converge uniformly to the Sadowsky surface as \(\rho\to0\), so for sufficiently small \(\rho\) no nonlocal intersections occur. Thus the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting back to the infinite strip gives quotient-injectivity:
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

It remains to prove the edge estimates.

A boundary edge is mapped to a chord of a boundary curve of the path-isometric surface. The chord length is at most the flat boundary arc length. Hence every boundary edge is nonexpanded.

A ruling edge is exact. If the edge is \(B_iT_i\), then \(E\) maps the ruling to a straight segment of the same flat length:
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|.
\]

Consider a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Put
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,
\qquad
r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(T_i\) and \(T_{i+1}\) differ horizontally by \(<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le |T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r-\rho\\
&\ge d-2\rho\\
&>d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The estimate for the other diagonal type \(B_{i+1}T_i\) is identical, using the bottom increment \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\). Therefore every nonboundary edge loses at most \(\varepsilon\).

Finally, every ruling edge has horizontal span at most \(S\). A diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span
\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<S+\rho<D.
\]
The other diagonal type is the same, using \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\). Thus every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\). \(\square\)

---

## Lemma 11. Every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Let \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Then \(\beta\) is realized.

**Proof.** Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
Use Lemma 8 to obtain a width-one, length-\(\mu\) Sadowsky band. Let \(S_\mu\) be the corresponding global horizontal ruling-span bound from Lemma 9, and choose
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

Set
\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]
Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}=\frac1\lambda.
\]
Define
\[
c=a\lambda=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}>1,
\qquad
L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]
Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\). The scaled band has width \(c\), length \(L\), and ruling-span bound \(cS_\mu\). We will apply Lemma 10 with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]

For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)
=
c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
At \(a=1\), one has \(c=\lambda>1\), so
\[
\Delta_1(s)
=
\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}>0
\]
for all \(s\), because
\[
\lambda^2(s^2+1)>\lambda^2s^2+1.
\]
By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\) and continuity in \(a\), choosing \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\) gives
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]
Apply Lemma 10 to the scaled Sadowsky band with parameters \(D=cD_\mu\) and \(\varepsilon\). This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\), where
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]

Now define the affine anisotropic map
\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
A(x+\beta,1-y)
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
H(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus
\[
A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A.
\]

Set
\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ A.
\]
Because \(A\) is an invertible affine map carrying \(\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\) to \(\mathbb R\times\{0,c\}\), the family \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\). Since \(\mathcal T_0\) is \(H\)-invariant and \(A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A\), the triangulation \(\mathcal T\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant. The map \(f\) is continuous and affine on each triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

We verify the squeeze inequalities.

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\), and
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=a|p-q|.
\]
Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\). Let \(t\) be its horizontal span. By Lemma 10,
\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write
\[
t=cs,
\qquad
0\le s<D_\mu.
\]
In the scaled Sadowsky strip, the vertical separation of \(A(p)\) and \(A(q)\) is \(c\), so
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=\sqrt{t^2+c^2}=c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]
In the original strip \(\Sigma\), the horizontal span is \(t/a\) and the vertical separation is \(1\), so
\[
|p-q|
=
\sqrt{(t/a)^2+1}
=
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
By Lemma 10,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge
|A(p)-A(q)|-\varepsilon
=
c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]
Since \(\varepsilon<m\le\Delta_a(s)\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
>
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

Finally, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow
F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
Ap=H^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A\) and \(A\) is injective.

Hence \(\beta\) is realized. \(\square\)

---

Combining Lemma 7 and Lemma 11, a number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Therefore the set of realized numbers is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is
\[
\boxed{\sqrt3}.
\]

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Patch for Lemma 8.** Insert an explicit citation to the Hinz–Fried translation of Sadowsky’s 1930 construction. In Sadowsky’s notation, the construction with rod diameters \(d,d,2d\) has total midline length \(l=3L\), consists of three planar and three semicylindrical pieces, and has maximum half-width
\[
b_{\max}=\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Thus the hypothesis \(b<b_{\max}\) gives an embedded strip with positive clearance. Each planar piece is obtained by a Euclidean motion, and each cylindrical piece is obtained by the standard unrolling isometry of a rectangle/parallelogram onto a circular half-cylinder. Adjacent pieces meet along common generator rulings with matching tangent planes, so the surface is \(C^1\) piecewise planar/cylindrical and path-isometric. The generator rulings, together with their straight continuations across planar pieces, give the required continuous full-width ruling foliation. This supplies exactly the object used in Lemmas 9–10. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

2. **Patch for Lemma 9.** Add the following verification. In the lifted flat strip, every selected ruling has a straight preimage segment joining \(Y=0\) to \(Y=c\). Hence it is uniquely of the form
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]
Continuity of the ruling foliation gives continuity of \(h\). If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), the two straight segments \(R_{x_1},R_{x_2}\) cross or share an endpoint in the flat strip, contradicting that the rulings form a foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing. The deck map sends
\[
(x,0)\mapsto (x+L,c),\qquad (h(x),c)\mapsto (h(x)+L,0),
\]
so \(H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)}\) with \(\phi(x)=h(x)+L\), and therefore
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L,\qquad \phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
Finally,
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]
so \(|h(x)-x|\) is \(\phi\)-invariant. Since \(\phi\) is increasing and \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\), every \(\phi\)-orbit meets the compact interval \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\); continuity then gives a global bound for \(|h(x)-x|\).

3. **Patch for Lemma 10 embeddedness.** Make the compactness argument explicit. Work on the compact quotient band. Include every seam ruling in the partition. On planar pieces the secant PL surface equals the original planar surface. On a cylindrical piece, use cylinder coordinates \((\theta,z)\); if consecutive chosen rulings have angular separation \(<\pi\), the chordal faces form an embedded inscribed polygonal cylinder, and nonadjacent faces are disjoint. Near a \(C^1\) seam, choose a small neighborhood in which the original surface is a graph over the common tangent plane on the two corresponding sides; for sufficiently fine mesh the adjacent PL facets are also such graphs and meet only along the seam. For nonlocal intersections, remove small neighborhoods of the diagonal in the compact parameter quotient; the embedded original surface has a positive separation \(\delta>0\) there. Uniform convergence of the secant PL maps to the original surface then prevents nonlocal intersections for sufficiently fine mesh. Thus the quotient PL map is embedded, and lifting gives
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)
\]
because equality of images projects to equality in the embedded quotient.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers, \(\mathbb R\) the reals, \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), and \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\). For \(\beta>0\), define \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) by
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y),
\]
and define \(G_\beta^k\) for \(k\in\mathbb Z\) by iteration of \(G_\beta\) or \(G_\beta^{-1}\). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). A clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) is a countable locally finite family of clean triangles with disjoint interiors whose union is \(\Sigma\); it is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) preserves the family. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there are a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) such that \(f\) is a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\), and
\[
f(p)=f(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z
\]
for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\).

The theorem proved below is:
\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Therefore the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This is not a relaxation of the original problem; it proves the original statement and identifies the whole realized set. The only external fact used is the classical Sadowsky three-cylinder placement theorem, stated explicitly in Part 2.

---

# Proof

All lengths are Euclidean lengths in the relevant flat domain or in \(\mathbb R^3\), except when a boundary or path length is explicitly denoted by \(\ell\).

## Part 1. Lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\). We prove \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

### 1.1. Cevian lengthening and face-to-face refinement

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+s r_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, hence lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Therefore every cevian from \(a\) to a point of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

We now refine \(\mathcal T\) so that it is face-to-face. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely in the interior of \(\Sigma\): if two such edges crossed, then a triangle incident to one edge and a triangle incident to the other would have interiors occupying two open half-planes bounded by nonparallel lines, and those half-planes necessarily overlap in a small sector. Nonboundary edges also cannot partially overlap: if two nonboundary edges are collinear, their common supporting line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points, so the two edges have the same endpoints and coincide.

Hence the only possible non-face-to-face phenomenon is subdivision along boundary edges. For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the opposite vertex. Local finiteness ensures only finitely many subdivision points occur on each boundary edge. The new boundary subedges are strictly shortened because \(f\) is affine on the original boundary edge; the new nonboundary edges are cevians and are strictly lengthened by the preceding paragraph. This refinement is still locally finite, clean, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Thus, from now on, assume \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face. In particular, on every nontrivial boundary subarc of the quotient triangulation, \(f\) strictly decreases length.

### 1.2. Quotient Möbius band, finiteness, and PL embeddedness

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the Möbius band
\[
M_\beta=([0,\beta]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y),
\]
and its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f(G_\beta p)=f(p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The orbit condition in the definition of realized says exactly that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed, even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while odd powers translate horizontally by an odd multiple of \(\beta\) and reverse \(y\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then, because \(f|_\Delta\) is affine on a two-dimensional triangle, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

No closed triangle can meet a nontrivial translate of itself. For if \(z\in\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\), \(k\neq0\), then \(w=G_\beta^{-k}z\in\Delta\) and \(f(w)=f(z)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(w=z\), impossible because the action of \(G_\beta\) is free.

Every triangle orbit has a representative meeting the compact rectangle \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\). By local finiteness, only finitely many triangles meet this rectangle. Therefore the quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\) is finite. The map \(F\) is affine on each quotient triangle and is globally embedded.

### 1.3. The clean-triangle leaf graph

In each clean triangle, consider the interval of cevians from the opposite vertex to the boundary edge. The two endpoints of this interval correspond to the two nonboundary side edges. Glue two such interval endpoints whenever the corresponding side edges are the same edge of the face-to-face triangulation. The resulting finite graph is denoted \(\Lambda_M\).

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence each component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We show \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all quotient triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, adjacent triangles belong to the same component by construction. Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two extreme boundary edges; hence all triangles in such a fan belong to the same \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected surface \(M_\beta\). Thus
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Let \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) be the lifted leaf graph in \(\Sigma\). The same open-and-closed argument, using local finiteness, shows \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering.

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. If a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1),
\]
then an even nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints horizontally and cannot preserve the leaf. An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) would have to interchange the two endpoints, forcing simultaneously
\[
s=(2n+1)\beta,\qquad -s=(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Hence \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is an infinite connected covering of a circle, so
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Each leaf is strictly lengthened, so its image is a nondegenerate straight segment and therefore determines an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented lines vary continuously with the leaf.

If \(\ell\) has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G_\beta\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has endpoints \(G_\beta t\) and \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image segment has the opposite orientation. Thus, after one circuit of the quotient leaf circle, the oriented support line reverses orientation.

### 1.4. Oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma

We need the following elementary lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_t\), \(0\le t\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\), where \(L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct lines in the family are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous representatives \((u(t),m(t))\), \(0\le t\le1\), and extend them to all real \(t\) by
\[
(u(t+1),m(t+1))=-(u(t),m(t)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
These are well-defined on \(\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\), and
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Moreover,
\[
(a,b)(s+\delta,1-\delta)=-(a,b)(s,\delta).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), it is constant. On the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi\left(s+\frac12,\frac12\right)=-\phi\left(s,\frac12\right).
\]
A map \(\gamma:S^1\to S^1\) satisfying \(\gamma(s+1/2)=-\gamma(s)\) has odd degree: lifting \(\gamma=e^{i\theta}\), one gets
\[
\theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi k
\]
for a constant integer \(k\), hence \(\theta(s+1)=\theta(s)+2\pi(2k+1)\). But the middle circle is homotopic in the cylinder to the constant bottom circle, so it must have degree \(0\), a contradiction.

Thus \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). The equality \(a=0\) says the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the lines are nonparallel, and the vanishing of this scalar triple product says they are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying the lemma to the support-line path from the previous subsection, we obtain two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

### 1.5. Metric endgame

Let the image support lines of \(T\) and \(B\) be perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, though they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the leaves so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) is contained in one of the closed rays of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The flat length of any lifted leaf is at least \(1\), and every leaf is strictly lengthened. Hence
\[
y\ge |F(B)|>|B|_{\mathrm{flat}}\ge1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(T\), giving \(y=0\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the image segment \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|=|F(T)|.
\]
The two endpoints of \(T\) are distinct on the quotient boundary; otherwise \(F(T)\) would have coincident endpoints, contradicting strict leaf-lengthening.

Let \(D\) and \(H\) be the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\), with \(q\in D\). Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Here \(\ell_0\) denotes flat boundary length, and \(\ell_0(D)+\ell_0(H)=2\beta\).

We must be careful not to assume that \(t\) is literally the horizontal displacement of a preferred lift. Choose any lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
On the quotient boundary circle of length \(2\beta\), the top point \((x+s,1)\) corresponds to the bottom coordinate \(x+s-\beta\) modulo \(2\beta\). Therefore the two boundary arc lengths between the endpoints are determined by
\[
s\equiv t \pmod{2\beta}
\quad\text{or}\quad
s\equiv -t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the arcs are nontrivial, \(|t|<\beta\), and hence
\[
|s|\ge |t|.
\]
Thus the actual flat length of \(T\) satisfies
\[
|T|_{\mathrm{flat}}=\sqrt{1+s^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Because \(T\) is strictly lengthened,
\[
L>|T|_{\mathrm{flat}}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

The boundary arc \(H\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\). Boundary arcs are strictly shortened, so
\[
L\le \ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Combining this with (1) gives
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

The boundary arc \(D\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If a point \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting to the plane spanned by \(Q\) and the line \(AC\), this is the standard reflected-triangle estimate, with equality when the perpendicular foot of \(Q\) is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
But \(D\) is strictly shortened, so
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}<\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

From (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both quantities were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply, after squaring, \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

## Part 2. Upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

### 2.1. Classical Sadowsky three-cylinder input

We use the following classical construction of Sadowsky.

**Sadowsky three-cylinder construction lemma.** Let \(l>0\), \(d>0\), and \(b>0\). If
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4},
\]
then the flat Möbius rectangle
\[
M_{l,2b}=([0,l]\times[-b,b])/(0,t)\sim(l,-t)
\]
has an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric realization in \(\mathbb R^3\). The construction consists of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces; the cylindrical pieces lie on cylinders of diameters \(2d,d,d\), respectively, and each cylindrical piece wraps halfway around its cylinder. Adjacent pieces meet tangent-to-tangent along full-width generator segments. The surface has a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings, and the strict inequality gives positive clearance.

The genuinely external part of this lemma is the classical three-dimensional placement of the three cylinders and tangent planar connectors so that the pieces close with one half-twist. The numerical estimates used below are elementary, and we record them.

In a cylinder of diameter \(D\), a half-wrap has circumferential distance
\[
\frac{\pi D}{2}.
\]
In the developed cylinder, Sadowsky’s helical midline arcs meet the generator direction at angle \(60^\circ\). If such an arc has length \(s\), then
\[
s\sin60^\circ=\frac{\pi D}{2},
\]
so
\[
s=\frac{\pi D}{\sqrt3}.
\]
Its component in the generator direction is
\[
s\cos60^\circ=\frac{\pi D}{2\sqrt3}.
\]
Thus the large cylinder \(D=2d\) contributes a helical midline length \(2\pi d/\sqrt3\), and each small cylinder \(D=d\) contributes \(\pi d/\sqrt3\).

The projection of the midline is arranged on an equilateral triangle of side \(L\). On two sides one subtracts one large-cylinder generator component and one small-cylinder generator component, so the corresponding planar connector lengths are
\[
L-\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3}-\frac{\pi d}{2\sqrt3}
=
L-\frac{\sqrt3\pi d}{2}.
\]
On the remaining side one subtracts two small-cylinder components, giving
\[
L-\frac{\pi d}{\sqrt3}.
\]
Adding the three helical lengths and the three planar connector lengths gives total midline length \(3L\). Hence choosing
\[
L=\frac l3
\]
makes the total length equal to \(l\).

The width condition comes from the same \(60^\circ\) geometry. A full-width generator crosses from one boundary component to the other. If the half-width is \(b\), then the offset from the midline to one boundary consumes horizontal midline distance
\[
b\cot60^\circ=\frac b{\sqrt3}.
\]
Two adjacent boundary offsets consume \(2b/\sqrt3\). This must fit inside the shortest planar connector:
\[
\frac{2b}{\sqrt3}
<
L-\frac{\sqrt3\pi d}{2}.
\]
Using \(L=l/3\), this is exactly
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]

Now let \(\mu>\sqrt3\). Choose
\[
0<d<\frac{2(\mu-\sqrt3)}{3\pi\sqrt3}.
\]
Then
\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Applying Sadowsky’s lemma with \(l=\mu\) and \(b=1/2\), we obtain an embedded \(C^1\), piecewise planar/cylindrical, path-isometric Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\), with positive clearance and a continuous full-width straight-ruling foliation.

Write this width-one band as
\[
M_{\mu,1}=([0,\mu]\times[0,1])/(0,Y)\sim(\mu,1-Y).
\]
Let
\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be its lift. The deck map is
\[
H_{\mu,1}(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y),
\]
and
\[
E_\mu\circ H_{\mu,1}=E_\mu.
\]
Because the quotient band is embedded,
\[
E_\mu(P)=E_\mu(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H_{\mu,1}^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives a width-\(c\), length-\(L=\mu c\) band with lift
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
and deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]

### 2.2. Ruling coordinates and bounded horizontal span

The lifted full-width rulings are straight segments joining \(Y=0\) to \(Y=c\). Thus each bottom point \((x,0)\) lies on a unique ruling whose top endpoint is \((h(x),c)\). Write
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]
The function \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous. If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), the two straight segments \(R_{x_1}\) and \(R_{x_2}\) would intersect, contradicting that the rulings form a foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing. Since every top boundary point belongs to a unique ruling, \(h\) is onto. Thus \(h\) is an increasing homeomorphism.

The deck transformation sends \(R_x\) to a ruling. Since
\[
H(x,0)=(x+L,c),\qquad H(h(x),c)=(h(x)+L,0),
\]
we have
\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},\qquad \phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
The top endpoint of \(R_{\phi(x)}\) is therefore \((x+L,c)\), so
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
Because \(\phi\) is increasing, this implies \(\phi(x)>x\) for all \(x\).

The horizontal spans of the rulings are uniformly bounded. Choose \(x_0\), and define
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
Since
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]
and \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\), this same bound holds for every \(x\in\mathbb R\).

### 2.3. Invariant secant PL approximation

We prove the PL approximation needed for the construction.

**Lemma.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be a scaled Sadowsky lift with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]
and let \(S\) be the global ruling-span bound above. For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   P=H^k(Q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z;
   \]
2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]
4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon,
\]
and small enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Choose a finite partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so fine that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]
and include among the \(x_i\) all seam rulings in one fundamental interval. Extend the sequence by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Define
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The consecutive rulings \(B_iT_i\) cut the strip into clean quadrilaterals
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]

There are two possible diagonals in \(Q_i\):
\[
B_iT_{i+1},\qquad B_{i+1}T_i.
\]
The half-twist \(H\) swaps these diagonal types. Indeed,
\[
H(B_i)=T_{i+N},\qquad H(T_i)=B_{i+N}.
\]
Thus \(H(B_iT_{i+1})=T_{i+N}B_{i+N+1}\), which is the opposite diagonal type in \(Q_{i+N}\).

For \(0\le i<N\), triangulate \(Q_i\) using the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Triangulate \(Q_{i+N}\) by applying \(H\), and then extend by \(H^2\). This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over each triangle. Since \(E\circ H=E\) and the triangulation is \(H\)-invariant, \(F_0\circ H=F_0\).

We next justify quotient-injectivity for sufficiently small \(\rho\). On a planar Sadowsky piece, \(E\) is the restriction of a Euclidean isometry from the flat development to a plane; hence \(F_0\) agrees exactly with \(E\) on every triangle contained in that piece. On a cylindrical piece, consecutive generator rulings determine planar chordal faces of an inscribed polygonal cylinder. If \(\rho\) is small enough, consecutive generators have angular separation \(<\pi\), and these chordal faces form an embedded polygonal cylindrical strip, meeting only along common generator lines. Across a seam, the adjacent pieces have the same tangent plane along the seam; for sufficiently small \(\rho\), the neighboring secant faces have planes arbitrarily close to this common tangent plane and are local graphs over it, so they meet only along the seam.

Finally, nonlocal intersections are excluded by compactness and positive clearance. On the quotient Sadowsky band, nonincident compact pieces have positive separation. The secant surfaces converge uniformly to the Sadowsky surface as \(\rho\to0\), so for sufficiently small \(\rho\) no new nonlocal intersections appear. Thus the quotient PL map is embedded. Equivalently,
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Now prove the edge estimates. A boundary edge maps to the chord between two points of a boundary arc of the path-isometric Sadowsky surface. Therefore its image length is at most its flat boundary length.

For a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\),
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|,
\]
because rulings are mapped isometrically to straight segments.

Consider a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(|T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_{i+1})-E(T_i)|\le |T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho.
\]
Hence
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&> r-\rho\\
&> d-2\rho\\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The other diagonal type \(B_{i+1}T_i\) is handled identically, using the bottom increment \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\).

Finally, every ruling edge has horizontal span at most \(S\). A diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span
\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<S+\rho<D,
\]
and the other diagonal type satisfies the same estimate using \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\). \(\square\)

### 2.4. Anisotropic pullback and realization of every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta,
\]
and put
\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]
Start with the width-one Sadowsky band of length \(\mu\). Let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound, and choose \(D_\mu>S_\mu\).

Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{\mu}{\beta}=\frac1\lambda.
\]
Define
\[
c=a\lambda=\frac{a\beta}{\mu}>1,
\qquad
L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]
Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\). Its width is \(c\), its length is \(L\), and its ruling-span bound is \(cS_\mu\). Apply the secant PL lemma with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]

We need a strict length margin. For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)=c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
At \(a=1\), we have \(c=\lambda\), and
\[
\Delta_1(s)=\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}>0
\]
because \(\lambda>1\). By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\) and continuity in \(a\), for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m
\]
and apply the secant PL lemma to obtain \(\mathcal T_0\) and \(F_0\).

Define the anisotropic affine map
\[
A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=(ax+L,c-cy)
=H(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to the Sadowsky deck map \(H\).

Set
\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

We verify the squeeze inequalities. If \(pq\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\), then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\) of length \(a|p-q|\). Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(t\) be the horizontal span of \(A(p)A(q)\) in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). By the secant lemma,
\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), with \(0\le s\le D_\mu\). Since \(A(p)\) and \(A(q)\) lie on opposite boundary components of the width-\(c\) strip,
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]
The original horizontal span is \(t/a\), and the original vertical separation is \(1\). Because \(c/a=\lambda\),
\[
|p-q|
=
\sqrt{\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)^2+1}
=
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
The secant lemma gives
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(Ap)-F_0(Aq)|
\ge c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]
Since \(\varepsilon<m\le \Delta_a(s)\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
>
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Therefore \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

Finally, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\Longleftrightarrow Ap=H^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\Longleftrightarrow p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A\). Hence \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), and every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. Therefore the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

1. **Sadowsky reference patch.** Insert an explicit citation to Sadowsky’s construction, preferably via the Hinz–Fried translation. State that the construction gives a developable Möbius band made from three planar and three semicylindrical pieces with rods of diameters \(d,d,2d\), and that the formula
\[
b\le \frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}
\]
is exactly the half-width bound. With strict inequality, all possible nonincident contacts are avoided; since the quotient surface has finitely many compact pieces, nonincident pieces have positive separation. The planar/cylindrical development is locally Euclidean, so the realization is path-isometric, and the generator lines give the required full-width straight-ruling foliation.

2. **Face-to-face refinement patch.** For two nonboundary edges in a clean triangulation, their interiors cannot cross transversely: near a transverse intersection, each incident triangle occupies one open side of its supporting line, and two open half-planes bounded by nonparallel lines always overlap in a sector, contradicting disjoint triangle interiors. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, their supporting line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points, so the two edges have the same endpoints and hence coincide. Thus the only non-face-to-face phenomenon is the presence of extra vertices along boundary edges. By local finiteness, each compact boundary edge contains only finitely many original boundary vertices. Subdivide at those vertices and cone to the opposite vertex. Boundary subedges are strictly shortened because the original boundary edge is affinely shortened, and new interior cevians are strictly lengthened by the concavity argument already given. This preserves local finiteness and \(G_\beta\)-invariance.

3. **Leaf-graph patch.** In the quotient Möbius band, every nonboundary edge is an interior edge of a surface triangulation and is incident to exactly two triangles. Hence the leaf graph is finite and 2-regular, so each component is a circle. For a component \(C\), let \(U_C\) be the union of triangles represented by \(C\). Across nonboundary edges, both adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive fan triangles share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes, so the whole fan lies in one \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is open and closed in the connected Möbius band, so the quotient leaf graph is connected and hence a single circle. The same local argument applies in \(\Sigma\), giving a connected lift. Since no nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) fixes a lifted leaf, the lifted graph cannot be a finite cover of the quotient circle; it is the infinite cover, homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\). One circuit in the quotient corresponds to applying \(G_\beta\), which swaps top and bottom endpoints and therefore reverses the oriented image line.

4. **Secant PL embedding patch.** Work on one compact quotient Sadowsky band. Include all seam rulings in the subdivision. On planar pieces, the secant map agrees with the original isometry. On cylindrical pieces, if consecutive generator rulings have angular separation \(<\pi\), the chordal quadrilateral strips form embedded inscribed polygonal cylinders. Near seams, adjacent pieces have the same tangent plane; for sufficiently fine subdivisions, the adjacent secant triangles are graphs over this common tangent plane with disjoint projected domains except along the shared seam. For nonlocal intersections, use compactness: because the original Sadowsky band is embedded with positive clearance, pairs of nonincident compact subsets have some separation \(\eta>0\). The secant maps converge uniformly to the original embedding as the mesh goes to zero, so choosing mesh small enough that the uniform error is \(<\eta/3\) prevents new nonlocal intersections. This proves the quotient PL surface is embedded, hence the lifted map satisfies
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q).
\]
The edge-length estimates in the proof then apply unchanged.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

- [Borsuk–Ulam theorem] NOT AN EXTERNAL DEPENDENCY — The proof names an “Oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma,” but proves the needed lemma directly by a degree argument. The classical standard theorem is the antipodal coincidence theorem for maps \(S^n\to\mathbb R^n\), not the exact oriented-line variant used here. ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Borsuk%E2%80%93Ulam_theorem?utm_source=openai))
