original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
claim_verified: As written, the proof rigorously establishes the lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\). The upper bound is established modulo the cited Sadowsky construction and the routine PL-approximation details above. With those patches inserted, the proof establishes that the realized set is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), hence its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).
solution_verified:
## Claim

A positive number \(\beta\) is realized if and only if
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

## Proof

Write \(|\cdot|\) for Euclidean length. A clean triangle has exactly one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it its boundary edge.

### 1. The lower bound

Assume first that \(\beta\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

#### Lemma 1: cevians are lengthened

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, with \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) its boundary edge. If \(A=f|_\Delta\), then every segment from \(a\) to a point of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

Indeed, set \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\) and
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, and hence
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
So every cevian is strictly lengthened.

#### Lemma 2: reduction to a face-to-face triangulation

We may refine \(\mathcal T\), equivariantly under \(G_\beta\), so that it is face-to-face, while keeping the same map \(f\) and preserving all squeeze inequalities.

Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely, because two triangle interiors would then overlap in a sector. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then they have the same endpoints on \(\mathbb R\times\{0\}\) and \(\mathbb R\times\{1\}\), hence coincide. Thus the only possible non-face-to-face behavior occurs along boundary edges.

For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at all boundary vertices lying on it. Local finiteness makes this a finite subdivision on each triangle. The new nonboundary edges are cevians of old triangles, so they are strictly lengthened by Lemma 1; the new boundary edges are subsegments of old shortened boundary edges, hence are strictly shortened. Performing this subdivision on all \(G_\beta\)-orbits gives a \(G_\beta\)-invariant face-to-face clean triangulation.

Henceforth assume \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face.

#### Lemma 3: the quotient is an embedded PL Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
Then \(M_\beta\) is a Möbius band whose boundary has flat length \(2\beta\). The map \(f\) descends to a finite PL embedding
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]

The orbit condition gives \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends. The same condition says the descended map is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

The quotient triangulation is finite: a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, and every triangle orbit has a representative meeting it.

Also, \(f\) is injective on each triangle. If an affine restriction \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective, then there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), but every nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\), contradiction.

Thus the quotient is a finite PL embedded Möbius band. Since every boundary edge is strictly shortened and \(F\) is affine on boundary edges, every nontrivial boundary subarc is strictly shortened in image path length.

#### Lemma 4: the leaf graph is a circle

In each clean triangle, consider the interval of cevians from the opposite vertex to the boundary edge. Glue two interval endpoints when the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge. The resulting finite graph \(\Lambda_M\) is homeomorphic to \(S^1\).

Every nonboundary edge is incident to two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Hence each component is a circle. To see connectedness, take a component \(C\) and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals belong to \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Around any boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, so the whole fan lies in the same \(U_C\). Thus \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected Möbius band \(M_\beta\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\).

The lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected by the same open-closed argument upstairs in \(\Sigma\). The covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is infinite, because no nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
An even nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints horizontally, so cannot fix the leaf. An odd power would have to swap the endpoints, forcing simultaneously
\[
s=(2n+1)\beta,\qquad s=-(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image is a nondegenerate segment by Lemma 1, so it determines an oriented line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line varies continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). Moreover \(G_\beta\) reverses the orientation of the image line, because it swaps the bottom and top endpoints while \(f\circ G_\beta=f\).

#### Lemma 5: oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct lines in the family intersect orthogonally.

Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Extend \((u(s),m(s))\) to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and
\[
(a,b)(s+\delta,1-\delta)=-(a,b)(s,\delta).
\]

If \((a,b)\) never vanished, then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
would define a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). The bottom circle \(\delta=0\) is constant, so has degree \(0\). But on the middle circle \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2),
\]
which forces odd degree. Contradiction. Thus \((a,b)=0\) somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, if the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then \(a=0\) gives \(u\perp v\), while
\[
b=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v)=0
\]
says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar. Hence they intersect, and they do so orthogonally.

#### Lemma 6: boundary arc lengths of a lifted leaf

Let a lifted leaf have endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+\sigma,1).
\]
Then
\[
|\sigma|<\beta.
\]

If \(\sigma=\beta\), then the two endpoints are related by \(G_\beta\), so their \(f\)-images coincide, contradicting nondegeneracy of the leaf image. The case \(\sigma=-\beta\) is identical.

If \(\sigma>\beta\), then the leaf from \((0,0)\) to \((\sigma,1)\) intersects its \(G_\beta\)-translate from \((\beta,1)\) to \((\sigma+\beta,0)\) in their interiors. Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\) on the same leaf with \(z=G_\beta w\), forcing \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity on the leaf. The case \(\sigma<-\beta\) is analogous.

In the quotient boundary circle, the top point \((x+\sigma,1)\) is equivalent to the bottom point with coordinate \(x+\sigma-\beta\). Since \(|\sigma|<\beta\), the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of the quotient leaf have flat lengths
\[
\beta+\sigma,\qquad \beta-\sigma.
\]
Thus if one of the two arcs has length \(\beta+t\), then the flat length of the leaf is
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

#### Lemma 7: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Apply Lemmas 4 and 5 to one fundamental interval of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). We obtain two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines intersect orthogonally at a point \(P\).

Since \(F\) is an embedding, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel them so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\). Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). If \(y\) denotes the distance from \(Q\) to the line through \(F(T)\), then orthogonality gives
\[
y=|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
But \(B\) is strictly lengthened and has flat length at least \(1\), so
\[
y>1.
\]

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc between the endpoints of \(T\) that contains \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
By Lemma 6, the flat length of \(T\) is \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\), hence
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

Since \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and boundary arcs are shortened,
\[
\beta-t>\ell_F(H)\ge L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

The path \(F(D)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\), so
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
For a point at distance \(y\) from the line through a segment of length \(L\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Since \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, the first term being \(<\sqrt3\) would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second being \(<\sqrt3\) would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\). Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

So every realized number is strictly larger than \(\sqrt3\).

---

### 2. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

We use the following classical construction of Sadowsky.

#### Sadowsky construction

Let \(l>0\), \(b>0\), \(d>0\) satisfy
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}. \tag{S}
\]
Then the flat Möbius rectangle
\[
M_{l,2b}=([0,l]\times[0,2b])/(0,Y)\sim(l,2b-Y)
\]
admits an embedded path-isometric realization in \(\mathbb R^3\), made of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces, glued \(C^1\)-smoothly along full-width generator rulings. The surface has a continuous full-width ruling foliation, and each ruling maps to a straight segment.

In Sadowsky’s construction, rods of diameters \(d,d,2d\) give three planar and three semicylindrical pieces, and the maximal half-width is exactly \(l/(2\sqrt3)-3\pi d/4\); strict inequality gives positive clearance from self-contact. The planar pieces are Euclidean motions of flat pieces, and the cylindrical pieces are standard unrollings onto circular half-cylinders, so the resulting surface is path-isometric. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

In particular, if \(\mu>\sqrt3\), then there is such a band of length \(\mu\) and width \(1\): take \(l=\mu\), \(b=\frac12\), and choose \(d>0\) sufficiently small.

#### Lemma 8: ruling coordinates in the lift

Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be the lift of a Sadowsky band of length \(L\) and width \(c\), with deck transformation
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Then
\[
E\circ H=E,
\]
and because the quotient surface is embedded,
\[
E(P)=E(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

The lifted ruling foliation consists of straight segments
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)]
\]
for a continuous strictly increasing function \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\). Define
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
Then
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L,\qquad \phi^2(x)=x+2L,\qquad \phi(x)>x,
\]
and
\[
\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|<\infty.
\]

The continuity of \(h\) follows from continuity of the ruling foliation. If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), then the two straight lifted rulings cross or share an endpoint, contradicting that they form a foliation. Thus \(h\) is strictly increasing.

The deck map sends
\[
(x,0)\mapsto(x+L,c),\qquad (h(x),c)\mapsto(h(x)+L,0).
\]
Therefore \(H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)}\) with \(\phi(x)=h(x)+L\), and its top endpoint gives
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Hence
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
Since \(\phi\) is increasing, \(\phi(x)\le x\) would imply \(\phi^2(x)\le x\), impossible. Thus \(\phi(x)>x\).

Finally,
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]
so \(|h(x)-x|\) is \(\phi\)-invariant. Every \(\phi\)-orbit meets the compact interval \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\), so \(|h(x)-x|\) is globally bounded.

#### Lemma 9: invariant secant PL approximation

Let \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\) be as in Lemma 8, and set
\[
S=\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\) iff \(P=H^k(Q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\);
2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]
4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]
Choose \(x_0\in\mathbb R\), and partition
\[
x_0=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so finely that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]
and so that every seam ruling between Sadowsky pieces in one quotient period is included. Refine further if necessary so that on each cylindrical piece, consecutive chosen rulings have angular separation \(<\pi\), and small enough for the embeddedness argument below.

Extend by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]
Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The segments \(R_i=[B_i,T_i]\) cut the strip into clean quadrilaterals
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]
Triangulate \(Q_i\) by one of its two diagonals. Since \(H\) swaps the two diagonal types, choose the diagonals for \(0\le i<N\), then choose the diagonals for \(N\le i<2N\) as their \(H\)-images, and extend by \(H^2\). This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation.

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over each triangle. Then \(F_0\circ H=F_0\).

For embeddedness, work on the compact quotient band. On planar Sadowsky pieces, the secant PL surface agrees exactly with the original planar surface. On cylindrical pieces, the chosen generator rulings determine chordal faces of an inscribed polygonal cylinder; for angular separation \(<\pi\), these faces are embedded and nonadjacent faces inside the same cylindrical piece are disjoint.

Near each seam ruling, the adjacent pieces meet with the same tangent plane. In a small seam neighborhood, both original pieces are graphs over that tangent plane on opposite sides of the seam in the parameter domain. For sufficiently fine mesh, the adjacent PL facets are also graphs over the same tangent plane and meet only along the seam.

Finally, away from a small neighborhood of the diagonal in the compact parameter square, the embedded original Sadowsky surface has positive separation. The secant PL surfaces converge uniformly to the Sadowsky surface as the mesh goes to \(0\), so for sufficiently fine mesh there are no nonlocal self-intersections. Hence the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting gives the quotient-injectivity in item 1.

Boundary edges are nonexpanded because their images are chords of boundary arcs of a path-isometric surface.

For a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\),
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
For a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Because \(|T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho,
\]
and
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le |T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\\
&\ge r-\rho\\
&>d-2\rho\\
&>d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The other diagonal type is identical, using \(|B_i-B_{i+1}|<\rho\).

Finally, ruling edges have horizontal span at most \(S\). A diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span
\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<S+\rho<D,
\]
and similarly for the other diagonal type.

#### Lemma 10: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
By Sadowsky’s construction, there is a width-one, length-\(\mu\) embedded path-isometric band. Let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling displacement bound from Lemma 8, and choose
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

Set
\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]
Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac1\lambda.
\]
Define
\[
c=a\lambda,\qquad L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]
Then \(c>1\). Scale the Sadowsky band by \(c\). It now has width \(c\), length \(L\), and ruling displacement bound \(cS_\mu\).

For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)=c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
When \(a=1\), we have \(c=\lambda\), so
\[
\Delta_1(s)=\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}>0.
\]
By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\), for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]
Apply Lemma 9 to the scaled band with
\[
D=cD_\mu
\]
and this \(\varepsilon\). We obtain an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\), where
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]

Now define the affine map
\[
A:\Sigma\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],\qquad A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=A(x+\beta,1-y)
=(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=H(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus
\[
A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A.
\]

Let
\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),\qquad f=F_0\circ A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

For a boundary edge \(pq\),
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=a|p-q|.
\]
Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\le a|p-q|<|p-q|.
\]
Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge. Then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\). Let its horizontal span be \(t\). Lemma 9 gives
\[
t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), with \(0\le s<D_\mu\). In \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\),
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]
In the original strip \(\Sigma\), the horizontal span is \(t/a=\lambda s\), so
\[
|p-q|=\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
By Lemma 9,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\ge c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]
Since \(\varepsilon<m\le\Delta_a(s)\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|>\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}=|p-q|.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally, for any \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff F_0(Ap)=F_0(Aq)\\
&\iff Ap=H^k(Aq)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\end{aligned}
\]
Therefore \(\beta\) is realized.

---

We have shown that every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), and that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. Hence the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
so its infimum is
\[
\boxed{\sqrt3}.
\]

The proof’s modern wording “embedded path-isometric realization,” “\(C^1\)-smoothly glued,” and “full-width ruling foliation” is not stated as a formal theorem in exactly those words, but follows from the described planar/cylindrical developable construction and tangent-plane gluing. - ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 4’s proof that the leaf graph is a single circle, and that its lift is \(\mathbb R\), needs the full open/closed argument and the covering/stabilizer argument written out.

The source supports the construction, but the proof should explicitly say it is using the standard modern interpretation of Sadowsky’s planar/cylindrical developable band.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 9’s invariant secant PL approximation, especially quotient embeddedness of \(F_0\), is only sketched. This is repairable by a standard compactness/local-graph PL approximation argument, but the argument should be inserted.
</MINOR_GAPS>

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Patch for Lemma 2.**  
   Insert the following planar-complex argument. Let \(e,e'\) be nonboundary edges. Each is a straight segment joining the two boundary components. If their relative interiors meet transversely at \(z\), choose a small disk \(U\) about \(z\) containing no vertices and meeting only the finitely many edges through \(z\). The edge \(e\) separates \(U\) into two half-disks, and because the triangles cover \(\Sigma\) with disjoint interiors, each side of \(e\) near \(z\) is occupied by triangle interiors. A transverse edge \(e'\) crosses from one side of \(e\) to the other, so one of the two triangles incident to \(e'\) necessarily occupies a sector already occupied by a triangle incident to \(e\), contradicting disjointness of interiors. Thus nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, the line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points, so the two edges have the same endpoints and coincide. Therefore all non-face-to-face behavior occurs only along boundary edges. Subdivide each boundary edge at all boundary vertices lying on it; local finiteness makes this finite on each edge. The new nonboundary edges are cevians and are lengthened by Lemma 1, while new boundary subedges are shortened. This yields the claimed equivariant face-to-face refinement.

2. **Patch for Lemma 4.**  
   In the quotient triangulation, every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of the leaf graph has valence \(2\); hence each component is a circle. For connectedness, for a component \(C\) let \(K_C\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). This set is closed because the quotient triangulation is finite. It is also open: interior points of its triangles are interior; points on nonboundary edges have both adjacent triangles in \(K_C\); points in the relative interior of boundary edges have a one-sided neighborhood inside the unique incident triangle; and at a boundary vertex the incident triangles form a fan in which consecutive triangles share nonboundary edges, so the entire fan lies in \(K_C\). Since the Möbius band is connected, \(K_C\) is the whole band, so the leaf graph is connected and hence is \(S^1\).  
   The same proof upstairs gives connectedness of the lifted leaf graph. The lifted graph covers the quotient circle. If the lifted graph were compact, some nonzero deck transformation would act trivially on it. But a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x,0)\) and \((x+\sigma,1)\). An even nonzero power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints by \(2n\beta\), so cannot fix the leaf. An odd power would have to swap endpoints, forcing simultaneously \(\sigma=(2n+1)\beta\) and \(\sigma=-(2n+1)\beta\), impossible. Hence the covering is infinite and the connected lifted leaf graph is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).

3. **Patch for Lemma 7’s elementary geometry.**  
   Add that two distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the surface: in one triangle distinct cevians meet only at the opposite boundary vertex, and across adjacent triangles identical nonboundary-edge leaves have already been glued to the same graph point. Thus if two distinct image segments both contained the orthogonal intersection point \(P\) in their relative interiors, the embedding \(F\) would identify two distinct interior points.  
   For the broken-line estimate, if \(D\) is the boundary arc from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(q\), then \(F(D)\) is a path from \(A\) to \(C\) through \(Q=F(q)\), so its length is at least \(|A-Q|+|Q-C|\). After placing \(A=(-L/2,0)\), \(C=(L/2,0)\), and \(Q=(u,y)\) in the plane perpendicular/parallel decomposition, convexity gives
   \[
   |A-Q|+|Q-C|
   =\sqrt{(u+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(u-L/2)^2+y^2}
   \ge 2\sqrt{(L/2)^2+y^2}
   =\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
   \]
   This justifies the quantitative step leading to \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

4. **Patch for the Sadowsky black box.**  
   State explicitly: For \(l,b,d>0\) satisfying \(b<l/(2\sqrt3)-3\pi d/4\), Sadowsky’s planar/cylindrical construction gives an embedded developable Möbius band isometric to the flat rectangle \([0,l]\times[0,2b]\) with opposite short sides identified by a half-twist. The pieces are Euclidean planar pieces and standard unrollings of half-cylinders; adjacent pieces meet along common generators with matching tangent planes; strict inequality gives positive separation from the first possible self-contact. The ruling generators give a continuous full-width foliation by straight image segments. This is the precise form of the cited construction needed in Lemmas 8–10. The source verifies the rods, the three planar/three semicylindrical pieces, and the half-width formula. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

5. **Patch for Lemma 9 embeddedness.**  
   Work on the compact quotient band. Include every seam ruling in the mesh. On planar pieces the secant PL map equals the original isometric planar map. On each cylindrical piece, consecutive rulings with angular separation \(<\pi\) produce the usual inscribed polygonal cylinder; its faces are embedded and nonadjacent faces in the same cylindrical piece are disjoint. Near a seam, because the planar and cylindrical pieces share a tangent plane along the seam generator, sufficiently small mesh stars on both sides are graphs over that common tangent plane over disjoint half-neighborhoods, meeting only along the seam.  
   For nonlocal intersections, choose a metric on the compact quotient domain. Since the Sadowsky map \(E\) is embedded, for every sufficiently small \(\eta>0\),
   \[
   \gamma=\min\{|E(p)-E(q)|:\operatorname{dist}(p,q)\ge\eta\}>0.
   \]
   The secant PL maps converge uniformly to \(E\) as mesh size tends to \(0\). Choose the mesh so fine that the uniform error is \(<\gamma/3\) and the local graph conditions above hold on an \(\eta\)-Lebesgue cover. Then equal PL images cannot occur for pairs at distance \(\ge\eta\) by the separation bound, and cannot occur for pairs at distance \(<\eta\) by local graph embeddedness. Thus the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting gives
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q).
   \]
   The remaining edge-length estimates in Lemma 9 then prove the required nonexpansion on boundary edges and \(\varepsilon\)-almost preservation on nonboundary edges.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
claim_verified: After inserting the routine patches above and explicitly citing the Sadowsky construction, the proof establishes the full claimed result: \(\beta\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Consequently, the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\). As written, the proof is conditional on the unexpanded Sadowsky citation and the sketched PL-embedding and leaf-graph details.
solution_verified:
## Claim

A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently, the set of realized numbers is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

---

# Proof

## 1. The lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

### 1.1. Cevian lengthening and face-to-face refinement

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite boundary vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave and therefore lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Thus every cevian from \(a\) to a point of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

We may refine \(\mathcal T\) so that it is face-to-face. Indeed, two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely in their interiors: near a transverse intersection, each incident triangle occupies one open side of its supporting line, and any two open half-planes bounded by nonparallel lines overlap in a sector, contradicting disjointness of triangle interiors. Two nonboundary edges also cannot partially overlap: a nonboundary edge lies on a nonhorizontal line, and such a line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points, so two collinear nonboundary edges have the same endpoints and coincide.

Thus the only possible non-face-to-face phenomenon is subdivision along boundary edges. For each original triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on it, and cone those new boundary vertices to the opposite vertex. Local finiteness guarantees only finitely many such vertices on each compact boundary edge. Boundary subedges remain strictly shortened because \(f\) is affine and strictly shortens the original boundary edge. New nonboundary edges are cevians, hence are strictly lengthened by the preceding paragraph. This refinement remains clean, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Henceforth assume \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face.

---

### 1.2. The quotient Möbius band and the induced PL embedding

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
M_\beta=([0,\beta]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y),
\]
so \(M_\beta\) is a Möbius band whose boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because the realization condition gives \(f(G_\beta p)=f(p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The orbit condition says exactly that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed, even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while odd powers translate horizontally by an odd multiple of \(\beta\) and reverse \(y\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then, because it is affine on a two-dimensional triangle, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

No closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If \(z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\) with \(k\neq0\), then \(w=G_\beta^{-k}z\in\Delta\) and \(f(w)=f(z)\). Injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(w=z\), impossible because the \(G_\beta\)-action is free.

Every triangle orbit has a representative meeting \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\). By local finiteness, only finitely many triangles meet this compact rectangle. Hence the quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\) is finite, and \(F\) is a finite PL embedding.

---

### 1.3. The leaf graph

Inside each clean triangle, consider the family of cevians from the vertex opposite the boundary edge to points of the boundary edge. The two endpoint cevians are the two nonboundary sides. For the quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\), form a graph \(\Lambda_M\) as follows: each triangle contributes one closed interval of leaves, and two interval endpoints are identified exactly when the corresponding nonboundary sides are the same edge of the triangulation.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Therefore each component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

We prove \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose leaf intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes, so the whole fan belongs to one \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected surface \(M_\beta\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Let \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) be the lifted leaf graph in \(\Sigma\). The same fan argument shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering map.

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
An even nonzero power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it cannot preserve the leaf. An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) would have to interchange the endpoints, forcing simultaneously
\[
s=(2n+1)\beta,\qquad -s=(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Hence no nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) fixes a lifted leaf.

Therefore \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is the infinite connected cover of a circle, so
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Since every leaf is strictly lengthened, its image is a nondegenerate straight segment, hence determines an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

If a lifted leaf \(\ell\) has endpoints \(b\in\mathbb R\times\{0\}\) and \(t\in\mathbb R\times\{1\}\), then \(G_\beta\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has endpoints \(G_\beta t\) and \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image orientation is reversed. Thus, after one circuit of \(\Lambda_M\), the oriented support line becomes the same line with the opposite orientation.

---

### 1.4. A lemma on oriented lines

We use the following elementary topological lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_t\), \(0\le t\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct lines in the family are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous representatives \((u(t),m(t))\) for \(0\le t\le1\), and extend to all \(t\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(t+1),m(t+1))=-(u(t),m(t)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
These functions are well-defined on \(S^1\times[0,1]\). Moreover,
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and
\[
(a,b)(s+\delta,1-\delta)=-(a,b)(s,\delta).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
is a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\phi\left(s+\frac12,\frac12\right)=-\phi\left(s,\frac12\right).
\]
A map \(\gamma:S^1\to S^1\) satisfying \(\gamma(s+\frac12)=-\gamma(s)\) has odd degree. But the middle circle \(\delta=\frac12\) is homotopic in the cylinder to the bottom circle \(\delta=0\), where \(\phi\) is constant, so its degree must be \(0\). Contradiction.

Thus \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). The equation \(a=0\) says the corresponding directions \(u,v\) are perpendicular. If the two oriented lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the two lines are nonparallel; the vanishing of this scalar triple product says they are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying the lemma to one circuit of the quotient leaf circle, we obtain two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

---

### 1.5. The metric estimate

Let the image support lines of \(T\) and \(B\) be perpendicular and intersect at \(P\). Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the domain, though they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the leaves if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\) at \(P\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
Every lifted leaf joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and every leaf is strictly lengthened. Therefore
\[
y\ge |F(B)|>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because otherwise \(Q\) would lie on the support line of \(T\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the image segment \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
Let \(D,H\) be the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\), with \(q\in D\). Since the boundary length of \(M_\beta\) is \(2\beta\), write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(\ell_0\) denotes flat boundary length. Because the endpoints of \(T\) are distinct, \(|t|<\beta\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Parametrize the boundary circle by arclength so that a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\pmod{2\beta}\), while a top point \((u,1)\) has coordinate \(u+\beta\pmod{2\beta}\). Thus the two boundary arc lengths between the endpoints of \(T\) are determined by
\[
s\equiv t\pmod{2\beta}
\quad\text{or}\quad
s\equiv -t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since \(|t|<\beta\), it follows that
\[
|s|\ge |t|.
\]
Hence the flat length of \(T\) is at least \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\). Since \(T\) is strictly lengthened,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

The boundary arc \(H\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\). Boundary arcs are strictly shortened, so
\[
L\le \ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(\ell_F\) denotes Euclidean length of the image path. Combining this with (1) gives
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

The boundary arc \(D\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Since \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}<\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

From (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), impossible.

Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

This proves the lower bound.

---

# 2. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

We use the classical Sadowsky three-cylinder construction. In the form needed here, it says the following: if \(l>0\), \(b>0\), \(d>0\), and
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4},
\]
then the flat Möbius rectangle
\[
([0,l]\times[-b,b])/(0,t)\sim(l,-t)
\]
has an embedded developable, path-isometric realization in \(\mathbb R^3\). The realization consists of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces supported on cylinders of diameters \(d,d,2d\), adjacent pieces meet tangentially along full-width generator segments, and the surface has a continuous foliation by full-width straight rulings. With strict inequality, nonincident pieces have positive separation. This is Sadowsky’s 1930 construction, in the Hinz–Fried translation and formulation. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1408.3034))

Let \(\mu>\sqrt3\). Choose \(d>0\) so small that
\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Applying Sadowsky’s construction with \(l=\mu\) and \(b=\frac12\), we get an embedded path-isometric Möbius band of width \(1\) and length \(\mu\). Write its lift as
\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3,
\]
with deck map
\[
H_\mu(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y).
\]
Then
\[
E_\mu\circ H_\mu=E_\mu,
\]
and because the quotient band is embedded,
\[
E_\mu(P)=E_\mu(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H_\mu^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives a width-\(c\), length-\(L=\mu c\) lift
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]

---

## 2.1. Ruling coordinates and bounded span

The lifted Sadowsky band is foliated by full-width straight rulings. Thus every bottom point \((x,0)\) lies on a unique ruling whose top endpoint is \((h(x),c)\). Write
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]
The function \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous. If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), then the two straight segments \(R_{x_1}\) and \(R_{x_2}\) would intersect or share a top endpoint, contradicting the ruling foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing. Since every top boundary point belongs to a unique ruling, \(h\) is onto; therefore \(h\) is an increasing homeomorphism.

The deck transformation sends rulings to rulings. Since
\[
H(x,0)=(x+L,c),\qquad H(h(x),c)=(h(x)+L,0),
\]
we have
\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},\qquad \phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
Thus the top endpoint of \(R_{\phi(x)}\) is \((x+L,c)\), so
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
Because \(\phi\) is increasing, this implies \(\phi(x)>x\) for all \(x\).

The horizontal spans of the rulings are uniformly bounded. Choose \(x_0\), and define
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
Since
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]
the same bound holds on every \(\phi\)-translate of \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\). Therefore
\[
|h(x)-x|\le S
\]
for all \(x\in\mathbb R\).

---

## 2.2. Secant PL approximation

We need a PL approximation of the Sadowsky band.

**Lemma.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be a scaled Sadowsky lift with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y),
\]
and let \(S\) be the global ruling-span bound above. For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   P=H^k(Q)
   \]
   for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\);

2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;

3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]

4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]

Choose a finite partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so fine that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho,
\]
and include among the \(x_i\) all seam rulings of the Sadowsky decomposition in one fundamental interval. Extend by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The consecutive rulings \(B_iT_i\) and \(B_{i+1}T_{i+1}\) bound a clean quadrilateral
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]
For \(0\le i<N\), triangulate \(Q_i\) using the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Since
\[
H(B_i)=T_{i+N},\qquad H(T_i)=B_{i+N},
\]
the image under \(H\) uses the opposite diagonal in \(Q_{i+N}\). Extend the triangulation by \(H\)-invariance. This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. Then \(F_0\circ H=F_0\).

It remains to choose the mesh sufficiently fine so that the quotient PL map is embedded. Work on the compact quotient Sadowsky band. Because the subdivision includes every seam ruling, each small quadrilateral lies inside a single planar or cylindrical piece, except along its boundary seams. On planar pieces, the secant map agrees exactly with the original planar isometry. On cylindrical pieces, if consecutive generator rulings have angular separation \(<\pi\), the chordal quadrilateral strips form embedded inscribed polygonal cylinders. Near a seam, the adjacent pieces have the same tangent plane; for sufficiently fine subdivision, the adjacent secant triangles are graphs over this common tangent plane with projected domains meeting only along the seam.

These local statements give a finite open cover of the compact quotient band on which the PL secant maps are injective for all sufficiently small mesh. For nonlocal intersections, let \(U_1,\dots,U_m\) be such a cover and define
\[
K=\{(p,q): p,q\text{ do not both lie in any }U_j\}.
\]
Then \(K\) is compact and avoids the diagonal. Since the original Sadowsky realization is embedded, there is \(\eta>0\) such that
\[
|E(p)-E(q)|\ge \eta
\]
for all \((p,q)\in K\). The secant maps converge uniformly to \(E\) as the mesh tends to \(0\), so choosing the mesh small enough that the uniform error is \(<\eta/3\) prevents any new nonlocal intersections. Hence the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting back gives the quotient-injectivity statement.

Now prove the edge estimates. A boundary edge maps to the chord between two points of a boundary arc of the path-isometric Sadowsky surface, so its image length is at most its flat length.

For a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\),
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|,
\]
because rulings are mapped isometrically to straight segments.

Consider a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(|T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_{i+1})-E(T_i)|\le |T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&> r-\rho \\
&> d-2\rho \\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The other diagonal type is identical, using \(x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho\).

Finally, every ruling edge has horizontal span at most \(S\). A diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span
\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<S+\rho<D.
\]
The other diagonal type satisfies the same estimate. \(\square\)

---

## 2.3. Pullback to realize any \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta,
\]
and set
\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]
Start with the width-one Sadowsky band of length \(\mu\). Let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound, and choose \(D_\mu>S_\mu\).

Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac{1}{\lambda}.
\]
Define
\[
c=a\lambda>1,\qquad L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]
Scale the width-one Sadowsky band by \(c\). Its width is \(c\), its length is \(L\), and its ruling-span bound is \(cS_\mu\). Apply the secant PL lemma with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]

We need a strict length margin. For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)=c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
At \(a=1\), we have \(c=\lambda\), and
\[
\Delta_1(s)
=
\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}>0
\]
because \(\lambda>1\). By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\), for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m,
\]
and apply the secant PL lemma to obtain \(\mathcal T_0\) and \(F_0\).

Define the anisotropic affine map
\[
\mathcal A:\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
\mathcal A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
\mathcal A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
(ax+L,c-cy)
=
H(\mathcal A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H\).

Set
\[
\mathcal T=\mathcal A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ\mathcal A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

If \(pq\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\), then \(\mathcal A(p)\mathcal A(q)\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\) of length \(a|p-q|\). Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(\mathcal A p)-F_0(\mathcal A q)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(t\) be the horizontal span of \(\mathcal A(p)\mathcal A(q)\) in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). By the secant lemma,
\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), so \(0\le s<D_\mu\). Since \(\mathcal A(p)\) and \(\mathcal A(q)\) lie on opposite boundary components,
\[
|\mathcal A(p)-\mathcal A(q)|=c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]
The original horizontal span is \(t/a\), and the original vertical separation is \(1\). Since \(c/a=\lambda\),
\[
|p-q|
=
\sqrt{\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)^2+1}
=
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
The secant lemma gives
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(\mathcal A p)-F_0(\mathcal A q)|
\ge c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]
Because \(\varepsilon<m\le \Delta_a(s)\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
>
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

Finally, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow F_0(\mathcal A p)=F_0(\mathcal A q) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \mathcal A p=H^k(\mathcal A q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z \\
&\Longleftrightarrow p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that \(\beta\) is realized exactly when \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Therefore the set of realized numbers is
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Precise Sadowsky black box.** Insert the following formulation: for \(l,b,d>0\) with  
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4},
\]
Sadowsky’s construction gives an embedded Möbius rectangle \(([0,l]\times[-b,b])/(0,t)\sim(l,-t)\) made from three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces, with cylinders of diameters \(d,d,2d\). Each piece is the isometric development of a Euclidean planar piece onto either a plane or a circular cylinder; seams are glued along corresponding generator segments, so curve lengths are preserved piecewise and hence the realization is path-isometric. The planes are tangent to the adjacent cylinders, so the ruling foliation extends continuously across seams. The strict inequality gives no contact between nonincident compact pieces; hence their mutual distance is positive.

2. **Leaf graph patch.** For the finite quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\), define \(\Lambda_M\) as a graph with one interval per triangle and endpoint identifications along nonboundary edges. Every nonboundary edge has exactly two incident triangle sides, counted with multiplicity, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite disjoint union of circles. For a component \(C\), let \(U_C\) be the union of all triangles whose leaf intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge both adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a single fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes; hence the whole fan lies in one \(U_C\). Therefore each \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected surface \(M_\beta\), so \(\Lambda_M\) is connected and hence \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\). The same argument gives connectedness of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). A deck transformation cannot fix a lifted leaf with endpoints \((x,0)\), \((x+s,1)\): even powers translate both endpoints by \(2n\beta\), while an odd power would force simultaneously \(s=(2n+1)\beta\) and \(-s=(2n+1)\beta\). Hence the deck action on leaves is free, and \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is a covering. Since the \(G_\beta\)-orbit of any leaf gives infinitely many distinct preimages of one quotient leaf, the cover has infinite degree; the connected infinite cover of \(S^1\) is \(\mathbb R\).

3. **Secant PL embedding patch.** Work on the compact Sadowsky quotient band and choose subdivisions whose mesh tends to zero and which contain all seam rulings. On planar pieces, the secant map agrees with the original affine isometry. On a cylindrical piece, consecutive generator rulings with angular separation \(<\pi\) determine planar chord strips forming an embedded inscribed polygonal cylinder; adjacent strips meet only along their shared generator. Near a seam, choose coordinates with the seam as an axis and the common tangent plane as \(\{z=0\}\). The planar piece lies in this plane, while the cylindrical piece and its sufficiently fine chordal approximants lie on one side of it except along the seam, so no local crossing is introduced. These local facts give a finite cover by neighborhoods on which the secant map is injective. For pairs not lying in a common such neighborhood, compactness and embeddedness of the Sadowsky surface give a separation \(\eta>0\); uniform convergence of the secant maps to the Sadowsky map then prevents nonlocal intersections for sufficiently fine mesh. This proves the quotient PL map is embedded; lifting gives quotient-injectivity.

4. **Boundary-arc and degree details.** If a boundary arc has endpoints in interiors of boundary edges, subdivide it at all boundary vertices. On each subarc, \(f\) is affine with shortening factor \(<1\), so the total image length is strictly less than the flat boundary length. For the degree step, choose a lift \(\theta\) of \(\gamma:S^1\to S^1\). From \(\gamma(s+1/2)=-\gamma(s)\), one gets \(\theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi k\) with constant integer \(k\); hence \(\theta(s+1)-\theta(s)=2\pi(2k+1)\), so the degree is odd.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
