original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
claim_verified: After inserting the routine patches above and using the verified Sadowsky construction as the stated black box, the proof establishes the stronger statement that a positive number \(\beta\) is realized exactly when \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Hence the infimum of the realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\). As written without the patches, the lower bound is essentially complete, while the upper bound is reduced to the Sadowsky construction plus the PL-approximation/embeddedness details in Lemma 9.
solution_verified:
**Claim.** A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if
\[
\beta>\sqrt3 .
\]
Consequently the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

## Proof

Write \(|\cdot|\) for Euclidean length. A clean triangle has exactly one edge contained in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it its **boundary edge**. The other two edges join the two boundary components.

### 1. Necessity: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

#### Lemma 1: cevians are strictly lengthened

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\), and let \(A=f|_\Delta\). Then every segment from \(a\) to a point of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

Indeed, put \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), \(0\le s\le1\), and define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2 .
\]
Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Also \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\), because the two nonboundary edges are lengthened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave and lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
So every cevian from \(a\) to \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened.

#### Lemma 2: reduction to a face-to-face triangulation

We may refine \(\mathcal T\), equivariantly under \(G_\beta\), so that it is face-to-face, while keeping the same map \(f\) and preserving the squeeze inequalities.

Let \(e,e'\) be nonboundary edges. Each is a straight segment joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). If their relative interiors met transversely at a point \(z\), choose a small disk \(U\) about \(z\) containing no vertices and meeting only finitely many triangles. Let \(\tau\) be a triangle incident to \(e\). One side of \(e\) near \(z\) lies in \(\operatorname{int}\tau\). Since \(e'\) crosses \(e\), a small subsegment of \(e'\) lies inside \(\operatorname{int}\tau\). But \(e'\) is an edge of some triangle \(\tau'\), and near any interior point of \(e'\), the interior of \(\tau'\) occupies one side of \(e'\). Taking the point sufficiently close to \(z\), this gives points of \(\operatorname{int}\tau'\) inside \(\operatorname{int}\tau\), contradicting disjointness of triangle interiors.

If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then the common line meets each boundary component in a unique point, so the two edges have the same endpoints and coincide. Therefore nonboundary edges cannot meet except by coincidence or at common boundary endpoints.

Hence the only possible non-face-to-face behavior occurs along boundary edges. Subdivide every boundary edge at every boundary vertex lying on it. Local finiteness implies that each boundary edge is subdivided into finitely many subedges. Inside each old triangle, join the opposite vertex to the new subdivision points. The new nonboundary edges are cevians, hence are strictly lengthened by Lemma 1, while the new boundary edges are subsegments of old shortened boundary edges, hence are strictly shortened. Performing this subdivision on every \(G_\beta\)-orbit gives a \(G_\beta\)-invariant face-to-face clean triangulation.

From now on assume \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face.

#### Lemma 3: the quotient is an embedded PL Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
Then \(M_\beta\) is a Möbius band whose boundary has flat length \(2\beta\), and \(f\) descends to a finite PL embedding
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3 .
\]

Indeed, \(M_\beta\) is obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying \((0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y)\). The orbit condition gives \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), so \(f\) descends to \(F\). The same condition says \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is an embedding.

The quotient triangulation is finite: a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many triangles by local finiteness, and every triangle orbit has a representative meeting that rectangle.

Also, \(f\) is injective on each triangle. If an affine restriction \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective, there would be distinct points of \(\Delta\) arbitrarily close together with the same image. The orbit condition would force them to differ by a nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\), but every nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\), impossible.

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc is strictly shortened in image length: on each boundary edge, \(F\) is affine and has length ratio \(<1\), so every boundary subarc has image path length strictly smaller than its flat length.

#### Lemma 4: the leaf graph is a circle, and its lift is a line

For each clean triangle \(\tau\), consider the interval of cevians from its opposite vertex to its boundary edge. If two adjacent triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the two corresponding endpoints of their cevian intervals. In the quotient \(M_\beta\), this produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), whose points parametrize the quotient leaves.

Every nonboundary edge is an interior edge of the triangulated Möbius band, hence is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\), so every component of \(\Lambda_M\) is a circle.

To prove connectedness, let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(K_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose cevian intervals belong to \(C\). The set \(K_C\) is closed because the quotient triangulation is finite. It is also open. Interior points of its triangles are interior to \(K_C\). Points in the relative interior of a nonboundary edge have both adjacent triangles in \(K_C\). Points in the relative interior of a boundary edge have a one-sided neighborhood inside the unique incident triangle. At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, so membership in \(K_C\) propagates across the entire fan. Thus \(K_C\) is open and closed. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(K_C=M_\beta\). Hence \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\).

The same open-closed argument in the locally finite lifted triangulation of \(\Sigma\) shows that the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. It covers \(\Lambda_M\). If this covering were finite, some nonzero power of \(G_\beta\) would act trivially on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). But no nonzero power fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+\sigma,1).
\]
An even nonzero power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints by \(2n\beta\), so cannot fix the leaf. An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) would have to swap the two endpoints, forcing simultaneously
\[
\sigma=(2n+1)\beta
\quad\text{and}\quad
\sigma=-(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Therefore the covering is infinite, and
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R .
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, its image is a nondegenerate segment, hence determines an oriented line in \(\mathbb R^3\). This oriented line varies continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). Since \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the oriented image line of \(G_\beta(\ell)\) is the reverse of the oriented image line of \(\ell\).

#### Lemma 5: an oriented-line Borsuk-Ulam lemma

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\), and suppose \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct lines in the family intersect orthogonally.

Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Extend \((u(s),m(s))\) to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and
\[
(a,b)(s+\delta,1-\delta)=-(a,b)(s,\delta).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). At \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant, so has degree \(0\). But at \(\delta=\tfrac12\),
\[
\phi(s+\tfrac12,\tfrac12)=-\phi(s,\tfrac12),
\]
which forces the degree to be odd. Contradiction. Thus \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, the two direction vectors are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Thus \(b=0\) says the two nonparallel lines are coplanar. Therefore they intersect, and since \(u\perp v\), they intersect orthogonally.

#### Lemma 6: boundary arc lengths determined by a leaf

Let a lifted leaf have endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+\sigma,1).
\]
Then
\[
|\sigma|<\beta.
\]

If \(\sigma=\beta\), the endpoints are related by \(G_\beta\), so their \(f\)-images coincide, contradicting nondegeneracy of the image leaf. The case \(\sigma=-\beta\) is identical.

If \(\sigma>\beta\), then the leaf from \((x,0)\) to \((x+\sigma,1)\) intersects its \(G_\beta\)-translate in their interiors. Indeed, solving for the intersection gives height
\[
y=\frac{\sigma+\beta}{2\sigma}\in(0,1).
\]
Thus there are distinct points \(z,w\) on the same leaf with \(z=G_\beta(w)\), hence \(f(z)=f(w)\), contradicting injectivity on the image segment. The case \(\sigma<-\beta\) is analogous. Hence \(|\sigma|<\beta\).

In the quotient boundary circle, the top point \((x+\sigma,1)\) is equivalent to the bottom point \((x+\sigma-\beta,0)\). Bottom coordinates are taken modulo \(2\beta\), so the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of the quotient leaf have flat lengths
\[
\beta+\sigma,\qquad \beta-\sigma .
\]
Thus if one of the two boundary arcs has flat length \(\beta+t\), then the flat length of the leaf is
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

#### Lemma 7: the lower bound

Choose a lifted leaf \(\ell\) and a path in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\) from \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta(\ell)\). The oriented image line at the endpoint is the reverse of the oriented image line at the start. By Lemma 5, there are two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines intersect orthogonally at a point \(P\).

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in the surface: inside one triangle, distinct cevians meet only at the opposite boundary vertex, and across adjacent triangles a common nonboundary edge represents the same leaf. Hence if both image segments contained \(P\) in their relative interiors, the embedding \(F\) would identify two distinct interior points. Therefore at least one of the two image segments does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Call that leaf \(B\).

Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\). Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). Let \(y\) be the distance from \(Q\) to the line through \(F(T)\). Since the two support lines meet orthogonally at \(P\),
\[
y=|Q-P|\ge \operatorname{length}(F(B)).
\]
But \(B\) is strictly lengthened and its flat length is at least \(1\), so
\[
y>1.
\]

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the segment \(F(T)\), and put
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc between the endpoints of \(T\) that contains \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. By Lemma 6, for some real \(t\),
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
and the flat length of \(T\) is \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\). Since \(T\) is strictly lengthened,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

The image path \(F(H)\) connects \(A\) to \(C\), so
\[
\beta-t>\ell_F(H)\ge L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

Similarly, \(F(D)\) is a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\), so
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Place \(A=(-L/2,0)\), \(C=(L/2,0)\), and \(Q=(u,y)\) in the plane spanned by the line \(AC\) and the perpendicular from \(Q\) to that line. Then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
=
\sqrt{(u+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(u-L/2)^2+y^2}.
\]
By convexity, this is minimized at \(u=0\), so
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge 2\sqrt{(L/2)^2+y^2}
=\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Since \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\beta+t>\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed,
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3 \iff t<\frac1{\sqrt3},
\]
whereas
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3 \iff t>\frac1{\sqrt3}.
\]
The two inequalities cannot hold simultaneously. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Thus every realized number is strictly larger than \(\sqrt3\).

---

### 2. Sufficiency: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

We use the following standard form of Sadowsky’s developable Möbius band construction.

**Sadowsky theorem.** Let \(l,b,d>0\) satisfy
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Then the flat Möbius rectangle
\[
M_{l,2b}=([0,l]\times[0,2b])/(0,Y)\sim(l,2b-Y)
\]
admits an embedded path-isometric realization in \(\mathbb R^3\), made of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces. The pieces meet along full-width generator rulings with matching tangent planes. The full-width rulings form a continuous foliation, and each ruling maps to a straight segment. The construction uses rods of diameters \(d,d,2d\), consists of three planar and three semicylindrical portions, and gives the half-width bound displayed above. ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/pdf/1408.3034))

In particular, if \(\mu>\sqrt3\), then a length-\(\mu\), width-\(1\) embedded path-isometric Sadowsky band exists: take \(l=\mu\), \(b=\tfrac12\), and choose \(d>0\) sufficiently small.

#### Lemma 8: ruling coordinates in the lift

Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be the lift of a Sadowsky band of length \(L\) and width \(c\), with deck transformation
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Then
\[
E\circ H=E,
\]
and because the quotient surface is embedded,
\[
E(P)=E(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]

The lifted ruling foliation consists of straight full-width segments
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)]
\]
for a continuous strictly increasing function \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\). Define
\[
\phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
Then
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L,\qquad \phi^2(x)=x+2L,\qquad \phi(x)>x,
\]
and
\[
S:=\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|<\infty.
\]

The continuity of \(h\) follows from continuity of the ruling foliation. If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), then the two straight rulings \(R_{x_1},R_{x_2}\) would cross or share an endpoint, contradicting that the rulings form a foliation. Thus \(h\) is strictly increasing.

The deck map sends
\[
(x,0)\mapsto(x+L,c),\qquad (h(x),c)\mapsto(h(x)+L,0).
\]
Hence
\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},\qquad \phi(x)=h(x)+L,
\]
and the top endpoint of \(R_{\phi(x)}\) gives
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Therefore
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
Since \(\phi\) is increasing, \(\phi(x)\le x\) would imply \(\phi^2(x)\le x\), contradicting \(\phi^2(x)=x+2L\). Thus \(\phi(x)>x\).

Finally,
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]
so \(|h(x)-x|\) is \(\phi\)-invariant. The intervals
\[
[\phi^n(x_0),\phi^{n+1}(x_0)]
\]
cover \(\mathbb R\), and \(|h(x)-x|\) is continuous on the compact interval \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\). Hence \(S<\infty\).

#### Lemma 9: invariant secant PL approximation

Let \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\) be as in Lemma 8, and let
\[
S=\sup_{x\in\mathbb R}|h(x)-x|.
\]
For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\) iff \(P=H^k(Q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\);
2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;
3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]
4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

Choose \(0<\rho\) such that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]
Choose \(x_0\in\mathbb R\), and partition
\[
x_0=x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
so finely that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho.
\]
Also include every seam ruling between Sadowsky pieces in one quotient period, and refine on cylindrical pieces so that consecutive chosen rulings have angular separation \(<\pi\). Extend by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The segments
\[
R_i=[B_i,T_i]
\]
cut the strip into clean quadrilaterals
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]
Triangulate each \(Q_i\) by one diagonal. Choose the diagonals for \(0\le i<N\) arbitrarily, choose those for \(N\le i<2N\) to be their \(H\)-images, and extend by \(H^2\). This gives an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over each triangle. By construction,
\[
F_0\circ H=F_0.
\]

We now justify quotient embeddedness. Work on the compact quotient band. Since seam rulings are included in the mesh, every triangle lies in a single planar or cylindrical piece. On planar pieces, the secant PL map agrees exactly with the original planar isometry. On a cylindrical piece, consecutive rulings with angular separation \(<\pi\) produce the usual embedded inscribed polygonal cylinder, and nonadjacent faces in that cylindrical piece are disjoint. Near a seam ruling, the two adjacent Sadowsky pieces share a tangent plane; in a sufficiently small neighborhood of the seam, both pieces are graphs over that plane over disjoint half-neighborhoods in the parameter domain. For sufficiently fine mesh, the adjacent PL facets are also graphs over the same plane and meet only along the seam.

Choose a finite cover of the compact quotient by such local neighborhoods, and let \(\eta>0\) be a Lebesgue number for that cover. Since the original Sadowsky map \(E\) is embedded, the compact set
\[
\{(p,q): \operatorname{dist}(p,q)\ge \eta\}
\]
has positive image separation:
\[
\gamma=\min_{\operatorname{dist}(p,q)\ge\eta}|E(p)-E(q)|>0.
\]
As the mesh tends to \(0\), the secant PL maps converge uniformly to \(E\). Choose the mesh so fine that the uniform error is \(<\gamma/3\) and all local graph conditions above hold. If two quotient points \(p,q\) satisfy \(F_0(p)=F_0(q)\) and \(\operatorname{dist}(p,q)\ge\eta\), then
\[
|E(p)-E(q)|<2\gamma/3,
\]
contradicting the definition of \(\gamma\). If \(\operatorname{dist}(p,q)<\eta\), then \(p,q\) lie in one local graph neighborhood, where \(F_0\) is embedded. Thus the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting gives
\[
F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\iff P=H^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Boundary edges are nonexpanded because their images are chords of boundary arcs of the path-isometric Sadowsky surface, and chord length is at most arc length.

For a nonboundary edge, there are two cases. If the edge is a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\), then \(E\) maps the ruling to a straight segment isometrically, so
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
If the edge is a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(|T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also,
\[
|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})|\le |T_i-T_{i+1}|<\rho.
\]
Therefore
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge r-\rho \\
&>d-2\rho \\
&>d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The other diagonal type \(B_{i+1}T_i\) is identical, using \(|B_{i+1}-B_i|<\rho\). Thus every nonboundary edge is lengthened up to error \(\varepsilon\).

Finally, ruling edges have horizontal span at most \(S<D\). For a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\),
\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<S+\rho<D,
\]
and similarly for the other diagonal type. Hence every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

#### Lemma 10: construction for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose
\[
\sqrt3<\mu<\beta.
\]
By Sadowsky’s theorem, there is a length-\(\mu\), width-\(1\) embedded path-isometric Sadowsky band. Let \(S_\mu\) be the ruling displacement bound from Lemma 8 for its lift, and choose
\[
D_\mu>S_\mu.
\]

Set
\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]
Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac1\lambda.
\]
Put
\[
c=a\lambda,\qquad L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]
Then \(c>1\). Scale the Sadowsky band by the factor \(c\). It now has width \(c\), length \(L\), and ruling displacement bound \(cS_\mu\).

For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)=a\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
When \(a=1\),
\[
\Delta_1(s)=\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}>0,
\]
because \(\lambda>1\). By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\), for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m.
\]

Apply Lemma 9 to the scaled Sadowsky band with
\[
D=cD_\mu.
\]
Since \(cS_\mu<cD_\mu\), the hypothesis \(D>S\) holds. We obtain an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map \(F_0\), where
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]

Define the affine map
\[
A:\Sigma\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],\qquad A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
A(x+\beta,1-y)
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
H(A(x,y)).
\]
Thus
\[
A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A.
\]

Let
\[
\mathcal T=A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),\qquad f=F_0\circ A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

Let \(pq\) be a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=a|p-q|.
\]
Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|\le a|p-q|<|p-q|.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Then \(A(p)A(q)\) is a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\). Let its horizontal span be \(t\). By Lemma 9,
\[
t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), with \(0\le s<D_\mu\). In the scaled Sadowsky strip,
\[
|A(p)-A(q)|=c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]
In the original strip \(\Sigma\), the horizontal span is \(t/a\), so
\[
|p-q|=\sqrt{\left(\frac{t}{a}\right)^2+1}
=\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
By Lemma 9,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(A(p))-F_0(A(q))|
\ge c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]
Since \(c=a\lambda\) and \(\varepsilon<m\le\Delta_a(s)\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
>
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally, for any \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\iff F_0(A(p))=F_0(A(q))\\
&\iff A(p)=H^k(A(q))\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z\\
&\iff p=G_\beta^k(q)\quad\text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z,
\end{aligned}
\]
because \(A\circ G_\beta=H\circ A\) and \(A\) is injective. Therefore \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), and every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. Hence the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]

The modern terms “embedded path-isometric,” “matching tangent planes,” and “continuous ruling foliation” are not stated verbatim, but are the geometric unpacking of the piecewise plane/cylinder inextensible no-overlap construction; the proof should explicitly state these as part of the black-box theorem. - ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 9’s PL embeddedness/uniform-convergence argument is too compressed. In particular, the triangulation uses full-width rulings, so ordinary “mesh diameter tends to \(0\)” is not literally true. The needed convergence and local embeddedness are true for the Sadowsky plane/cylinder pieces, but must be justified using the ruled/cylindrical structure.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 4 uses the quotient as a genuine finite face-to-face triangulation before explicitly ruling out cell self-identifications under the deck group. This is easy to fix using the already-proved injectivity of \(f\) on each lifted triangle.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — Lemma 5’s assertion that \(\phi(s+1/2)=-\phi(s)\) forces odd degree should be written out or cited as the standard \(S^1\)-degree fact.
- EXPOSITION ONLY — The Sadowsky citation is verified in substance, but the proof should explicitly say that it is invoking the modern black-box form with embeddedness, path-isometry, tangent seam matching, and a continuous full-width ruling foliation.
</MINOR_GAPS>

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Quotient triangulation patch.**  
   After proving \(f|_\tau\) is injective for every lifted triangle \(\tau\), add: if two distinct points of the same closed cell of \(\mathcal T\) were identified in \(M_\beta\), then they would differ by a nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\), hence would have the same \(f\)-image, contradicting injectivity on that cell. Thus the quotient map is injective on each closed triangle and edge. Since the lifted triangulation is face-to-face, intersections of quotient cells are exactly common quotient faces. Therefore the quotient is a genuine finite face-to-face triangulation of the Möbius band, and every nonboundary edge is an interior edge incident to exactly two triangles.

2. **Degree detail in Lemma 5.**  
   At \(\delta=1/2\), choose a continuous lift \(\theta:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) of \(\phi(s,1/2)\in S^1\). The relation \(\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2)\) gives
   \[
   \theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi k
   \]
   for a constant integer \(k\). Applying this twice gives
   \[
   \theta(s+1)=\theta(s)+2\pi(1+2k),
   \]
   so the degree is \(1+2k\), odd. This contradicts homotopy through \(\delta\in[0,1/2]\) to the constant map at \(\delta=0\).

3. **Lemma 9 PL approximation patch.**  
   On planar Sadowsky pieces, the secant PL map equals the original affine isometry exactly. On a cylindrical piece of radius \(R\), if adjacent sampled rulings have angular separation \(\alpha\), the PL panel is the usual chordal panel of the cylinder and lies within distance \(R(1-\cos(\alpha/2))\) of the cylindrical surface. Hence, by taking all angular separations sufficiently small, the secant PL maps converge uniformly to \(E\) on every cylindrical piece. If \(\alpha<\pi\), these chordal panels form an embedded inscribed polygonal cylinder.

   At seam rulings, use that the two Sadowsky pieces share the tangent plane along the seam. Choose finitely many seam neighborhoods in which the two original pieces are graphs over that tangent plane and meet only along the seam. For sufficiently fine adjacent ruling mesh, the neighboring PL panels are also graphs over the same plane and meet only along the seam.

   Now take a finite cover of the compact quotient by these local embeddedness neighborhoods and by embedded neighborhoods inside individual planar/cylindrical pieces. Let \(\eta\) be a Lebesgue number. Since \(E\) is embedded, the compact set of pairs \((p,q)\) with \(\operatorname{dist}(p,q)\ge\eta\) has positive image separation \(\gamma\). Refine the ruling mesh so that \(\|F_0-E\|_\infty<\gamma/3\). If \(F_0(p)=F_0(q)\) and \(\operatorname{dist}(p,q)\ge\eta\), then \(|E(p)-E(q)|<2\gamma/3\), contradiction. If \(\operatorname{dist}(p,q)<\eta\), then \(p,q\) lie in one local embeddedness neighborhood, so \(p=q\). Thus the quotient PL map is embedded, and lifting gives \(F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\) iff \(P=H^k(Q)\).

4. **Sadowsky black-box wording.**  
   State explicitly: for \(b<l/(2\sqrt3)-3\pi d/4\), the classical Sadowsky construction gives an embedded piecewise plane/cylinder path-isometry of \(M_{l,2b}\) with the listed full-width rulings. Then for \(\mu>\sqrt3\), set \(l=\mu\), \(b=1/2\), and choose
   \[
   0<d<\frac{2}{3\pi}\left(\frac{\mu}{\sqrt3}-1\right),
   \]
   which makes the inequality strict.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

   Now take a finite cover of the compact quotient by these local embeddedness neighborhoods and by embedded neighborhoods inside individual planar/cylindrical pieces. Let \(\eta\) be a Lebesgue number. Since \(E\) is embedded, the compact set of pairs \((p,q)\) with \(\operatorname{dist}(p,q)\ge\eta\) has positive image separation \(\gamma\). Refine the ruling mesh so that \(\|F_0-E\|_\infty<\gamma/3\). If \(F_0(p)=F_0(q)\) and \(\operatorname{dist}(p,q)\ge\eta\), then \(|E(p)-E(q)|<2\gamma/3\), contradiction. If \(\operatorname{dist}(p,q)<\eta\), then \(p,q\) lie in one local embeddedness neighborhood, so \(p=q\). Thus the quotient PL map is embedded, and lifting gives \(F_0(P)=F_0(Q)\) iff \(P=H^k(Q)\).

4. **Sadowsky black-box wording.**  
   State explicitly: for \(b<l/(2\sqrt3)-3\pi d/4\), the classical Sadowsky construction gives an embedded piecewise plane/cylinder path-isometry of \(M_{l,2b}\) with the listed full-width rulings. Then for \(\mu>\sqrt3\), set \(l=\mu\), \(b=1/2\), and choose
   \[
   0<d<\frac{2}{3\pi}\left(\frac{\mu}{\sqrt3}-1\right),
   \]
   which makes the inequality strict.
parsed.problem_solved: As written, the proof essentially establishes the lower bound \(\beta>\sqrt3\) for realized \(\beta\), and reduces the upper bound to the stated Sadowsky black box plus the missing PL embeddedness/approximation details in Lemma 9. After inserting the routine patches above, it proves the stronger statement that \(\beta\) is realized exactly for \(\beta>\sqrt3\), hence the infimum of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
claim_verified: With the patches above and the verified Sadowsky construction used as a black box, the proof establishes the stronger statement that \(\beta\) is realized exactly when \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Consequently, the realized set is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\). As written, the proof leaves the leaf-graph and PL-embedding justifications too compressed.
solution_verified:
**Claim.** A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if
\[
\beta>\sqrt3 .
\]
Therefore the set of realized numbers is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

---

## 1. The lower bound

Assume \(\beta\) is realized by a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\).

### 1.1. A refinement and a cevian-lengthening fact

Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset \partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge and \(a\) is the opposite vertex. Let \(A=f|_\Delta\). For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2 .
\]
Since the two nonboundary edges are lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, so it lies above its chord:
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+sq(1)>0.
\]
Hence every cevian from \(a\) to a point of the boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\).

We may refine \(\mathcal T\) so that it is face-to-face. Indeed, two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely in their interiors: near a transverse crossing, the interiors of the incident triangles would occupy two open half-planes bounded by nonparallel lines, and any two such half-planes overlap in a sector, contradicting disjointness of triangle interiors. Two nonboundary edges also cannot partially overlap: a nonhorizontal line meets each of \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in a unique point, so two collinear nonboundary edges have the same endpoints and coincide.

Thus the only non-face-to-face phenomenon is subdivision along boundary edges. Subdivide every boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on it, and cone the new boundary subvertices to the opposite vertex. Local finiteness guarantees only finitely many such vertices on each compact boundary edge. The refinement is still clean, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant. Boundary subedges are strictly shortened, and the new nonboundary edges are cevians, hence are strictly lengthened by the preceding paragraph. Replacing \(\mathcal T\) by this refinement, we assume from now on that \(\mathcal T\) is face-to-face.

---

### 1.2. The quotient Möbius band and its PL embedding

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle .
\]
Equivalently,
\[
M_\beta=([0,\beta]\times[0,1])/(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y),
\]
so \(M_\beta\) is a Möbius band whose boundary is a circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Since \(f(G_\beta p)=f(p)\), \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3 .
\]
The orbit condition says precisely that \(F\) is injective. Because \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For \(k\neq0\), every point of \(\Sigma\) is moved by \(G_\beta^k\) a distance at least \(\beta\): even powers translate horizontally by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while odd powers translate horizontally by an odd multiple of \(\beta\) and reverse \(y\).

If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on some triangle \(\Delta\), then, since \(f|_\Delta\) is affine on a genuine two-dimensional triangle, there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f\) is injective on every triangle.

No closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\) with \(k\neq0\), then \(w=G_\beta^{-k}z\in\Delta\), and \(f(w)=f(z)\). Since \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(w=z\), impossible because the \(G_\beta\)-action is free.

Every triangle orbit has a representative meeting the compact rectangle \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\). By local finiteness, only finitely many triangles meet that rectangle. Hence the quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\) is finite, and \(F\) is a finite PL embedding.

---

### 1.3. The leaf graph

Inside every clean triangle, consider the family of cevians joining the vertex opposite the boundary edge to points of the boundary edge. These cevians will be called leaves.

For the finite quotient triangulation of \(M_\beta\), define a graph \(\Lambda_M\) as follows. Each quotient triangle contributes one closed interval parametrizing its leaves. The two endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary sides of the triangle. Whenever two triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the corresponding interval endpoints.

Every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangle sides, counted with multiplicity, so every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite disjoint union of circles.

We show it is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all triangles whose leaf intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a single fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes, so the whole fan belongs to one \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is both open and closed in the connected surface \(M_\beta\). Therefore \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, and so
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1 .
\]

Let \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) be the analogous leaf graph upstairs in \(\Sigma\). The same fan argument, using local finiteness, shows that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The deck action of \(\langle G_\beta\rangle\) acts on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), and the quotient graph is \(\Lambda_M\).

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. Indeed, a lifted leaf has endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
An even nonzero power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it cannot preserve the leaf. An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) would have to interchange the endpoints, forcing
\[
s=(2n+1)\beta
\quad\text{and}\quad
-s=(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Thus the deck action on leaves is free. Hence
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering map. Since the orbit of any lifted leaf under \(\langle G_\beta\rangle\) is infinite, this covering has infinite degree. Therefore the connected covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to S^1\) is the universal cover:
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong \mathbb R .
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Each leaf is strictly lengthened, hence its image is a nondegenerate straight segment and determines an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

Choose one lifted circuit, namely a path in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from a leaf \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\) projecting homeomorphically onto \(\Lambda_M\). If \(\ell\) has endpoints \(b\in y=0\) and \(t\in y=1\), then \(G_\beta\ell\), reoriented from bottom to top, has endpoints \(G_\beta t\) and \(G_\beta b\). Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the image orientation is reversed. Thus one full circuit of \(\Lambda_M\) changes an oriented support line into the same support line with the opposite orientation.

We now use the following elementary lemma.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_t\), \(0\le t\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then two distinct lines in the family are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous representatives \((u(t),m(t))\) for \(0\le t\le1\), and extend them to all \(t\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(t+1),m(t+1))=-(u(t),m(t)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
These are well-defined on \(S^1\times[0,1]\). We have
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0),
\]
and
\[
(a,b)(s+\delta,1-\delta)=-(a,b)(s,\delta).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
is a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). The bottom circle \(\delta=0\) is constant, so it has degree \(0\). On the middle circle \(\delta=\frac12\),
\[
\phi\left(s+\frac12,\frac12\right)=-\phi\left(s,\frac12\right).
\]
If \(\gamma:S^1\to S^1\) satisfies \(\gamma(s+\frac12)=-\gamma(s)\), then \(\gamma\) has odd degree: choosing a lift \(\theta:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) of \(\gamma\), we get
\[
\theta\left(s+\frac12\right)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi k
\]
for a constant integer \(k\), hence
\[
\theta(s+1)-\theta(s)=2\pi(2k+1).
\]
Thus the middle circle has odd degree. But it is homotopic in the cylinder to the bottom circle, which has degree \(0\), a contradiction.

Therefore \((a,b)=(0,0)\) for some \(0<\delta<1\). The equation \(a=0\) says the corresponding directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the lines are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says they are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying the lemma to the support-line path around \(\Lambda_M\), we obtain two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

---

### 1.4. The metric estimate

Let the support lines of \(F(T)\) and \(F(B)\) be perpendicular and meet at \(P\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Rename the leaves if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of its support line starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because the support line of \(B\) is perpendicular to the support line of \(T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\operatorname{line}F(T))=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge \operatorname{length}(F(B)).
\]
Every lifted leaf joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and every leaf is strictly lengthened. Therefore
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\).

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the segment \(F(T)\), and set
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
Let \(D\) and \(H\) be the two boundary arcs of \(\partial M_\beta\) between the endpoints of \(T\), with \(q\in D\). Since \(\partial M_\beta\) has flat length \(2\beta\), write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t
\]
for some \(|t|<\beta\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Parametrize the boundary circle by arclength so that a bottom point \((u,0)\) has coordinate \(u\pmod{2\beta}\), while a top point \((u,1)\) has coordinate \(u+\beta\pmod{2\beta}\). Then the two arc lengths between the endpoints of \(T\) imply
\[
s\equiv t \pmod{2\beta}
\quad\text{or}\quad
s\equiv -t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), it follows that
\[
|s|\ge |t|.
\]
Thus the flat length of \(T\) is at least \(\sqrt{1+t^2}\). Since \(T\) is strictly lengthened,
\[
L>\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

The boundary arc \(H\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\). Boundary arcs are strictly shortened: indeed, subdivide the arc at all boundary vertices of the finite quotient triangulation; on each positive-length subarc, \(F\) is affine with shortening factor \(<1\). Therefore
\[
L\le \ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\]
where \(\ell_F\) denotes Euclidean length of the image path. Combining this with (1),
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

The boundary arc \(D\) maps to a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\). Hence
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
If \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
\ell_F(D)>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Since \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}<\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

From (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\ \sqrt{5+t^2}-t\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), impossible.

Therefore every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

---

## 2. The upper bound

We now prove that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

We use the following standard form of Sadowsky’s construction.

**Sadowsky construction.** Let \(l,b,d>0\) satisfy
\[
b<\frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Then the flat Möbius rectangle
\[
([0,l]\times[-b,b])/(0,t)\sim(l,-t)
\]
has an embedded path-isometric realization in \(\mathbb R^3\). The realized surface consists of three planar pieces and three semicylindrical pieces supported on cylinders of diameters \(d,d,2d\). Each piece is the isometric development of a Euclidean planar piece onto either a plane or a circular cylinder; adjacent pieces meet along full-width generator segments, and the planes are tangent to the adjacent cylinders. Consequently the ruling foliation extends continuously across seams. The construction has a continuous full-width foliation by straight rulings, and strict inequality gives positive separation between nonincident compact pieces.

Choose
\[
\mu\in(\sqrt3,\beta).
\]
Since \(\mu>\sqrt3\), choose \(d>0\) so small that
\[
\frac12<\frac{\mu}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
Applying Sadowsky’s construction with \(l=\mu\) and \(b=\frac12\), and shifting the vertical coordinate to \([0,1]\), gives a lift
\[
E_\mu:\mathbb R\times[0,1]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with deck map
\[
H_\mu(X,Y)=(X+\mu,1-Y)
\]
such that
\[
E_\mu\circ H_\mu=E_\mu
\]
and
\[
E_\mu(P)=E_\mu(Q)
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
P=H_\mu^k(Q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Scaling by \(c>0\) gives a width-\(c\), length-\(L=\mu c\) lift
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]

---

### 2.1. Ruling coordinates and bounded horizontal span

For the scaled Sadowsky lift \(E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3\), every bottom point \((x,0)\) lies on a unique full-width ruling whose top endpoint is \((h(x),c)\). Write
\[
R_x=[(x,0),(h(x),c)].
\]
The function \(h:\mathbb R\to\mathbb R\) is continuous. If \(x_1<x_2\) but \(h(x_1)\ge h(x_2)\), then the two straight segments \(R_{x_1}\) and \(R_{x_2}\) would intersect or share a top endpoint, contradicting the fact that the rulings form a foliation. Hence \(h\) is strictly increasing. Since every top boundary point lies on a unique ruling, \(h\) is onto; therefore \(h\) is an increasing homeomorphism.

The deck transformation sends rulings to rulings. Since
\[
H(x,0)=(x+L,c),\qquad H(h(x),c)=(h(x)+L,0),
\]
we have
\[
H(R_x)=R_{\phi(x)},\qquad \phi(x)=h(x)+L.
\]
The top endpoint of \(R_{\phi(x)}\) is \((x+L,c)\), so
\[
h(\phi(x))=x+L.
\]
Consequently,
\[
\phi^2(x)=x+2L.
\]
Because \(\phi\) is increasing, \(\phi(x)>x\) for all \(x\).

The horizontal spans of the rulings are uniformly bounded. Fix \(x_0\), and set
\[
S=\max_{x\in[x_0,\phi(x_0)]}|h(x)-x|.
\]
Since
\[
h(\phi(x))-\phi(x)=x-h(x),
\]
the same bound holds on every \(\phi\)-translate of \([x_0,\phi(x_0)]\). Those translates cover \(\mathbb R\), so
\[
|h(x)-x|\le S
\]
for all \(x\in\mathbb R\).

---

### 2.2. A PL secant approximation

We need a controlled PL approximation of the Sadowsky lift.

**Lemma.** Let
\[
E:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
be a scaled Sadowsky lift with deck map
\[
H(X,Y)=(X+L,c-Y).
\]
Let \(S\) be the global bound for the horizontal spans of the rulings. For every \(D>S\) and every \(\varepsilon>0\), there exist an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\) and an \(H\)-invariant PL map
\[
F_0:\mathbb R\times[0,c]\to\mathbb R^3
\]
such that:

1. \(F_0\) is quotient-injective:
   \[
   F_0(P)=F_0(Q)
   \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
   P=H^k(Q)
   \]
   for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\);

2. every boundary edge is nonexpanded;

3. every nonboundary edge \(PQ\) satisfies
   \[
   |F_0(P)-F_0(Q)|\ge |P-Q|-\varepsilon;
   \]

4. every nonboundary edge has horizontal span \(<D\).

**Proof.** Choose \(\rho>0\) so small that
\[
S+\rho<D,\qquad 2\rho<\varepsilon.
\]
Choose a finite partition
\[
x_0<x_1<\cdots<x_N=\phi(x_0)
\]
which contains all seam rulings in one fundamental interval and is fine enough that
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i<\rho,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)<\rho
\]
for all \(i\). Extend it by
\[
x_{i+N}=\phi(x_i).
\]
Then
\[
x_{i+2N}=x_i+2L.
\]

Set
\[
B_i=(x_i,0),\qquad T_i=(h(x_i),c).
\]
The consecutive rulings \(B_iT_i\) and \(B_{i+1}T_{i+1}\) bound a clean quadrilateral
\[
Q_i=\operatorname{conv}\{B_i,B_{i+1},T_{i+1},T_i\}.
\]
For \(0\le i<N\), triangulate \(Q_i\) using the diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\). Since
\[
H(B_i)=T_{i+N},\qquad H(T_i)=B_{i+N},
\]
the image under \(H\) uses the opposite diagonal in \(Q_{i+N}\). Extend this triangulation by \(H\)-invariance. The result is an \(H\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\).

Define \(F_0\) on vertices by
\[
F_0(B_i)=E(B_i),\qquad F_0(T_i)=E(T_i),
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle. Then \(F_0\circ H=F_0\).

We choose the partition sufficiently fine so that the quotient PL map is embedded. Work on the compact Sadowsky quotient band. Consider a sequence of such partitions with the maximum of the quantities
\[
x_{i+1}-x_i,\qquad h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)
\]
tending to \(0\), always including the seam rulings. The corresponding secant maps converge uniformly to the Sadowsky embedding. Indeed, let \(p\in Q_i\), and let \(R_x\) be the ruling through \(p\), with bottom endpoint \(B_x=(x,0)\) and top endpoint \(T_x=(h(x),c)\). If \(p\) has height \(Y=vc\), then
\[
p=(1-v)B_x+vT_x.
\]
The Sadowsky map is linear on every ruling, so
\[
E(p)=(1-v)E(B_x)+vE(T_x).
\]
On the triangle of \(\mathcal T_0\) containing \(p\), the barycentric weights of the top vertices sum to \(v\), and the barycentric weights of the bottom vertices sum to \(1-v\). All bottom vertices of that triangle are within flat distance \(<\rho\) of \(B_x\), and all top vertices are within flat distance \(<\rho\) of \(T_x\). Since \(E\) is path-isometric, Euclidean distances between their images are at most those flat distances. Hence \(|F_0(p)-E(p)|\le \rho\). Thus the secant maps converge uniformly as \(\rho\to0\).

For sufficiently fine partitions, local injectivity holds. On planar pieces, the secant map agrees with the original affine isometry. On a cylindrical piece, consecutive generator rulings with angular separation \(<\pi\) determine planar chord strips forming an embedded inscribed polygonal cylinder; adjacent strips meet only along their shared generator. Near a seam, choose coordinates with the seam as an axis and the common tangent plane as \(\{z=0\}\). The planar piece lies in \(\{z=0\}\), while the cylindrical piece and its sufficiently fine chordal approximants lie on one side of that plane, meeting it only along the seam. Therefore no local crossing is introduced near seams.

These local statements give a finite open cover \(U_1,\dots,U_m\) of the compact quotient band such that the quotient secant map is injective on each \(U_j\). Let
\[
K=\{(p,q): p,q \text{ do not both lie in any }U_j\}.
\]
Then \(K\) is compact and avoids the diagonal. Since the original Sadowsky realization is embedded, there is \(\eta>0\) such that
\[
|E(p)-E(q)|\ge \eta
\]
for all \((p,q)\in K\). By uniform convergence, after refining further we have \(|F_0-E|<\eta/3\), so no pair in \(K\) is identified by \(F_0\). Hence the quotient PL map is embedded. Lifting back gives quotient-injectivity.

Now verify the edge estimates. A boundary edge maps to the chord between two points on a boundary arc of the path-isometric Sadowsky surface, so its image length is at most its flat length.

For a ruling edge \(B_iT_i\),
\[
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_i)|=|E(B_i)-E(T_i)|=|B_i-T_i|,
\]
because rulings are mapped isometrically to straight segments.

For a diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\), let
\[
d=|B_i-T_{i+1}|,\qquad r=|B_i-T_i|.
\]
Since \(|T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho\),
\[
|d-r|<\rho.
\]
Also
\[
|E(T_{i+1})-E(T_i)|\le |T_{i+1}-T_i|<\rho.
\]
Thus
\[
\begin{aligned}
|F_0(B_i)-F_0(T_{i+1})|
&=|E(B_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&\ge |E(B_i)-E(T_i)|-|E(T_i)-E(T_{i+1})| \\
&> r-\rho \\
&> d-2\rho \\
&> d-\varepsilon.
\end{aligned}
\]
The other diagonal type is identical, using \(|B_{i+1}-B_i|<\rho\).

Finally, every ruling edge has horizontal span at most \(S\). A diagonal \(B_iT_{i+1}\) has horizontal span
\[
|h(x_{i+1})-x_i|
\le |h(x_i)-x_i|+|h(x_{i+1})-h(x_i)|
<S+\rho<D.
\]
The other diagonal type satisfies the same estimate. \(\square\)

---

### 2.3. Realizing every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Choose
\[
\mu\in(\sqrt3,\beta),
\]
and define
\[
\lambda=\frac{\beta}{\mu}>1.
\]
Start with the width-one Sadowsky band of length \(\mu\). Let \(S_\mu\) be its ruling-span bound, and choose \(D_\mu>S_\mu\).

Choose \(a<1\), sufficiently close to \(1\), with
\[
a>\frac1\lambda.
\]
Set
\[
c=a\lambda>1,\qquad L=\mu c=a\beta.
\]
Scale the width-one Sadowsky band by \(c\). Its width is \(c\), its length is \(L\), and its ruling-span bound is \(cS_\mu\). Apply the PL secant lemma with
\[
D=cD_\mu .
\]

We need a strict length margin. For \(0\le s\le D_\mu\), define
\[
\Delta_a(s)=c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
When \(a=1\), we have \(c=\lambda\), and
\[
\Delta_1(s)=\lambda\sqrt{s^2+1}-\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}>0
\]
because \(\lambda>1\). By compactness of \([0,D_\mu]\), for \(a<1\) sufficiently close to \(1\),
\[
m:=\min_{0\le s\le D_\mu}\Delta_a(s)>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\varepsilon<m,
\]
and apply the PL secant lemma with this \(\varepsilon\).

Define the anisotropic affine map
\[
\mathcal A:\Sigma\to\mathbb R\times[0,c],
\qquad
\mathcal A(x,y)=(ax,cy).
\]
Since \(L=a\beta\),
\[
\mathcal A(G_\beta(x,y))
=
(ax+a\beta,c-cy)
=
(ax+L,c-cy)
=
H(\mathcal A(x,y)).
\]
Thus \(\mathcal A\) conjugates \(G_\beta\) to \(H\).

Let
\[
\mathcal T=\mathcal A^{-1}(\mathcal T_0),
\qquad
f=F_0\circ\mathcal A.
\]
Then \(\mathcal T\) is a countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\), and \(f\) is affine on every triangle.

If \(pq\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T\), then \(\mathcal A(p)\mathcal A(q)\) is a boundary edge of \(\mathcal T_0\) of length \(a|p-q|\). Since \(F_0\) does not expand boundary edges,
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
=
|F_0(\mathcal A p)-F_0(\mathcal A q)|
\le a|p-q|
<|p-q|.
\]
Thus boundary edges are strictly shortened.

Now let \(pq\) be a nonboundary edge of \(\mathcal T\). Let \(t\) be the horizontal span of \(\mathcal A(p)\mathcal A(q)\) in \(\mathbb R\times[0,c]\). By the secant lemma,
\[
0\le t<cD_\mu.
\]
Write \(t=cs\), so \(0\le s<D_\mu\). Since \(\mathcal A(p)\) and \(\mathcal A(q)\) lie on opposite boundary components,
\[
|\mathcal A(p)-\mathcal A(q)|=c\sqrt{s^2+1}.
\]
The original horizontal span is \(t/a\), and the original vertical separation is \(1\). Since \(c/a=\lambda\),
\[
|p-q|=\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}.
\]
The secant lemma gives
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
\ge c\sqrt{s^2+1}-\varepsilon.
\]
Because \(\varepsilon<m\le \Delta_a(s)\),
\[
|f(p)-f(q)|
>
\sqrt{\lambda^2s^2+1}
=
|p-q|.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of \(\mathcal T\).

Finally, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
\begin{aligned}
f(p)=f(q)
&\Longleftrightarrow F_0(\mathcal A p)=F_0(\mathcal A q) \\
&\Longleftrightarrow \mathcal A p=H^k(\mathcal A q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z \\
&\Longleftrightarrow p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\end{aligned}
\]
Hence \(\beta\) is realized.

Combining the lower and upper bounds, \(\beta\) is realized exactly when \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Therefore the set of realized numbers is
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Citation/black-box insertion.**  
Insert after “Sadowsky construction” a citation to Sadowsky’s 1930 construction, e.g. the Hinz–Fried translation in *Journal of Elasticity* 119 (2015), 3–6. State that Sadowsky’s construction gives a developable embedded Möbius strip made from planar pieces and semicylindrical pieces with maximal half-width
\[
b_{\max}= \frac{l}{2\sqrt3}-\frac{3\pi d}{4}.
\]
For \(b<b_{\max}\), take the corresponding smaller-width substrip. Since the quotient surface is a finite union of compact planar/cylindrical patches and nonincident patches are disjoint in the strict-width case, their mutual distances are positive. The planar and cylindrical developments are path-isometries, and their generator rulings give the needed full-width ruling foliation.

2. **Leaf-graph patch.**  
After defining \(\Lambda_M\), add the following argument. In the finite face-to-face quotient triangulation, every nonboundary edge has interior in the interior of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangle sides; hence each vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\). Thus \(\Lambda_M\) is a finite disjoint union of circles. For a component \(C\subset\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\) be the union of the corresponding quotient triangles. Across a nonboundary edge, both adjacent triangles lie in \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the link is a finite interval of triangle corners; consecutive corners except the two boundary extremes share nonboundary edges, so the entire fan lies in the same \(U_C\). Therefore \(U_C\) is open. The same argument applied to the complement shows \(U_C\) is closed. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected, hence \(\Lambda_M\simeq S^1\).

The same proof upstairs uses local finiteness to ensure that each boundary fan is finite; hence \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The deck group acts cellularly on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a leaf, and no nontrivial deck transformation can stabilize an edge interval, since a homeomorphism of a compact interval has a fixed point. Thus the action is free and without inversions, so the quotient map \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) is a graph covering. Each fiber is an infinite deck orbit, so this is an infinite connected cover of \(S^1\). Therefore \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\), and a fundamental interval from \(\ell\) to \(G_\beta\ell\) projects homeomorphically to \(\Lambda_M\).

3. **Distinct endpoints patch for the metric step.**  
Before introducing the boundary arcs \(D,H\), add: choose a lift of \(T\) lying in a lifted triangle \(\Delta\). If the two endpoints of \(T\) represented the same boundary point of \(M_\beta\), then two points of \(\Delta\) would differ by a nontrivial deck transformation, so \(\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta\neq\varnothing\), contradicting the previously proved fact that no closed triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Thus the endpoints of \(T\) are distinct on \(\partial M_\beta\), and the two boundary arcs have positive lengths \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), with \(|t|<\beta\).

4. **PL secant embedding patch.**  
Replace the compressed local/global injectivity paragraph in the PL secant lemma with the following. Work on the compact Sadowsky quotient. Choose partitions containing all seam rulings and with mesh \(\rho\to0\). The secant maps \(F_\rho\) converge uniformly to the Sadowsky embedding \(E\) by the barycentric estimate already given.

For sufficiently small \(\rho\), local injectivity holds uniformly. On each planar piece, \(F_\rho=E\). On a cylindrical piece, choose coordinates in which \(E(\theta,z)=(R\cos\theta,R\sin\theta,z)\) over a semicircular interval. If adjacent generator angles differ by \(<\pi\), the chordal cross-section is a simple polygonal arc, and its product with the generator direction gives embedded planar chord strips meeting only along common generators. Near a seam, choose coordinates so the planar piece lies in the tangent plane and the cylindrical piece lies on one side of it; for small angular mesh, the adjacent chord strips also lie on that same side and meet the tangent plane only along the seam. Since there are finitely many pieces and seams, choose a finite open cover \(U_1,\dots,U_m\) of the quotient, independent of all sufficiently small \(\rho\), such that every \(F_\rho\) is injective on each \(U_j\).

Now set
\[
K=\{(p,q): p,q\text{ are not both contained in any }U_j\}.
\]
Then \(K\) is compact and disjoint from the diagonal. Since \(E\) is an embedding, there is \(\eta>0\) such that \(|E(p)-E(q)|\ge\eta\) on \(K\). Choose \(\rho\) so small that \(\|F_\rho-E\|_\infty<\eta/3\). If \(F_\rho(p)=F_\rho(q)\) and \(p,q\) lie in some \(U_j\), then \(p=q\). If \((p,q)\in K\), then
\[
\eta\le |E(p)-E(q)|
\le |E(p)-F_\rho(p)|+|F_\rho(q)-E(q)|
<2\eta/3,
\]
a contradiction. Hence the quotient PL map is embedded, and lifting gives the claimed quotient-injectivity.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
