solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

For every real number \(\beta>0\), let \(\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1]\), let \(\partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\}\), and let \(G_\beta:\Sigma\to\Sigma\) be \(G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y)\). For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), let \(G_\beta^k\) denote the \(k\)-fold iterate, with negative \(k\) meaning iterates of \(G_\beta^{-1}\). A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). A clean triangulation is a countable locally finite covering of \(\Sigma\) by clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors. It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) preserves the collection of triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there is a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) which is a squeeze map on every triangle of some \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and satisfies \(f(p)=f(q)\) exactly when \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).  

The proof below establishes the stronger theorem:
\[
\beta \text{ is realized } \Longleftrightarrow \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), so its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This strengthens the requested conclusion and is not a relaxation.

## Proof

We prove the two implications separately.

---

# I. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Assume \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Put
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},
\qquad
L_0=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Then
\[
W>L_0
\]
because \(W^2>L_0^2\) is equivalent to \(4\beta^2/9>1+\beta^2/9\), i.e. \(\beta^2>3\).

Choose
\[
L_0<\sigma<W,
\]
and set
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_0,\;W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

## 1. A \(9\)-periodic clean triangulation of the strip

Define a positive sequence \(\ell_j\), \(j\in\mathbb Z\), by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Thus every block of three consecutive lengths has sum
\[
\lambda+\lambda+(W-2\lambda)=W.
\]
Let \(c_0=0\), and define \(c_j\) by
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+3}=c_j+W,
\qquad
c_{j+9}=c_j+3W=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),
\qquad
Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)
=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Therefore, for each fixed \(s\), the intervals
\[
[x_j(s),x_{j+1}(s)]
\]
tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{s\}\). Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\) as \(j\to\pm\infty\), the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\). This gives two clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),
\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]

The action of \(G_\beta\) is compatible with this tiling. Indeed,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Hence
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},
\qquad
G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
Thus the triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

Let \(v_j\) denote the quotient vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since
\[
G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9},
\]
the index \(j\) is taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\), because \(Y_j=G_\beta(X_{j-4})\). Therefore \(D_j\) descends to the quotient triangle
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\]
with all indices modulo \(9\). The triangle \(E_j\) descends to \(T_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has exactly the nine faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),
\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

## 2. The target \(9\)-triangle Möbius band

Let \(a_0,a_1,a_2\in\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\subset\mathbb R^3\) be the vertices of an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\), centered at the origin. Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), write
\[
q_i(r,z)=r a_i+z e_3.
\]

Choose small numbers \(\eta>0\) and \(h>0\). Define three horizontal homothetic copies:

\[
H_0:\quad q_i(1,0),
\]
\[
H_1:\quad q_i(1-\eta,h),
\]
\[
H_2:\quad q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]

Assign target vertices \(P_j\in\mathbb R^3\) as follows:
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0(1+\eta,2h)&
q_0(1-\eta,h)&
q_0(1,0)&
q_1(1,0)&
q_1(1+\eta,2h)&
q_1(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1,0)&
q_2(1+\eta,2h).
\end{array}
\]
Equivalently,
\[
H_0=(v_2,v_3,v_7),\qquad
H_1=(v_1,v_5,v_6),\qquad
H_2=(v_0,v_4,v_8).
\]

As \(\eta,h\to0\), vertices in the same cluster near a fixed \(a_i\) approach one another, while vertices in different clusters have distances approaching \(\sigma\). Hence we may choose \(\eta,h>0\) so small that:

1. every same-cluster target edge has length \(<\lambda/2\);
2. every different-cluster target edge has length in
   \[
   (\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4).
   \]

Define a PL map \(F\) on the quotient by
\[
F(v_j)=P_j,
\]
and extend affinely over every face
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}).
\]

The nine target faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
F(T_0)&=[P_0,P_1,P_5],&
F(T_1)&=[P_1,P_2,P_6],&
F(T_2)&=[P_2,P_3,P_7],\\
F(T_3)&=[P_3,P_4,P_8],&
F(T_4)&=[P_4,P_5,P_0],&
F(T_5)&=[P_5,P_6,P_1],\\
F(T_6)&=[P_6,P_7,P_2],&
F(T_7)&=[P_7,P_8,P_3],&
F(T_8)&=[P_8,P_0,P_4].
\end{aligned}
\]
The faces \(T_2,T_5,T_8\) are the three horizontal triangles \(H_0,H_1,H_2\). The remaining six faces form three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
R_{01}=F(T_0)\cup F(T_4),
\]
joining the side between \(a_0\) and \(a_1\);
\[
R_{12}=F(T_3)\cup F(T_7),
\]
joining the side between \(a_1\) and \(a_2\);
\[
R_{20}=F(T_1)\cup F(T_6),
\]
joining the side between \(a_2\) and \(a_0\).

## 3. Embeddedness of the target surface

We prove that these nine triangles meet only along their prescribed common edges and vertices.

For \(r>0\), let
\[
\Delta(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{r a_0,r a_1,r a_2\}
\subset \mathbb R^2.
\]
Let \(S_{ij}(r)\) be the side segment of \(\Delta(r)\) joining \(r a_i\) to \(r a_j\).

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to \(a_0,a_1,a_2\). Since the centroid is \(0\), one has
\[
\Delta(r)=\left\{\lambda_i\ge\frac{1-r}{3}\text{ for }i=0,1,2\right\}.
\]
The side \(S_{ij}(r)\), opposite the remaining index \(k\), is given by
\[
S_{ij}(r)=
\left\{
\lambda_k=\frac{1-r}{3},\;
\lambda_i,\lambda_j\ge \frac{1-r}{3}
\right\}.
\]

It follows immediately that two adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and if \(r=s\), they meet only at their common endpoint. Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r)\cap \Delta(s)=\varnothing.
\]

Now examine horizontal slices. The panel \(R_{20}\) occurs for \(0\le z\le h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh\right).
\]
The panel \(R_{12}\) occurs for \(0\le z\le2h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right).
\]
The panel \(R_{01}\) occurs for \(h\le z\le2h\), and its slice at height \(z\) is
\[
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right).
\]

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{20}\) and \(R_{12}\) appear. Their scales are respectively
\[
1-\eta z/h<1
\]
and
\[
1+\eta z/(2h)>1,
\]
so the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) appear. Their scales satisfy
\[
1+\eta\frac z{2h}
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0,
\]
so again the adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the bottom horizontal triangle \(H_0=\Delta(1)\) meets the panels exactly along the sides \(S_{20}(1)\) and \(S_{12}(1)\). At \(z=h\), the middle triangle \(H_1=\Delta(1-\eta)\) meets the panels \(R_{20}\) and \(R_{01}\) exactly along two of its sides, while the slice of \(R_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2>1-\eta\) and is disjoint from \(\Delta(1-\eta)\). At \(z=2h\), the top triangle \(H_2=\Delta(1+\eta)\) meets the panels exactly along the sides \(S_{01}(1+\eta)\) and \(S_{12}(1+\eta)\).

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles in a panel meet exactly along that diagonal. The slice analysis shows that different panels and horizontal triangles have no additional intersections. Therefore \(F\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

## 4. Squeeze inequalities

We now compare flat edge lengths in the domain with target edge lengths.

### Boundary edges

The quotient boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\).

If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) are in the same cluster. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda=\ell_j.
\]

If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) are in different clusters. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}.
\]
Because \(\lambda<\Delta/20\),
\[
W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

### Nonboundary edges

Consider the nonboundary edge represented by \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|^2=1+(d_j-\beta)^2.
\]
A direct computation from the length pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\), we get
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\beta/3
\]
using \(\lambda<\beta/12\). Hence
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_0.
\]

The other type of nonboundary edge is \(X_{j+1}Y_j\). Its horizontal displacement is
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}
=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it satisfies the same estimate. Therefore every flat nonboundary edge has length \(<L_0\).

On the target side, every nonboundary edge joins vertices from different clusters, and hence has length
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Since \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_0\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4\ge L_0+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L_0.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Therefore \(F\), and hence its lift \(f\), is a squeeze map on each clean triangle.

## 5. The lifted map and the orbit condition

Define \(P_{j+9}=P_j\). On the strip, set
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,
\qquad
f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over each \(D_j\) and \(E_j\). The vertex assignments agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous.

Also,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
By affineness on corresponding triangles,
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]
Thus \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(F\). Therefore
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p,q\) project to the same point in the quotient, which is equivalent to
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\).

Hence every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

# II. The lower bound: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume now that \(\beta>0\) is realized. Let \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) be a realizing map for a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

## 1. Cevian lengthening and face-to-face refinement

A clean triangle has one edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\); call it the boundary edge. The opposite vertex is its apex.

### Lemma 1: Cevian lengthening

Let
\[
\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}
\]
be a clean triangle with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\), and let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

Proof. Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so
\[
q(0)>0,\qquad q(1)>0.
\]
Because \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\Bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\Bigr)<0,
\]
since the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Hence \(q\) is strictly concave. In particular,
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
Thus \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened.

On the boundary edge, \(A\) is affine with contraction factor
\[
\frac{|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|}{|r_1-r_0|}<1,
\]
so every nontrivial boundary subsegment is strictly shortened. \(\square\)

Now refine the triangulation as follows. In each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every vertex of the original triangulation lying on that boundary edge, and cone those subdivision points to the apex. Local finiteness guarantees that only finitely many such subdivision points occur on each compact boundary edge. Performing this construction equivariantly gives a new countable locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

By Lemma 1, \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every new triangle.

After this refinement, the triangulation is face-to-face. Boundary vertex-in-edge incidences have been removed by construction. Nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely: if two such edges crossed, the interiors of the adjacent triangles would occupy two open half-planes with nonempty overlap near the crossing point. Finally, two collinear nonboundary edges must coincide, because a line crossing the strip from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) has a unique endpoint on each boundary component. Thus cell intersections are common faces.

From now on, use this refined face-to-face triangulation.

## 2. The embedded PL Möbius band quotient

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence \([p]=[q]\). Therefore \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is an embedding.

The quotient cellulation is finite. Indeed, a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles by local finiteness, and every triangle has some translate meeting that rectangle.

We also need that the quotient has no self-identified triangle. Every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\). For even powers,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and for odd powers,
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus if \(k\ne0\), then
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta.
\]

If \(f\) were not injective on some refined triangle, then because \(f\) is affine there would be distinct points \(p,q\) in that triangle with \(|p-q|<\beta\) and \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\ne0\), contradicting the distance bound. Hence \(f\) is injective on every refined triangle.

Consequently a closed refined triangle cannot meet a nontrivial translate of itself. Thus the quotient is a finite regular PL cellulation of \(M_\beta\).

Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): subdivide the arc at boundary vertices and at its endpoints; on each nontrivial subarc inside a boundary edge, the affine restriction has contraction factor \(<1\).

## 3. The leaf graph

In each clean triangle, consider all cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. These cevians are naturally parametrized by an interval. Glue endpoints of these intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge of the quotient triangulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\), called the leaf graph.

Each vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) corresponds to a nonboundary edge. Since the triangulation is face-to-face and \(M_\beta\) is a surface, every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\).

We claim \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a component of \(\Lambda_M\), and let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals belong to \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges except at the two boundary extremes. Hence all triangles incident to a boundary vertex also belong to the same \(U_C\).

It follows that \(U_C\) is open in \(M_\beta\). Since the quotient cellulation is finite, \(U_C\) is also closed. The Möbius band is connected, so there is only one component. Thus
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) in the strip. The same open-closed argument applies in \(\Sigma\); local finiteness ensures that a union of closed triangles from a subcollection is closed. Hence \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
has infinite degree. Indeed, no nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. If a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even power of \(G_\beta\) translates both endpoints by a nonzero multiple of \(2\beta\), while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\qquad
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, every leaf is strictly lengthened, so its image is a nondegenerate segment and has a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). Along one fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), the endpoint leaf is obtained from the initial leaf by applying \(G_\beta\). Since \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the oriented image line is reversed. Thus we get a continuous path of oriented affine lines whose endpoint is the same underlying line with opposite orientation.

## 4. An oriented-line lemma

### Lemma 2

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist two distinct parameters \(s\ne t\) such that the underlying unoriented lines of \(L_s\) and \(L_t\) are perpendicular and intersect.

Proof. Represent an oriented affine line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Choose continuous coordinates \((u(s),m(s))\) for \(0\le s\le1\), and extend them to all real \(s\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),
\qquad
(a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map from the cylinder \(S^1\times[0,1]\) to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. But on \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A map \(S^1\to S^1\) with this antipodal symmetry has odd degree, whereas it is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), which has degree \(0\). This is impossible. Hence \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere, with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two direction vectors are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)
=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel; the equation \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 2 to the oriented support-line path from the leaf graph gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose image support lines are perpendicular and intersect. Let those support lines be \(\ell_T\) and \(\ell_B\), and let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap \ell_B.
\]

## 5. The metric estimate

The relative interiors of distinct leaves are disjoint in \(M_\beta\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel the two leaves if necessary so that the image segment \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Since \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The flat length of any lifted leaf is at least \(1\), because it joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, \(B\) is strictly lengthened, so
\[
|F(B)|>1.
\]
Thus
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\), because endpoints of \(T\) map into \(\ell_T\).

Let \(A\) and \(C\) be the endpoints of the segment \(F(T)\), and put
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
Let the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\) have flat lengths
\[
\beta-t
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\beta+t,
\]
with \(q\) lying on the latter arc. Call the arc not containing \(q\) by \(H\), and the arc containing \(q\) by \(D\). Thus
\[
\ell_0(H)=\beta-t,
\qquad
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),
\qquad
(x+h,1).
\]
Parametrize the quotient boundary circle of length \(2\beta\) by the coordinate
\[
\rho(u,0)=u\pmod{2\beta},
\qquad
\rho(u,1)=u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
This is well-defined under \(G_\beta\). The coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is therefore
\[
h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths are \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\), this implies
\[
h\equiv \pm t \pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), the representative \(h\) satisfies
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
The flat length of \(T\) is \(\sqrt{1+h^2}\), and \(T\) is strictly lengthened, so
\[
L>\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. The arc \(H\) connects the endpoints of \(T\), so its image path connects \(A\) to \(C\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
But \(H\) is strictly shortened:
\[
\ell_F(H)<\ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]
Hence, using (1),
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, the arc \(D\) connects \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the elementary broken-path estimate gives
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Indeed, after projecting \(Q\) to the line \(AC\), the sum is minimized when the projection is the midpoint of \(AC\).

Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\), we get
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Since \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\ell_F(D)<\ell_0(D)=\beta+t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]

Finally, for every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
To see this, suppose both terms were \(<\sqrt3\). Then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
implies, after squaring,
\[
t<\frac1{\sqrt3}.
\]
On the other hand,
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
implies, after squaring,
\[
t>\frac1{\sqrt3}.
\]
This is impossible. Equality occurs only at \(t=1/\sqrt3\).

Thus every realized \(\beta\) satisfies
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]

Together with the explicit construction for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have proved
\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Therefore the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is
\[
\sqrt3.
\]
\(\square\)

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Face-to-face refinement patch.** After subdividing each boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on it, add the following argument. Distinct boundary edges cannot have overlapping relative interiors: at an interior point of such an overlap, both adjacent clean triangles contain the same sufficiently small half-disk inside \(\Sigma\), contradicting disjointness of triangle interiors. A nonboundary edge can meet a boundary edge only at its endpoint; if that endpoint lies in the interior of a boundary edge, it was inserted as a subdivision vertex. If two nonboundary edges meet in the interior of \(\Sigma\), then either they cross transversely or are collinear. A transverse crossing is impossible because near the crossing each incident triangle occupies an open half-neighborhood on one side of its edge, and two such half-neighborhoods for the two crossing lines overlap in an open sector, contradicting disjoint interiors. If the two nonboundary edges are collinear, then the supporting line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points, so both edges have the same endpoints and coincide. Hence refined closed triangles meet only in common faces.

2. **Regular quotient patch.** First note explicitly that the orbit condition gives \(f\circ G_\beta=f\). If \(f\) failed to be injective on a refined triangle, affine degeneracy would give arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\) in that triangle with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\), \(k\ne0\), but every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by at least \(\beta\), contradiction. Thus \(f\) is injective on each refined triangle. Therefore no closed refined triangle can meet a nontrivial translate of itself; otherwise two distinct points of that triangle would be in the same \(G_\beta\)-orbit. This also rules out self-identification of edges and vertices. Since a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, the quotient is a finite regular PL triangulation of \(M_\beta\).

3. **Leaf graph patch.** In the regular quotient triangulation, every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every vertex of the leaf graph has valence \(2\). For any component \(C\) of the leaf graph, the union \(U_C\) of the corresponding closed triangles is open: interiors are immediate, nonboundary-edge points have both adjacent triangles in \(U_C\), and at a boundary vertex the incident triangles form a fan whose consecutive triangles share nonboundary edges. It is also closed because the quotient triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, the leaf graph is connected and hence a circle. The same argument in \(\Sigma\), using local finiteness for closedness, shows the lifted leaf graph is connected. No nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) fixes a lifted leaf: even powers translate both endpoints by \(2n\beta\), while odd powers would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
impossible. Thus the lifted leaf graph is the infinite cover of the quotient circle, hence homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\). If a leaf is oriented from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), then \(G_\beta\) swaps its endpoints, so \(f\circ G_\beta=f\) sends the oriented image segment to the same support line with opposite orientation.

4. **Degree step in Lemma 2.** Let \(\gamma(s)=\phi(s,1/2)\). The symmetry gives \(\gamma(s+1/2)=-\gamma(s)\). Choose a continuous angle lift \(\theta\) on \([0,1]\), so \(\gamma(s)=e^{i\theta(s)}\). Then
\[
\theta(s+1/2)-\theta(s)=(2m+1)\pi
\]
for a locally constant, hence constant, integer \(m\). Therefore
\[
\theta(1)-\theta(0)=2(2m+1)\pi,
\]
so \(\deg\gamma=2m+1\), odd. But \(\gamma\) is homotopic through \(\phi(s,\delta)\), \(0\le\delta\le1/2\), to the constant loop \(\phi(s,0)=(1,0)\), so its degree must be \(0\), contradiction. This proves the required zero of \((a,b)\).

5. **Boundary-coordinate patch.** Since no triangle or leaf has its endpoints identified by a nontrivial deck transformation, the endpoints of \(T\) are distinct boundary points of \(M_\beta\). Hence the two boundary arcs between them have positive lengths summing to \(2\beta\), so they can be written as \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\) with \(|t|<\beta\). For a lift of \(T\) with endpoints \((x,0)\) and \((x+h,1)\), the boundary coordinate
\[
\rho(u,0)=u\pmod{2\beta},\qquad \rho(u,1)=u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}
\]
gives coordinate difference \(h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}\). Since the two unoriented arc lengths are \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\), this difference is congruent to either \(\beta+t\) or \(\beta-t\), modulo \(2\beta\). Hence \(h\equiv t\) or \(h\equiv -t\pmod{2\beta}\). Because \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of the congruence class \(\pm t\pmod{2\beta}\) has absolute value at least \(|t|\), so the actual horizontal displacement satisfies \(|h|\ge |t|\).
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
claim_verified:
After inserting the routine patches above, the proof establishes the stronger classification
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
Consequently the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\). As written, the proof leaves several incidence, leaf-graph, and embeddedness verifications implicit, but these are repairable without changing the strategy.
solution_verified:
**Claim.** With the definitions in the problem, a number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if
\[
\beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

## 1. Necessity: every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume that \(\beta\) is realized by a map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\). The orbit condition immediately gives
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]

### 1.1. Refinement and cevian lengthening

Let \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, with boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\), and let \(A:\tau\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For \(r(s)=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), define
\[
Q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
Since the two nonboundary edges are lengthened, \(Q(0)>0\) and \(Q(1)>0\). Since the boundary edge is shortened,
\[
Q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0.
\]
Thus \(Q\) is strictly concave, so
\[
Q(s)\ge (1-s)Q(0)+sQ(1)>0.
\]
Hence every cevian from \(a\) to a point of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly lengthened. Also, because \(A\) is affine on \([r_0,r_1]\) and shortens that edge, every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

Now refine \(\mathcal T_0\) as follows. For each original triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original vertex lying on that boundary edge, and cone those subintervals to the opposite apex. A compact boundary edge meets only finitely many original triangles by local finiteness, hence contains only finitely many original vertices. Thus each original triangle is subdivided into finitely many clean triangles. The refinement is still countable, locally finite, and \(G_\beta\)-invariant. By the cevian argument above, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

Call the refined triangulation \(\mathcal T\). It is face-to-face upstairs. Indeed, boundary-edge overlaps have been subdivided at all boundary vertices. Two nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely in their relative interiors, because then the interiors of the two triangles adjacent to those edges would overlap locally. Finally, two collinear nonboundary edges with a common relative interior point must coincide, since a line meets each boundary component \(\mathbb R\times\{0\}\) and \(\mathbb R\times\{1\}\) in at most one point.

### 1.2. The quotient Möbius band

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
It is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of flat length \(2\beta\).

Since \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the orbit condition \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence \([p]=[q]\). Therefore \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

For later use, note that every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point of \(\Sigma\) by distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed,
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus, for \(k\neq 0\),
\[
|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge \beta.
\]

We claim that \(f\) is injective on every closed refined triangle \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\). If not, the affine linear part of \(f|_\Delta\) would have rank \(<2\), so there would be arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) for some \(k\neq0\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\).

It follows that no closed refined triangle meets a nontrivial translate of itself. If
\[
z\in \Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta,\qquad k\neq0,
\]
then \(z=G_\beta^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\). Since \(f|_\Delta\) is injective, \(z=w\), contradicting the fact that \(G_\beta^k\) has no fixed points.

Therefore the quotient projection \(\pi:\Sigma\to M_\beta\) is injective on every closed refined triangle. Since a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, there are finitely many \(G_\beta\)-orbits of refined triangles. Hence the quotient has a finite PL \(\Delta\)-complex structure inherited from \(\mathcal T\). Incidences are inherited from the upstairs face-to-face triangulation. In particular, every nonboundary edge has exactly two incident triangle sides, and every boundary edge has exactly one incident triangle side.

Because every boundary edge is strictly shortened by \(F\), every nontrivial boundary arc in \(\partial M_\beta\) is strictly shortened: subdivide the arc at the finitely many boundary vertices and sum the strict contractions on the resulting subarcs.

### 1.3. The leaf graph

For each triangle \(\tau\in\mathcal T\), consider the closed interval \(I_\tau\) parametrizing the cevians from its apex to points of its boundary edge. The endpoints of \(I_\tau\) correspond to the two nonboundary edges of \(\tau\). Whenever two triangles share a nonboundary edge, identify the corresponding endpoints. The resulting graph is the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\).

Every vertex of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) has valence \(2\), because every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles.

We show that \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. Let \(C\) be a connected component of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), and let \(U_C\subset\Sigma\) be the union of all triangles \(\tau\) with \(I_\tau\subset C\). If a point lies in the interior of a selected triangle, it has a neighborhood in \(U_C\). If it lies in the relative interior of a selected nonboundary edge, then both adjacent triangles are selected, because their cevian intervals meet at the corresponding vertex of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). If it lies in the relative interior of a selected boundary edge, it has a one-sided neighborhood in the selected triangle. Finally, at a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan, and consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges; hence if one incident triangle is selected, then all incident triangles are selected. Thus \(U_C\) is open. The same argument applies to the complement, so \(U_C\) is also closed. Since \(\Sigma\) is connected, \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected.

The deck action preserves \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a leaf. Indeed, a leaf has endpoints \(b=(x_0,0)\) and \(t=(x_1,1)\). An even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) preserves boundary components and translates both endpoints horizontally by \(2n\beta\), so it fixes the leaf only if \(n=0\). An odd power \(G_\beta^{2n+1}\) swaps boundary components; if it fixed the leaf, then
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible since \(\beta>0\).

Therefore the quotient
\[
\Lambda_M=\Lambda_\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle
\]
is a finite connected \(2\)-valent graph, hence a circle. The covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\) has infinite degree, so
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Choose an orientation of \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\). Since \(G_\beta\) acts freely on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\), it is a fixed-point-free homeomorphism of \(\mathbb R\). Such a homeomorphism is orientation-preserving. After reversing the coordinate if necessary, we may assume \(G_\beta x>x\) for all \(x\). Thus the interval \([x,G_\beta x]\) is a fundamental interval for the action.

Orient every lifted leaf from its endpoint on \(y=0\) to its endpoint on \(y=1\). Because every leaf is lengthened by \(f\), its image is a nondegenerate segment with a well-defined oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). These oriented lines vary continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). If \(\ell\) is a leaf, then \(G_\beta\ell\) has the same image segment but with the opposite orientation, because \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\).

We need the following elementary fact.

**Lemma.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\), and suppose \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist two distinct parameters modulo \(1\) whose underlying unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented affine line by a unit direction vector \(u\in S^2\) and its moment
\[
m=p\times u,
\]
where \(p\) is any point on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Extend \((u(s),m(s))\) to all \(s\in\mathbb R\) by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
and
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
one has
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\neq(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), \(\phi\) is constant. On \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2),
\]
so \(s\mapsto \phi(s,1/2)\) has odd degree. But it is homotopic through the cylinder \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), contradiction. Therefore \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere, necessarily with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two direction vectors are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by points \(p,q\) and directions \(u,v\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Apply the lemma to the oriented support lines along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). We obtain two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) in \(M_\beta\) whose support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect. Let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap \ell_B.
\]

Because \(F\) is embedded and distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors, the image segments \(F(T)\) and \(F(B)\) cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\). Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and put \(Q=F(q)\). Since \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in one ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) lifts to a segment joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), hence has flat length at least \(1\). Since leaves are strictly lengthened,
\[
|F(B)|>|B|_0\ge1.
\]
Therefore
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\).

Let the endpoints of \(T\) be \(a,c\in\partial M_\beta\), and set
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
Let \(D\) be the boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Since \(\partial M_\beta\) has flat length \(2\beta\), we may write
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t
\]
for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\).

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+s,1).
\]
Parametrize the boundary circle by
\[
(u,0)\mapsto u\pmod{2\beta},\qquad (u,1)\mapsto u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Thus the coordinate difference between the two endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
s-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
The two boundary arcs between them have lengths \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), so
\[
s-\beta\equiv \beta\pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Hence
\[
s\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of this congruence class has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Therefore
\[
L=|F(T)|>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+s^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}.
\]

Using boundary shortening on \(H\),
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Thus
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{1}
\]

Now use the arc \(D\), which contains \(q\). Since \(F(D)\) is a path from \(A\) to \(C\) passing through \(Q\),
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Let \(y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1\). In the plane spanned by \(Q\) and the line \(AC\), place
\[
A=(-L/2,0),\qquad C=(L/2,0),\qquad Q=(u,y).
\]
Then
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|
=
\sqrt{(u+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(u-L/2)^2+y^2}.
\]
This expression is minimized at \(u=0\), so
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge 2\sqrt{(L/2)^2+y^2}
=\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Since \(y>1\) and \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|,
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{2}
\]

Combining (1) and (2),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge \sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Thus every realized \(\beta\) is strictly larger than \(\sqrt3\).

## 2. Sufficiency: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad R=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\),
\[
W^2-R^2=\frac{4\beta^2}{9}-\left(1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}\right)
=\frac{\beta^2}{3}-1>0,
\]
so \(W>R\). Choose
\[
R<\sigma<W,
\]
and define
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-R,\; W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

### 2.1. A \(G_\beta\)-invariant triangulation

Define a \(9\)-periodic sequence of positive increments by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\) and \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\). Every three consecutive increments sum to \(W\), hence every nine consecutive increments sum to \(3W=2\beta\). Thus
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let \(Q_j\) be the quadrilateral with vertices
\[
X_j,\ X_{j+1},\ Y_{j+1},\ Y_j.
\]
For fixed height \(s\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(s)=(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
and
\[
x_{j+1}(s)-x_j(s)=(1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Moreover,
\[
x_{j+9}(s)=x_j(s)+2\beta.
\]
Hence, for each \(s\), the intervals \([x_j(s),x_{j+1}(s)]\) tile the horizontal line \(\mathbb R\times\{s\}\). Therefore the \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\). Since the widths are bounded below by a positive constant, the tiling is locally finite.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), producing clean triangles
\[
D_j=(X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j),\qquad
E_j=(Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}).
\]
The action of \(G_\beta\) satisfies
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=Y_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(Y_j)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So this is a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

In the quotient \(M_\beta\), let \(v_j\) be the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(X_{j+9}=G_\beta^2(X_j)\), indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). The quotient has nine triangular faces
\[
\Theta_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

### 2.2. Target vertices

Let \(p_0,p_1,p_2\) be an equilateral triangle of side length \(\sigma\) in a horizontal plane of \(\mathbb R^3\), let \(o\) be its centroid, and let \(e_3\) be a unit vector perpendicular to that plane. For \(r>0\) and height \(z\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Choose \(0<\eta<1\) and \(h>0\) sufficiently small so that:

1. every distance between two points in the same cluster
\[
\{q_i(1,0),q_i(1-\eta,h),q_i(1+\eta,2h)\}
\]
is \(<\lambda/2\);

2. every distance between points in distinct clusters is in
\[
(\sigma-\Delta/4,\sigma+\Delta/4).
\]

This is possible by continuity, since as \(\eta,h\to0\), the \(i\)-th cluster collapses to \(p_i\), and the distances between distinct clusters tend to \(\sigma\).

Set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define nine target vertices by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
C_0&M_0&A_0&A_1&C_1&M_1&M_2&A_2&C_2.
\end{array}
\]
Extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map \(\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j
\]
and extending affinely over each face \(\Theta_j\).

The nine image triangles are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\Phi(\Theta_0)&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\Phi(\Theta_1)&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],\\
\Phi(\Theta_2)&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],&
\Phi(\Theta_3)&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],\\
\Phi(\Theta_4)&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\Phi(\Theta_5)&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\Phi(\Theta_6)&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\Phi(\Theta_7)&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],\\
\Phi(\Theta_8)&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

### 2.3. Embeddedness of the quotient map

The three horizontal triangles are
\[
[A_0,A_1,A_2],\qquad [M_1,M_2,M_0],\qquad [C_2,C_0,C_1].
\]
The remaining six triangles form three side panels:
\[
R_{01}=[C_0,M_0,M_1]\cup[C_1,M_1,C_0],
\]
\[
R_{12}=[A_1,C_1,C_2]\cup[A_2,C_2,A_1],
\]
and
\[
R_{20}=[M_0,A_0,M_2]\cup[M_2,A_2,A_0].
\]

Use barycentric coordinates \(\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2\) with respect to the triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\). For a horizontal plane of height \(z\), define
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}.
\]
Writing
\[
\alpha(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]
one has
\[
\mathcal H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge \alpha(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\}.
\]
Let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) denote the side of \(\mathcal H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\). If \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=\alpha(r),\ \lambda_i,\lambda_j\ge\alpha(r)\}.
\]

Two elementary consequences will be used. First, adjacent sides with different scales are disjoint: if \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) met, then one would need both \(\alpha(r)\ge\alpha(s)\) and \(\alpha(s)\ge\alpha(r)\), hence \(r=s\). Second, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap \mathcal H(s,z)=\varnothing,
\]
because \(\alpha(r)<\alpha(s)\).

The side-panel slices are:
\[
R_{01}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h},z\right),
\qquad h\le z\le2h,
\]
\[
R_{12}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h},z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
and
\[
R_{20}\cap\{z\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac zh,z\right),
\qquad 0\le z\le h.
\]

For \(0<z<h\), only \(R_{12}\) and \(R_{20}\) occur, with scales \(>1\) and \(<1\), respectively. Their slices are adjacent sides with different scales, so they are disjoint.

For \(h<z<2h\), only \(R_{01}\) and \(R_{12}\) occur. Their scales differ by
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac z{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{z-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac zh\right)>0.
\]
Thus their slices are adjacent sides with different scales and are disjoint.

At \(z=0\), the lower horizontal triangle meets \(R_{12}\) along \([A_1,A_2]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([A_2,A_0]\). At \(z=h\), the middle horizontal triangle meets \(R_{01}\) along \([M_0,M_1]\) and meets \(R_{20}\) along \([M_2,M_0]\), while the slice of \(R_{12}\) has larger scale and is disjoint from that middle triangle. At \(z=2h\), the top horizontal triangle meets \(R_{01}\) along \([C_0,C_1]\) and meets \(R_{12}\) along \([C_1,C_2]\).

Each side panel is a convex trapezoid split by one diagonal, so its two triangular faces meet exactly along that diagonal. Since different heights are disjoint and the horizontal-slice analysis accounts for all possible intersections, the nine target triangles meet only along the edges and vertices prescribed by the quotient triangulation. Hence \(\Phi\) is injective. Therefore \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

### 2.4. Squeeze inequalities

We now verify that every face map is a squeeze map.

First consider boundary edges. The boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has flat length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in distinct clusters. Hence
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Because \(\Delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j,
\]
using \(\lambda<\Delta/20\). Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider nonboundary edges. Every nonboundary quotient edge is of the form
\[
e_j=v_jv_{j+5}.
\]
It is lifted by the segment \(X_jY_j\). Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
Then
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}.
\]
A direct computation from the increments gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Therefore
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=R.
\]

The endpoints \(P_j,P_{j+5}\) lie in distinct clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+5}|>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Because \(\Delta\le\sigma-R\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4>R.
\]
Hence every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Thus the affine map on every quotient face is a squeeze map. Equivalently, the lifted affine map on every triangle \(D_j\) and \(E_j\) is a squeeze map.

### 2.5. The realizing map on \(\Sigma\)

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
and extend affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous. By the preceding subsection, \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation.

On vertices,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) sends each triangle affinely to another triangle and both maps are affine on triangles, it follows that
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3\).

Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\iff
\Phi(\pi(p))=\Phi(\pi(q))
\iff
\pi(p)=\pi(q)
\iff
p=G_\beta^k(q)\text{ for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Thus \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that \(\beta\) is realized exactly when \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Therefore the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

<MINOR_GAPS>
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The refinement/face-to-face/incidence assertions are plausible and standard for locally finite planar triangulations, but the proof compresses the verification that nonboundary edges have exactly two incident sides and boundary edges exactly one.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The leaf-graph argument needs a fully explicit justification that the quotient map is a graph covering, that the lifted graph is an infinite cover of a circle, and that distinct quotient leaves have disjoint relative interiors.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — In the oriented-line lemma, the assertion that a map \(g:S^1\to S^1\) satisfying \(g(s+1/2)=-g(s)\) has odd degree is not proved.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The embeddedness verification for the constructed PL Möbius band needs explicit checks that all target triangles are nondegenerate, that side panels are planar convex quadrilaterals split by diagonals, and that the horizontal-slice analysis gives global injectivity.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED — The squeeze verification should explicitly tabulate the cluster incidences \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) and \(P_j,P_{j+5}\), since the proof uses “same cluster” and “distinct clusters” for all quotient edges.
</MINOR_GAPS>

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Incidence patch after refinement.**  
   After subdividing boundary edges, prove explicitly: if two refined triangles meet in more than one point, their intersection is a common full edge. A transverse crossing of two nonboundary edges is impossible because each triangle occupies an open half-neighborhood on one side of its edge, and two nonparallel half-planes always overlap locally. Collinear nonboundary edges with a common relative interior point coincide, since their supporting line meets each boundary component \(y=0,1\) in a unique point. Boundary/nonboundary intersections are only endpoints after the boundary subdivision. Hence the refined triangulation is face-to-face.

   Then, for a relative interior point of a nonboundary edge, a small disk in \(\Sigma\) is covered by incident triangles. At most one triangle can lie on each side of the edge, otherwise interiors overlap; at least one lies on each side, otherwise the union misses a sector. Thus every nonboundary edge has exactly two incident triangle sides. Similarly, at a relative interior point of a boundary edge, a small half-disk shows exactly one incident triangle side.

2. **Leaf-graph covering patch.**  
   At a boundary vertex, local finiteness gives a finite fan of incident triangles. Consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, except for the two exterior boundary edges, so membership in one component of the leaf graph propagates through the whole fan. This completes the open-and-closed proof that the lifted leaf graph is connected.

   If a nontrivial deck transformation fixed a point of the leaf graph, it would fix the corresponding leaf. The endpoint computation in the proof rules this out. If a deck transformation preserved an interval of the graph setwise, its action on that compact interval would have a fixed point, again impossible. Therefore the deck action on the graph is free and properly discontinuous, and the quotient is a finite connected \(2\)-valent graph, hence a circle. Since every fiber is an infinite \(\mathbb Z\)-orbit, the lifted graph is the infinite connected cover of a circle, therefore homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).

   Also record that quotient leaves have disjoint relative interiors: inside one triangle distinct cevians meet only at the apex, and across triangles the face-to-face property prevents interior crossings. Projection injectivity on each closed triangle prevents a leaf from having its two endpoints identified in the quotient.

3. **Degree patch in the oriented-line lemma.**  
   Let \(g(s)=\phi(s,1/2)\). If \(g(s+1/2)=-g(s)\), choose a continuous lift \(g(s)=e^{i\theta(s)}\) on \([0,1]\). For \(s\in[0,1/2]\),
   \[
   \theta(s+1/2)-\theta(s)-\pi\in 2\pi\mathbb Z.
   \]
   By continuity this integer is constant, say \(m\). Hence
   \[
   \theta(1/2)-\theta(0)=\pi+2\pi m,\qquad
   \theta(1)-\theta(1/2)=\pi+2\pi m,
   \]
   so
   \[
   \deg g=\frac{\theta(1)-\theta(0)}{2\pi}=2m+1,
   \]
   which is odd. But \(g\) is homotopic through \(\phi(s,\delta)\), \(0\le\delta\le1/2\), to the constant map at \(\delta=0\), so its degree must be \(0\), contradiction.

4. **Embeddedness patch for the construction.**  
   First note that all target faces are nondegenerate. Horizontal faces are scaled equilateral triangles with scales \(1\), \(1-\eta\), \(1+\eta\), all positive. For side-panel faces, two vertices lie on the same cluster line \(q_i(r,z)\), whose barycentric coordinates satisfy equality of the two coordinates different from \(i\); the third vertex lies in a different cluster \(q_j(r,z)\), \(j\ne i\), and violates that equality because its scale \(r>0\). Hence no side-panel triangle is collinear.

   Next, each side panel \(R_{ij}\) is planar: its four vertices lie in the affine plane spanned by \(p_j-p_i\) and the vector joining the two corresponding endpoint levels. It is a convex trapezoid because its horizontal slices are the segments \(S_{ij}(r(z),z)\) with \(r(z)>0\). The chosen diagonal splits it into two triangles meeting exactly along that diagonal.

   Finally, equality of points in \(\mathbb R^3\) forces equality of their \(z\)-coordinates. For each fixed \(z\), the proof’s barycentric slice computation shows:
   - for \(0<z<h\), the only slices are \(S_{12}(r)\) and \(S_{20}(s)\) with \(r\ne s\), so they are disjoint;
   - for \(h<z<2h\), the only slices are \(S_{01}(r)\) and \(S_{12}(s)\) with \(r\ne s\), so they are disjoint;
   - at \(z=0,h,2h\), the intersections are exactly the prescribed common edges and vertices of the quotient triangulation.
   Thus distinct faces meet only in the prescribed quotient incidences. Therefore \(\Phi\) is injective, and since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) Hausdorff, \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

5. **Cluster-incidence patch for squeeze inequalities.**  
   The cluster labels of \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) are
   \[
   0,0,0,\;1,1,1,\;2,2,2.
   \]
   Hence \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) are in the same cluster exactly when \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), and in distinct clusters when \(j\equiv2\pmod3\). Also \(P_j\) and \(P_{j+5}\) are in distinct clusters for every \(j\pmod9\). This justifies all uses of the intra-cluster and inter-cluster distance bounds in the boundary-edge shortening and nonboundary-edge lengthening checks.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
