   \]
   Hence \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) are in the same cluster exactly when \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), and in distinct clusters when \(j\equiv2\pmod3\). Also \(P_j\) and \(P_{j+5}\) are in distinct clusters for every \(j\pmod9\). This justifies all uses of the intra-cluster and inter-cluster distance bounds in the boundary-edge shortening and nonboundary-edge lengthening checks.
parsed.problem_solved:
After the routine patches above are inserted, the proof establishes the stronger classification
\[
\{\beta>0:\beta\text{ is realized}\}=(\sqrt3,\infty).
\]
In particular, the infimum of the set of realized numbers is \(\sqrt3\).

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.
claim_verified: With the routine patches above inserted, the proof establishes the stronger statement that \(\beta\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Therefore the realized set is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\).
solution_verified:
**Claim.** A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if and only if \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Hence the set of realized numbers is \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), whose infimum is \(\sqrt3\).

## Proof

We prove the two directions.

---

## 1. Construction for every \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>\sqrt3\). Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L_0=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(L_0<W\). Choose
\[
L_0<\sigma<W
\]
and put
\[
\Delta=\min\{\sigma-L_0,\; W-\sigma\}>0.
\]
Choose
\[
0<\lambda<\min\{\Delta/20,\beta/12\}.
\]

Define a positive sequence \((\ell_j)_{j\in\mathbb Z}\) by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod 3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod 3.
\end{cases}
\]
Let \(c_0=0\) and define \(c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j\). Then
\[
c_{j+3}=c_j+W,\qquad c_{j+9}=c_j+3W=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
Let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
For \(s\in[0,1]\), the horizontal slice of \(Q_j\) at height \(s\) is the interval between
\[
(1-s)c_j+s(c_{j+5}-\beta)
\]
and
\[
(1-s)c_{j+1}+s(c_{j+6}-\beta).
\]
Its length is \((1-s)\ell_j+s\ell_{j+5}>0\). Hence the \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely. Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), giving clean triangles
\[
D_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j\},
\]
\[
E_j=\operatorname{conv}\{Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}\}.
\]

The triangulation is \(G_\beta\)-invariant. Indeed,
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Thus
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]

In the quotient, write \(v_j\) for the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), indices are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Therefore the quotient has faces
\[
T_j=(v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}),\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

Now choose an equilateral triangle in \(\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\subset\mathbb R^3\) with vertices \(a_0,a_1,a_2\), side length \(\sigma\), and centroid \(0\). Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\), and define
\[
q_i(r,z)=r a_i+z e_3.
\]
For small \(\eta,h>0\), assign target vertices \(P_j\in\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
\begin{array}{c|ccccccccc}
j&0&1&2&3&4&5&6&7&8\\ \hline
P_j&
q_0(1+\eta,2h)&
q_0(1-\eta,h)&
q_0(1,0)&
q_1(1,0)&
q_1(1+\eta,2h)&
q_1(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1-\eta,h)&
q_2(1,0)&
q_2(1+\eta,2h).
\end{array}
\]
Choose \(\eta,h\) so small that:

1. if \(P_i,P_j\) lie in the same cluster near one \(a_k\), then
   \[
   |P_i-P_j|<\lambda/2;
   \]
2. if \(P_i,P_j\) lie in different clusters, then
   \[
   \sigma-\Delta/4<|P_i-P_j|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
   \]

Define \(F\) on the quotient by \(F(v_j)=P_j\), extending affinely over every \(T_j\).

The nine image triangles form an embedded PL Möbius band. Indeed, the triangles
\[
T_2=(v_2,v_3,v_7),\quad T_5=(v_5,v_6,v_1),\quad T_8=(v_8,v_0,v_4)
\]
map to three horizontal equilateral triangles of scales \(1\), \(1-\eta\), and \(1+\eta\), respectively. The other six triangles form three trapezoidal side panels.

Using barycentric coordinates relative to \(a_0,a_1,a_2\), the homothetic triangle
\[
\Delta(r)=\operatorname{conv}\{r a_0,r a_1,r a_2\}
\]
is described by
\[
\lambda_i\ge \frac{1-r}{3}.
\]
Its side \(S_{ij}(r)\) joining \(r a_i\) to \(r a_j\) is given by \(\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\), where \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\). Hence adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) are disjoint when \(r\ne s\), and \(S_{ij}(r)\cap\Delta(s)=\varnothing\) when \(r>s\).

The three side panels have horizontal slices
\[
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta z/h\right),\qquad 0\le z\le h,
\]
\[
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta z/(2h)\right),\qquad 0\le z\le2h,
\]
and
\[
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h\right),\qquad h\le z\le2h.
\]
The slice comparison above shows that distinct panels meet only along their prescribed common vertices or edges, and the horizontal triangles meet the panels exactly along the prescribed sides. Therefore \(F\) is injective on the quotient surface.

We now verify the squeeze inequalities.

The quotient boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has domain length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in the same cluster, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\lambda/2<\ell_j.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and \(P_j,P_{j+1}\) lie in different clusters, so
\[
|P_j-P_{j+1}|<\sigma+\Delta/4.
\]
Because \(\Delta\le W-\sigma\),
\[
\sigma+\Delta/4\le W-\frac{3\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
Thus every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

For the nonboundary edges, put
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
A direct computation from the period-three pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\beta/3.
\]
Therefore
\[
|X_jY_j|<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L_0.
\]
The other nonboundary edge type \(X_{j+1}Y_j\) has the same estimate. On the target side, every nonboundary edge joins two different clusters, hence has length
\[
>\sigma-\Delta/4.
\]
Because \(\Delta\le\sigma-L_0\),
\[
\sigma-\Delta/4\ge L_0+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L_0.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened.

Finally define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with \(P_{j+9}=P_j\), and extend affinely over all \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous. Also \(f\circ G_\beta=f\) on vertices and hence on every triangle. Since the induced quotient map \(F\) is embedded,
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\]
if and only if \(p,q\) project to the same quotient point, i.e. if and only if
\[
p=G_\beta^k(q)
\]
for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\). Hence every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized.

---

## 2. No \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. Let \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) be a realizing map for a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation. The orbit condition immediately gives
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f.
\]

We first record a useful elementary fact.

### Lemma 1: cevians are lengthened

Let \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle whose boundary edge is \([r_0,r_1]\), and let \(A:\tau\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For
\[
r(s)=(1-s)r_0+s r_1,\qquad 0\le s\le1,
\]
the segment \([a,r(s)]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

Indeed, define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r(s))|^2-|a-r(s)|^2.
\]
The two nonboundary edges are strictly lengthened, so \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\left(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\right)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is strictly shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave, and therefore
\[
q(s)\ge (1-s)q(0)+s q(1)>0.
\]
So every cevian from the apex to the boundary edge is strictly lengthened. The statement about boundary subsegments follows because \(A\) restricts to an affine contraction on the boundary edge. \(\square\)

Refine the triangulation as follows. In each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every original boundary vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the apex. Local finiteness guarantees that only finitely many subdivision points occur on each boundary edge. Performing this equivariantly gives a new locally finite \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation. By Lemma 1, \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

This refined triangulation is face-to-face. Distinct boundary edges cannot have overlapping relative interiors: at an interior point of such an overlap, both adjacent triangles would contain the same small half-disk inside \(\Sigma\), contradicting disjoint interiors. A nonboundary edge meets \(\partial\Sigma\) only at its endpoints; if such an endpoint lies in the interior of a boundary edge, it was inserted as a subdivision vertex. If two nonboundary edges meet in the interior of \(\Sigma\), then either they cross transversely or are collinear. A transverse crossing is impossible because near the crossing the two incident triangles contain open half-neighborhoods whose intersection is an open sector, again contradicting disjoint interiors. If two nonboundary edges are collinear, then their supporting line meets \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) in unique points, so the two edges have the same endpoints and coincide. Hence closed refined triangles meet only in common faces.

Let
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle.
\]
This is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Because \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the map \(f\) descends to a map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3.
\]
The orbit condition implies that \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact, \(F\) is an embedding.

The quotient triangulation is finite: a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, and every triangle has some translate meeting that rectangle.

We also need that the quotient cellulation is regular. Every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by distance at least \(\beta\). Indeed, for \(k=2n\ne0\),
\[
G_\beta^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),
\]
and for \(k=2n+1\),
\[
G_\beta^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y).
\]
Thus \(|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta\) for \(k\ne0\).

If \(f\) were not injective on some refined triangle, then, since \(f\) is affine there, its linear part would have a nontrivial kernel, giving arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\) in that triangle with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The orbit condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k(q)\) with \(k\ne0\), contradicting the distance bound. Therefore \(f\) is injective on each refined triangle. Consequently no closed refined triangle, edge, or leaf has a self-identification under the deck action. The quotient is therefore a finite regular PL triangulation of \(M_\beta\).

Moreover, every nontrivial boundary arc in \(M_\beta\) is strictly shortened by \(F\): subdivide the arc at boundary vertices and endpoints; on each nontrivial subarc inside one boundary edge, \(F\) is an affine contraction.

Now construct the leaf graph. In each clean triangle, consider the interval of cevians from the apex to the boundary edge. Glue endpoints of these intervals whenever the corresponding cevians are the same nonboundary edge. The resulting finite graph \(\Lambda_M\) parametrizes all leaves in \(M_\beta\).

Every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\), because every nonboundary edge of the regular triangulation is incident to exactly two triangles. The graph is connected. Indeed, if \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, both adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan, and consecutive triangles in that fan share nonboundary edges, so they also all lie in the same \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is closed because the triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Thus \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\).

The lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected by the same open-closed argument, using local finiteness to ensure closedness. The projection
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M
\]
is a covering. It is infinite: no nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) fixes a lifted leaf. If a lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\), then an even power \(G_\beta^{2n}\) fixes it only if \(2n\beta=0\), while an odd power would require simultaneously
\[
x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible. Therefore \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is the infinite cyclic cover of \(\Lambda_M\), so
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R.
\]

Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). By Lemma 1, each leaf is strictly lengthened by \(f\), so its image is a nondegenerate segment with an oriented support line in \(\mathbb R^3\). Along a fundamental interval in \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) from a leaf \(L\) to \(G_\beta L\), the final oriented image line is the initial one with the opposite orientation, because \(G_\beta\) swaps the two boundary components while \(f\circ G_\beta=f\).

We use the following topological lemma.

### Lemma 2: an oriented-line antipodal path contains perpendicular intersecting lines

Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\), and suppose \(L_1\) is \(L_0\) with the opposite orientation. Then there exist distinct parameters \(s,t\) such that the underlying unoriented lines of \(L_s\) and \(L_t\) are perpendicular and intersect.

Represent an oriented affine line by \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is the unit direction and \(m=p\times u\) for any point \(p\) on the line. Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\). Extend the path by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under
\[
I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta),
\]
we have
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\ne(0,0)\) everywhere. Then
\[
\phi(s,\delta)=\frac{(a(s,\delta),b(s,\delta))}
{\sqrt{a(s,\delta)^2+b(s,\delta)^2}}
\]
defines a map \(S^1\times[0,1]\to S^1\). Put
\[
\gamma(s)=\phi(s,1/2).
\]
The symmetry gives
\[
\gamma(s+1/2)=-\gamma(s).
\]
Choose a continuous angle lift \(\theta\) on \([0,1]\), so \(\gamma(s)=e^{i\theta(s)}\). Then
\[
\theta(s+1/2)-\theta(s)=(2m+1)\pi
\]
for a locally constant, hence constant, integer \(m\). Therefore
\[
\theta(1)-\theta(0)=2(2m+1)\pi,
\]
so \(\deg(\gamma)=2m+1\), which is odd. But \(\gamma\) is homotopic through the lower half-cylinder \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant loop \(\phi(s,0)=(1,0)\), so its degree must be \(0\). Contradiction.

Thus \((a,b)=(0,0)\) somewhere. At such a point, \(a=0\) means the two directions are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the directions are nonparallel, and \(b=0\) says the two lines are coplanar. Therefore they intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 2 to the support-line path gives two distinct quotient leaves \(T\) and \(B\) whose image support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect. Let
\[
P=\ell_T\cap \ell_B.
\]

The relative interiors of distinct leaves are disjoint in \(M_\beta\). Since \(F\) is embedded, the two image segments cannot both contain \(P\) in their relative interiors. Relabel if necessary so that \(F(B)\) does not contain \(P\) in its relative interior. Then \(F(B)\) lies in one closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\). Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set
\[
Q=F(q).
\]
Because \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Also
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
A lifted leaf joins \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and by Lemma 1 its image length is strictly larger. Hence
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)>1.
\]

Let \(A,C\) be the endpoints of the image segment \(F(T)\), and put
\[
L=|A-C|.
\]
The endpoints of \(T\) are distinct in the quotient: otherwise two distinct points of one refined triangle would be deck-equivalent, contradicting injectivity of \(f\) on that triangle. Therefore the two boundary arcs between the endpoints of \(T\) have positive lengths summing to \(2\beta\). Write those lengths as
\[
\beta-t,\qquad \beta+t,
\]
with \(|t|<\beta\). Let \(D\) be the arc containing \(q\), and \(H\) the other arc.

Choose a lift of \(T\) with endpoints
\[
(x,0),\qquad (x+h,1).
\]
The boundary circle has length \(2\beta\), with arclength coordinate
\[
\rho(u,0)=u\pmod{2\beta},\qquad \rho(u,1)=u-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Thus the coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is
\[
h-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two unoriented boundary arc lengths are \(\beta-t\) and \(\beta+t\), this difference is congruent to either \(\beta-t\) or \(\beta+t\) modulo \(2\beta\). Hence
\[
h\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), every representative of the class \(\pm t\pmod{2\beta}\) has absolute value at least \(|t|\). Therefore
\[
|h|\ge |t|.
\]
The leaf \(T\) is strictly lengthened, so
\[
L>\sqrt{1+h^2}\ge \sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

Now use boundary shortening. The arc \(H\) connects the endpoints of \(T\), so
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(H))\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
But \(H\) is strictly shortened:
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(H))<\beta-t.
\]
Therefore
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

Similarly, the arc \(D\) connects the endpoints of \(T\) and passes through \(q\), so
\[
\operatorname{Len}(F(D))\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line through \(A,C\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Because \(D\) is strictly shortened,
\[
\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2},
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\;
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), then
\[
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while
\[
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t<\sqrt3
\]
would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

Thus every realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\), while every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized. Therefore the realized set is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

1. **Embeddedness of the constructed quotient map.**  
   As \((\eta,h)\to(0,0)\), all same-cluster distances tend to \(0\), while all different-cluster distances tend to the equilateral side length \(\sigma\). Since only finitely many pairs occur, the two required distance inequalities hold for sufficiently small \(\eta,h>0\).

   For embeddedness, identify the three horizontal triangles and three side panels:
   \[
   T_2=\Delta(1)\times\{0\},\quad T_5=\Delta(1-\eta)\times\{h\},\quad T_8=\Delta(1+\eta)\times\{2h\},
   \]
   and
   \[
   T_1\cup T_6:\ S_{20}(1-\eta z/h),\ 0\le z\le h,
   \]
   \[
   T_3\cup T_7:\ S_{12}(1+\eta z/(2h)),\ 0\le z\le2h,
   \]
   \[
   T_0\cup T_4:\ S_{01}(1-\eta+2\eta(z-h)/h),\ h\le z\le2h.
   \]
   Using barycentric coordinates, \(\Delta(r)=\{\lambda_i\ge(1-r)/3\}\), and \(S_{ij}(r)\) is given by \(\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\). Hence adjacent sides \(S_{ij}(r)\) and \(S_{jk}(s)\) meet only when \(r=s\), in their common vertex, and \(S_{ij}(r)\cap\Delta(s)=\varnothing\) for \(r>s\).

   Comparing slices at common heights: the first and second panels meet only at \(z=0\), at the prescribed vertex \(P_7\); the first and third meet only at \(z=h\), at \(P_1\); the second and third meet only at \(z=2h\), at \(P_4\). The horizontal triangles meet panels only along their prescribed edges. Thus no unintended intersections occur, so the quotient map \(F\) is injective.

2. **Missing nonboundary-edge computation.**  
   For \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), the horizontal displacement is
   \[
   c_{j+5}-c_{j+1}-\beta.
   \]
   From the period-three pattern,
   \[
   c_{j+5}-c_{j+1}=
   \begin{cases}
   W+\lambda,& j\equiv0,2\pmod3,\\
   2W-2\lambda,& j\equiv1\pmod3.
   \end{cases}
   \]
   Since \(W=2\beta/3\) and \(\lambda<\beta/12\), in all cases
   \[
   |c_{j+5}-c_{j+1}-\beta|<\beta/3.
   \]
   Hence every edge of type \(X_{j+1}Y_j\) has domain length \(<L_0\), as required.

3. **Regular quotient and leaf graph.**  
   After subdivision, two refined triangles meet only in a common face: overlapping boundary edges have been subdivided at all boundary vertices; nonboundary edges cannot cross transversely without producing overlapping triangle interiors; and collinear nonboundary edges have the same endpoints, hence coincide. Thus the refined triangulation is face-to-face.

   The quotient is finite because a compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles. If a nontrivial deck transformation identified two points of one closed refined triangle, then the affine restriction of \(f\) to that triangle would fail to be injective. Noninjectivity of an affine map on a triangle gives arbitrarily close distinct points with the same image, contradicting the orbit condition and the lower bound \(|G_\beta^k(p)-p|\ge\beta\) for \(k\ne0\). Hence no quotient simplex has self-identifications, and the quotient is a finite regular PL triangulation of \(M_\beta\).

   For the leaf graph, each triangle contributes one interval of cevians, and endpoints are glued exactly across common nonboundary edges. Since every nonboundary edge of a regular surface triangulation is incident to exactly two triangles, every leaf-graph vertex has valence \(2\). For a component \(C\), the union \(U_C\) of triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\) is open: across nonboundary edges both adjacent triangles are included, and around boundary vertices the incident fan is connected through nonboundary edges. It is also closed, since the quotient triangulation is finite. Because \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected; finite connected \(2\)-regular graphs are circles, so \(\Lambda_M\cong S^1\). The same local argument gives connectedness of the lifted graph, and the endpoint calculation in the proof shows the covering is infinite, so \(\Lambda_\Sigma\cong\mathbb R\).

4. **Support-line path.**  
   On each cevian interval, the image segment endpoints depend continuously on the cevian parameter, and Lemma 1 ensures the image segment is nondegenerate. At a glued endpoint corresponding to a common nonboundary edge, the two adjacent affine maps agree on that edge, and the bottom-to-top orientation is the same from both sides. Therefore the oriented support line varies continuously along \(\Lambda_\Sigma\). Since \(G_\beta\) swaps \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) while \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), the oriented line at \(G_\beta L\) is the opposite orientation of the line at \(L\). Lemma 2 therefore applies on a fundamental interval. Its perpendicular-intersection pair cannot be merely the identified endpoints, so it gives two distinct quotient leaves.

5. **Boundary-arc convention and distance estimate.**  
   Define \(t\) so that the boundary arc \(H\) not containing \(q\) has length \(\beta-t\), and the arc \(D\) containing \(q\) has length \(\beta+t\). Since \(q\) has image distance \(>1\) from \(\ell_T\), it is not an endpoint of \(T\), so \(D\) contains \(q\) in its relative interior.

   For the geometric estimate, put \(A=(-L/2,0,0)\), \(C=(L/2,0,0)\), and write \(Q=(s,y,0)\) after an isometry, where \(y=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)\). Then
   \[
   |A-Q|+|Q-C|
   =
   \sqrt{(s+L/2)^2+y^2}+\sqrt{(s-L/2)^2+y^2}.
   \]
   This even convex function of \(s\) is minimized at \(s=0\), giving
   \[
   |A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge 2\sqrt{(L/2)^2+y^2}=\sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
   \]
   This justifies the inequality used to derive \(\beta+t>\sqrt{5+t^2}\).

original_problem:
Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

solution_verified:
## Original Problem

Let $\Z$ denote the integers and let $\R$ denote the reals.
Let $\Sigma= \R \times [0,1]$ and also let
$\partial \Sigma=\R \times \{0,1\}$ denote the boundary
of $\Sigma$. 
For $\beta>0$ define $G_{\beta}: \Sigma \to \Sigma$ as follows.
$$G_{\beta}(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).$$
Let $G_{\beta}^k$ denote the $k$-fold composition of either
$G_{\beta}$
or the inverse $G_{\beta}^{-1}$, according as $k>0$ or $k<0$.
When $k=0$ the map $G_{\beta}^k$ is the identity map.

A {\it clean triangle\/} is the convex hull  of $3$
non-collinear points
in $\partial \Sigma$. 
A {\it clean triangulation\/} of $\Sigma$ is a countable and
locally finite collection
of clean triangles, having pairwise disjoint interiors, whose union
is $\Sigma$.  The clean triangulation is
$G_{\beta}$-{\it invariant\/} if the action of $G_{\beta}$ preserves
the triangulation.

A {\it squeeze map\/} of a clean triangle $\tau$
is an affine map from $\tau$ into $\R^3$ which decreases the
length of the edge of $\tau$ that is contained in
$\partial \Sigma$ and increases the
lengths of the other two edges of $\tau$.

Call the number $\beta>0$ {\it realized\/} if there is a
continuous map $f: \Sigma \to \R^3$
with the following properties.
\begin{enumerate}
\item $f$ restricts to a squeeze map on each triangle of a
  $G_{\beta}$-invariant clean triangulation of $\Sigma$.
  \item   $f(p)=f(q)$ if and only if  $p=G_{\beta}^k(q)$ for some $k
    \in \Z$.
    This is meant to hold for all $p,q \in \Sigma$.
  \end{enumerate}
  Prove that $\sqrt 3$ is the infimum of the set of realized numbers.

## Problem Solved

Let \(\mathbb Z\) be the integers and \(\mathbb R\) the reals. For \(\beta>0\), let
\[
\Sigma=\mathbb R\times[0,1],\qquad \partial\Sigma=\mathbb R\times\{0,1\},
\]
and let
\[
G_\beta(x,y)=(x+\beta,1-y).
\]
For \(k\in\mathbb Z\), \(G_\beta^k\) denotes the \(k\)-fold iterate of \(G_\beta\), using \(G_\beta^{-1}\) when \(k<0\), and \(G_\beta^0\) is the identity. A clean triangle is the convex hull of three non-collinear points of \(\partial\Sigma\). Its unique edge lying in \(\partial\Sigma\) is called its boundary edge. A clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) is a countable locally finite family of clean triangles with pairwise disjoint interiors and union \(\Sigma\). It is \(G_\beta\)-invariant if \(G_\beta\) permutes its triangles. A squeeze map on a clean triangle is an affine map into \(\mathbb R^3\) that strictly shortens the boundary edge and strictly lengthens the other two edges. A number \(\beta>0\) is realized if there are a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) whose restriction to every triangle is a squeeze map and such that, for all \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
The proof below establishes the stronger exact classification:
\[
\beta\text{ is realized}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad \beta>\sqrt3.
\]
Consequently the set of realized numbers is exactly \((\sqrt3,\infty)\), and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). This is not a relaxation of the original problem; it proves the original infimum assertion and identifies the realized set exactly.

## Proof

### I. The lower bound: a realized \(\beta\) satisfies \(\beta>\sqrt3\)

Assume \(\beta>0\) is realized. Write \(G=G_\beta\). Thus there is a \(G\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T_0\) of \(\Sigma\) and a continuous map \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) satisfying the two realization properties.

#### 1. Cevian lengthening and a face-to-face refinement

We first record a useful elementary consequence of the squeeze condition.

**Lemma 1.** Let \(\Delta=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\) be a clean triangle, where \([r_0,r_1]\subset\partial\Sigma\) is its boundary edge, and let \(A:\Delta\to\mathbb R^3\) be a squeeze map. For every point \(r=(1-s)r_0+sr_1\), \(0\le s\le1\), the cevian \([a,r]\) is strictly lengthened by \(A\). Also every nontrivial subsegment of \([r_0,r_1]\) is strictly shortened.

**Proof.** Define
\[
q(s)=|A(a)-A(r)|^2-|a-r|^2.
\]
Since the two nonboundary sides are lengthened, \(q(0)>0\) and \(q(1)>0\). Since \(A\) is affine,
\[
q''(s)=2\bigl(|A(r_1)-A(r_0)|^2-|r_1-r_0|^2\bigr)<0,
\]
because the boundary edge is shortened. Thus \(q\) is strictly concave and lies strictly above its chord on \(0<s<1\). Hence \(q(s)>0\) for all \(s\in[0,1]\). The restriction of \(A\) to the boundary edge is an affine contraction by a factor \(<1\), so all nontrivial boundary subsegments are shortened. \(\square\)

We now refine \(\mathcal T_0\). For each triangle, subdivide its boundary edge at every triangulation vertex lying on that edge, and cone those subdivision points to the opposite apex. Local finiteness implies that only finitely many such vertices lie on any compact boundary edge, so this produces a countable locally finite refinement. It is still \(G\)-invariant. By Lemma 1, \(f\) remains a squeeze map on every refined triangle.

Denote the refined triangulation by \(\mathcal T\). It may be taken to be face-to-face: boundary vertex-in-edge incidences and boundary edge overlaps have been subdivided away; transverse crossings of nonboundary edges would force overlap of triangular interiors in a small sector; and two collinear nonboundary edges cannot overlap properly, because a line joining \(y=0\) to \(y=1\) has unique endpoints on those two boundary lines. Thus distinct closed triangles of \(\mathcal T\) meet, if at all, in a common face.

#### 2. Passage to an embedded finite PL Möbius band

The quotient
\[
M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G\rangle
\]
is the flat Möbius band obtained from \([0,\beta]\times[0,1]\) by identifying
\[
(0,y)\sim(\beta,1-y).
\]
Its boundary is one circle of length \(2\beta\).

Because \(f(Gp)=f(p)\), the map \(f\) descends to a continuous map
\[
F:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3,\qquad F([p])=f(p).
\]
If \(F([p])=F([q])\), then \(f(p)=f(q)\), so by the realization condition \(p=G^k(q)\) for some \(k\in\mathbb Z\), hence \([p]=[q]\). Thus \(F\) is injective. Since \(M_\beta\) is compact and \(\mathbb R^3\) is Hausdorff, \(F\) is a topological embedding.

We also need a finite regular quotient triangulation. For \(k\ne0\),
\[
G^{2n}(x,y)=(x+2n\beta,y),\qquad
G^{2n+1}(x,y)=(x+(2n+1)\beta,1-y),
\]
so every nontrivial deck transformation moves every point by Euclidean distance at least \(\beta\).

If \(f\) were noninjective on some refined triangle \(\Delta\), then the affine linear part of \(f|_\Delta\) would have rank \(<2\), giving arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G^k(q)\) for some nonzero \(k\), contradicting \(|p-q|<\beta\). Hence \(f\) is injective on every closed triangle.

Consequently no closed triangle \(\Delta\in\mathcal T\) meets a nontrivial translate of itself. Indeed, if \(z\in\Delta\cap G^k\Delta\), \(k\ne0\), then \(z=G^k w\) for some \(w\in\Delta\), so \(f(z)=f(w)\); injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) gives \(z=w\), impossible because the deck action is free.

A compact fundamental rectangle meets only finitely many refined triangles, and every triangle has some translate meeting it. Therefore \(\mathcal T/\langle G\rangle\) is a finite regular clean triangulation of \(M_\beta\), and \(F\) is affine on each quotient triangle.

Finally, \(F\) strictly shortens every nontrivial boundary arc. Indeed, subdivide such an arc at its endpoints and at the finitely many boundary vertices of the quotient triangulation. Each resulting subarc lies in a boundary edge on which \(F\) has contraction factor \(<1\).

#### 3. The cevian leaf graph

For each quotient triangle, consider the interval of cevians from its apex to its boundary edge. The two endpoints of that interval correspond to the two nonboundary sides. Glue two such endpoints whenever the corresponding nonboundary sides are the same edge of the quotient triangulation. This produces a finite graph \(\Lambda_M\). A point of \(\Lambda_M\) will be called a leaf; geometrically it is a single cevian segment in \(M_\beta\).

Each nonboundary edge is an interior edge of the Möbius band and is incident to exactly two triangles. Therefore every vertex of \(\Lambda_M\) has valence \(2\), so \(\Lambda_M\) is a disjoint union of circles.

It is connected. If \(C\) is a component of \(\Lambda_M\), let \(U_C\subset M_\beta\) be the union of all closed triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, the two adjacent triangles belong to the same \(U_C\). At a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a fan; consecutive triangles in the fan share nonboundary edges, except at the two boundary extremes, so all triangles incident to that boundary vertex belong to the same \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is also closed, because the quotient triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, \(\Lambda_M\) is connected. Therefore
\[
\Lambda_M\cong S^1.
\]

Construct the lifted leaf graph \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) in the same way using the triangulation \(\mathcal T\) upstairs. The same open-closed argument applies; closedness follows because a locally finite union of closed triangles is closed. Since \(\Sigma\) is connected, \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected. The projection \(\Sigma\to M_\beta\) induces a covering
\[
\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M.
\]

No nontrivial deck transformation fixes a lifted leaf. A lifted leaf has endpoints \((x_0,0)\) and \((x_1,1)\). An even power \(G^{2n}\) translates both endpoints by \(2n\beta\), so it can fix the leaf only when \(n=0\). An odd power \(G^{2n+1}\) would force simultaneously
\[
x_0=x_1+(2n+1)\beta,\qquad x_1=x_0+(2n+1)\beta,
\]
which is impossible for \(\beta>0\). Hence the covering \(\Lambda_\Sigma\to\Lambda_M\cong S^1\) has infinite degree. Since \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) is connected, it is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).

Choose a coordinate \(s\in\mathbb R\) on \(\Lambda_\Sigma\) such that the deck transformation \(G\) sends \(s\) to \(s+1\). Orient every lifted leaf from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Its image under \(f\) is a nondegenerate segment, because cevians are strictly lengthened. Let \(L_s\) be the oriented support line of the image segment.

If a leaf has bottom endpoint \(b\) and top endpoint \(t\), then \(G\) sends it to a leaf whose bottom endpoint is \(G(t)\) and whose top endpoint is \(G(b)\). Since \(f\circ G=f\), the image segment is the same geometric segment with the opposite orientation. Therefore
\[
L_{s+1}=L_s^{\mathrm{opp}}.
\]

#### 4. A lemma on paths of oriented lines

**Lemma 2.** Let \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), be a continuous path of oriented affine lines in \(\mathbb R^3\) such that \(L_1=L_0^{\mathrm{opp}}\). Then there are two distinct parameters whose underlying unoriented lines are perpendicular and intersect.

**Proof.** Represent an oriented line by a pair \((u,m)\), where \(u\in S^2\) is its unit direction and
\[
m=p\times u
\]
for any point \(p\) on the line. The vector \(m\) is independent of the choice of \(p\). Reversing orientation sends \((u,m)\) to \((-u,-m)\).

Extend the path anti-periodically by
\[
(u(s+1),m(s+1))=-(u(s),m(s)).
\]
For \(s\in\mathbb R/\mathbb Z\) and \(0\le\delta\le1\), define
\[
a(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot u(s+\delta),
\]
\[
b(s,\delta)=u(s)\cdot m(s+\delta)+u(s+\delta)\cdot m(s).
\]
Then
\[
(a,b)(s,0)=(1,0),\qquad (a,b)(s,1)=(-1,0).
\]
Also, under the involution \(I(s,\delta)=(s+\delta,1-\delta)\),
\[
(a,b)\circ I=-(a,b).
\]

Suppose \((a,b)\) never vanishes. Then
\[
\phi=\frac{(a,b)}{\sqrt{a^2+b^2}}
\]
defines a continuous map to \(S^1\). On \(\delta=0\), this map is constant. But on the middle circle \(\delta=1/2\),
\[
\phi(s+1/2,1/2)=-\phi(s,1/2).
\]
A circle map with this antipodal half-period property has odd degree: lifting its argument \(\theta(s)\) gives \(\theta(s+1/2)=\theta(s)+\pi+2\pi N\) for a fixed integer \(N\), hence degree \(2N+1\). This contradicts the fact that the middle-circle map is homotopic through \(0\le\delta\le1/2\) to the constant map on \(\delta=0\). Therefore \((a,b)=0\) somewhere with \(0<\delta<1\).

At such a zero, \(a=0\) says the two directions \(u,v\) are perpendicular. If the two lines are represented by \((u,p\times u)\) and \((v,q\times v)\), then
\[
b=u\cdot(q\times v)+v\cdot(p\times u)=(p-q)\cdot(u\times v).
\]
Since \(u\perp v\), the lines are nonparallel; \(b=0\) says they are coplanar. Nonparallel coplanar lines intersect. \(\square\)

Applying Lemma 2 to the path \(L_s\), \(0\le s\le1\), gives two distinct quotient leaves, call them \(T\) and \(B\), whose support lines \(\ell_T,\ell_B\) are perpendicular and intersect at a point \(P\).

#### 5. The metric \(T\)-pattern estimate

Distinct leaves have disjoint relative interiors in \(M_\beta\), though they may share boundary endpoints. Since \(F\) is an embedding, \(P\) cannot lie in the relative interiors of both image segments. After possibly interchanging the two leaves, assume \(P\) is not in the relative interior of \(F(B)\). Then the segment \(F(B)\) lies in a closed ray of \(\ell_B\) starting at \(P\).

Let \(q\) be the endpoint of \(B\) farther from \(P\), and set \(Q=F(q)\). Since \(\ell_B\perp\ell_T\),
\[
y:=\operatorname{dist}(Q,\ell_T)=|Q-P|.
\]
Because \(F(B)\) lies in a ray from \(P\),
\[
|Q-P|\ge |F(B)|.
\]
The leaf \(B\) connects \(y=0\) to \(y=1\), so its flat length is at least \(1\), and by Lemma 1 its image is strictly longer. Hence
\[
y>1.
\]
In particular, \(q\) is not an endpoint of \(T\); otherwise \(Q\in\ell_T\), so \(y=0\).

Let \(a,c\) be the endpoints of \(T\), and put
\[
A=F(a),\qquad C=F(c),\qquad L=|A-C|.
\]
The endpoints \(a,c\) are distinct on the boundary circle of \(M_\beta\), since otherwise a lift of them would be deck-equivalent and would have equal \(f\)-images, contradicting the strict lengthening of \(T\).

Let \(D\) be the boundary arc from \(a\) to \(c\) containing \(q\), and let \(H\) be the other boundary arc. Their flat lengths sum to \(2\beta\), so for some \(t\in(-\beta,\beta)\),
\[
\ell_0(D)=\beta+t,\qquad \ell_0(H)=\beta-t.
\]

Choose a lift of \(T\) with bottom endpoint \((x,0)\) and top endpoint \((x+h_T,1)\). Identify the boundary of \(M_\beta\) with \(\mathbb R/2\beta\mathbb Z\) by assigning coordinate \(u\) to \((u,0)\) and coordinate \(u-\beta\) to \((u,1)\). The coordinate difference between the endpoints of \(T\) is therefore
\[
h_T-\beta\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Since the two boundary arc lengths between them are \(\beta+t\) and \(\beta-t\), we get
\[
h_T\equiv \pm t\pmod{2\beta}.
\]
Because \(|t|<\beta\), this implies
\[
|h_T|\ge |t|.
\]
Thus, by strict lengthening of \(T\),
\[
L>|T|_0=\sqrt{1+h_T^2}\ge\sqrt{1+t^2}. \tag{1}
\]

For the arc \(H\), strict boundary shortening gives
\[
\beta-t=\ell_0(H)>\ell_F(H).
\]
The image \(F(H)\) joins \(A\) to \(C\), so
\[
\ell_F(H)\ge |A-C|=L.
\]
Using (1),
\[
\beta-t>L>\sqrt{1+t^2},
\]
hence
\[
\beta>\sqrt{1+t^2}+t. \tag{2}
\]

For the arc \(D\), the path \(F(D)\) joins \(A\) to \(C\) and passes through \(Q\). Therefore
\[
\ell_F(D)\ge |A-Q|+|Q-C|.
\]
Since \(Q\) has distance \(y\) from the line \(AC\), the broken-line length is minimized when the orthogonal projection of \(Q\) to \(AC\) is the midpoint of \(AC\), so
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|\ge \sqrt{L^2+4y^2}.
\]
Using \(L>\sqrt{1+t^2}\) and \(y>1\),
\[
|A-Q|+|Q-C|>\sqrt{5+t^2}.
\]
Strict boundary shortening on \(D\) gives
\[
\beta+t=\ell_0(D)>\ell_F(D),
\]
so
\[
\beta>\sqrt{5+t^2}-t. \tag{3}
\]

Combining (2) and (3),
\[
\beta>
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}.
\]
For every real \(t\),
\[
\max\left\{
\sqrt{1+t^2}+t,\,
\sqrt{5+t^2}-t
\right\}\ge\sqrt3.
\]
Indeed, if both terms were \(<\sqrt3\), the first inequality would imply \(t<1/\sqrt3\), while the second would imply \(t>1/\sqrt3\), a contradiction. Hence \(\beta>\sqrt3\).

This proves the lower bound.

---

### II. The upper bound: every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized

Fix \(\beta>\sqrt3\). We construct a \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation and a realizing map.

#### 1. A \(9\)-periodic triangulation of the strip

Set
\[
W=\frac{2\beta}{3},\qquad L=\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}.
\]
Since \(\beta>\sqrt3\), we have \(L<W\). Define
\[
\sigma=\frac{L+W}{2},\qquad \Delta=\frac{W-L}{2}.
\]
Thus
\[
\sigma=L+\Delta=W-\Delta,\qquad \Delta>0.
\]
Choose
\[
\lambda=\min\left\{\frac{\Delta}{40},\frac{\beta}{24}\right\}.
\]

Define a \(3\)-periodic sequence \((\ell_j)_{j\in\mathbb Z}\) by
\[
\ell_j=
\begin{cases}
W-2\lambda,& j\equiv2\pmod3,\\
\lambda,& j\not\equiv2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Every block of three consecutive \(\ell_j\)’s has sum \(W\), and every block of nine has sum \(3W=2\beta\).

Let \(c_0=0\) and define
\[
c_{j+1}-c_j=\ell_j,\qquad j\in\mathbb Z.
\]
Then
\[
c_{j+9}=c_j+2\beta.
\]

Define boundary vertices
\[
X_j=(c_j,0),\qquad Y_j=(c_{j+5}-\beta,1).
\]
For each \(j\), let
\[
Q_j=\operatorname{conv}\{X_j,X_{j+1},Y_{j+1},Y_j\}.
\]
At height \(\theta\in[0,1]\), the left and right sides of \(Q_j\) have \(x\)-coordinates
\[
x_j(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_j+\theta(c_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)=(1-\theta)c_{j+1}+\theta(c_{j+6}-\beta),
\]
and their difference is
\[
x_{j+1}(\theta)-x_j(\theta)
=(1-\theta)\ell_j+\theta\ell_{j+5}>0.
\]
Thus, for each fixed height \(\theta\), the intervals \([x_j(\theta),x_{j+1}(\theta)]\) tile the horizontal line. Since \(c_j\to\pm\infty\), the quadrilaterals \(Q_j\) tile \(\Sigma\) locally finitely.

Split \(Q_j\) along the diagonal \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), and define
\[
D_j=[X_j,X_{j+1},Y_j],
\qquad
E_j=[Y_j,Y_{j+1},X_{j+1}].
\]
Then
\[
\mathcal T=\{D_j,E_j:j\in\mathbb Z\}
\]
is a clean locally finite triangulation of \(\Sigma\).

Now
\[
G_\beta(X_j)=(c_j+\beta,1)=Y_{j+4},
\]
because \(Y_{j+4}=(c_{j+9}-\beta,1)=(c_j+\beta,1)\), and
\[
G_\beta(Y_j)=(c_{j+5},0)=X_{j+5}.
\]
Therefore
\[
G_\beta(D_j)=E_{j+4},\qquad G_\beta(E_j)=D_{j+5}.
\]
So \(\mathcal T\) is \(G_\beta\)-invariant.

In the quotient \(M_\beta=\Sigma/\langle G_\beta\rangle\), let \(v_j\) be the vertex represented by \(X_j\). Since \(G_\beta^2(X_j)=X_{j+9}\), the indices \(j\) are taken modulo \(9\). Also \(Y_j\) represents \(v_{j+5}\). Thus the quotient has nine triangular faces
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}],\qquad j\in\mathbb Z/9\mathbb Z.
\]

#### 2. The target vertices

Choose an equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\subset\mathbb R^2\times\{0\}\) of side length \(\sigma\), with centroid \(o\). Let
\[
R=|p_i-o|=\frac{\sigma}{\sqrt3}.
\]
Let \(e_3=(0,0,1)\). Choose
\[
\eta=\frac12\min\left\{\frac12,\frac{\lambda}{8R},\frac{\Delta}{16R}\right\},
\qquad
h=\frac12\min\left\{\frac{\lambda}{8},\frac{\Delta}{16}\right\}.
\]
Then
\[
\eta R\le\frac{\lambda}{16},\qquad h\le\frac{\lambda}{16},
\]
and
\[
\eta R\le\frac{\Delta}{32},\qquad h\le\frac{\Delta}{32}.
\]

For \(r>0\), define
\[
q_i(r,z)=o+r(p_i-o)+ze_3.
\]
Set
\[
A_i=q_i(1,0),\qquad M_i=q_i(1-\eta,h),\qquad C_i=q_i(1+\eta,2h).
\]
Define nine target vertices \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) by
\[
(P_0,\dots,P_8)
=
(C_0,M_0,A_0,A_1,C_1,M_1,M_2,A_2,C_2),
\]
and extend periodically by \(P_{j+9}=P_j\).

Define a quotient PL map
\[
\Phi:M_\beta\to\mathbb R^3
\]
by
\[
\Phi(v_j)=P_j
\]
and extending affinely over each face
\[
T_j=[v_j,v_{j+1},v_{j+5}].
\]
The nine image faces are
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mathcal F_0&=[C_0,M_0,M_1],&
\mathcal F_1&=[M_0,A_0,M_2],&
\mathcal F_2&=[A_0,A_1,A_2],\\
\mathcal F_3&=[A_1,C_1,C_2],&
\mathcal F_4&=[C_1,M_1,C_0],&
\mathcal F_5&=[M_1,M_2,M_0],\\
\mathcal F_6&=[M_2,A_2,A_0],&
\mathcal F_7&=[A_2,C_2,A_1],&
\mathcal F_8&=[C_2,C_0,C_1].
\end{aligned}
\]

#### 3. Embeddedness of the target Möbius band

Let
\[
H(r,z)=\operatorname{conv}\{q_0(r,z),q_1(r,z),q_2(r,z)\}
\]
be the horizontal triangle of scale \(r\) at height \(z\), and let \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) be the side of \(H(r,z)\) joining \(q_i(r,z)\) to \(q_j(r,z)\).

Use barycentric coordinates \((\lambda_0,\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\) with respect to the original equilateral triangle \(p_0p_1p_2\) in each horizontal plane. With
\[
a(r)=\frac{1-r}{3},
\]
we have
\[
H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge a(r)\text{ for }i=0,1,2\},
\]
and if \(\{i,j,k\}=\{0,1,2\}\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)=\{\lambda_k=a(r)\}\cap H(r,z).
\]
It follows that adjacent side segments \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) and \(S_{jk}(s,z)\) are disjoint if \(r\ne s\), and meet only at their common vertex if \(r=s\). Also, if \(r>s\), then
\[
S_{ij}(r,z)\cap H(s,z)=\varnothing.
\]

The three horizontal triangular faces are
\[
\mathcal F_2=H(1,0),\qquad
\mathcal F_5=H(1-\eta,h),\qquad
\mathcal F_8=H(1+\eta,2h).
\]
The remaining six faces form three trapezoidal side panels:
\[
\Pi_{01}=\mathcal F_0\cup\mathcal F_4,\qquad
\Pi_{12}=\mathcal F_3\cup\mathcal F_7,\qquad
\Pi_{20}=\mathcal F_1\cup\mathcal F_6.
\]
Their horizontal slices are
\[
\Pi_{01}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{01}\!\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad h\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
\[
\Pi_{12}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{12}\!\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le2h,
\]
and
\[
\Pi_{20}\cap\{z=\zeta\}
=
S_{20}\!\left(1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h},\zeta\right),
\qquad 0\le \zeta\le h.
\]

For \(0<\zeta<h\), only \(\Pi_{12}\) and \(\Pi_{20}\) occur. Their scales are
\[
1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}>1,\qquad
1-\eta\frac{\zeta}{h}<1,
\]
so the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

For \(h<\zeta<2h\), only \(\Pi_{01}\) and \(\Pi_{12}\) occur. Their scales satisfy
\[
\left(1+\eta\frac{\zeta}{2h}\right)
-
\left(1-\eta+2\eta\frac{\zeta-h}{h}\right)
=
\frac{3\eta}{2}\left(2-\frac{\zeta}{h}\right)>0,
\]
so again the corresponding adjacent side segments are disjoint.

At \(\zeta=0\), the triangle \(H(1,0)\) meets the panels exactly along the intended sides \(A_1A_2\) and \(A_2A_0\). At \(\zeta=h\), the triangle \(H(1-\eta,h)\) meets the panels exactly along \(M_0M_1\) and \(M_2M_0\); the extra slice of \(\Pi_{12}\) has scale \(1+\eta/2\), larger than \(1-\eta\), and is disjoint from the middle triangle and from the other two side slices. At \(\zeta=2h\), the triangle \(H(1+\eta,2h)\) meets the panels exactly along \(C_0C_1\) and \(C_1C_2\).

Each \(\Pi_{ij}\) is a convex planar trapezoid split by one diagonal, so the two triangles inside a panel meet only along that diagonal. The slice analysis shows that distinct panels and horizontal triangles have no unintended intersections. Thus the nine image triangles meet exactly according to the quotient complex. Hence \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

#### 4. The squeeze inequalities

Let the three clusters be
\[
\mathcal C_i=\{A_i,M_i,C_i\},\qquad i=0,1,2.
\]
If \(U_i,V_i\in\mathcal C_i\), then
\[
|U_iV_i|\le 2\eta R+2h\le\frac{\lambda}{4}<\frac{\lambda}{2}.
\]
If \(i\ne k\), \(U_i\in\mathcal C_i\), and \(V_k\in\mathcal C_k\), then \(U_iV_k\) differs from the original equilateral edge \(p_ip_k\) by a perturbation of norm at most \(2\eta R+2h\le\Delta/8\). Therefore
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}<|U_iV_k|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}. \tag{4}
\]

The cluster labels of \(P_0,\dots,P_8\) are
\[
0,0,0,1,1,1,2,2,2.
\]
Thus the boundary edge \(P_jP_{j+1}\) lies inside one cluster when \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), and joins two different clusters when \(j\equiv2\pmod3\).

The flat boundary edge \(v_jv_{j+1}\) has length \(\ell_j\). If \(j\not\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=\lambda\), while
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\frac{\lambda}{2}<\lambda.
\]
If \(j\equiv2\pmod3\), then \(\ell_j=W-2\lambda\), and by (4),
\[
|P_jP_{j+1}|<\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(W=\sigma+\Delta\) and \(2\lambda\le\Delta/20\),
\[
\sigma+\frac{\Delta}{4}<W-2\lambda=\ell_j.
\]
So every boundary edge is strictly shortened.

Now consider nonboundary edges. Let
\[
d_j=c_{j+5}-c_j.
\]
A direct computation from the \(3\)-periodic pattern gives
\[
d_j=
\begin{cases}
W+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
2W-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Since \(W=2\beta/3\),
\[
d_j-\beta=
\begin{cases}
-\beta/3+2\lambda,& j\equiv0\pmod3,\\
\beta/3-\lambda,& j\equiv1,2\pmod3.
\end{cases}
\]
Because \(\lambda<\beta/12\),
\[
|d_j-\beta|<\frac{\beta}{3}.
\]
Thus
\[
|X_jY_j|=\sqrt{1+(d_j-\beta)^2}<\sqrt{1+\frac{\beta^2}{9}}=L.
\]
The other nonboundary edge in \(D_j\), namely \(X_{j+1}Y_j\), has horizontal displacement
\[
c_{j+5}-\beta-c_{j+1}=-(d_{j+5}-\beta),
\]
so it also has length \(<L\). These edge types account for all nonboundary edges of the triangles \(D_j,E_j\).

Every target nonboundary edge is of the form \(P_kP_{k+5}\), up to reversing orientation, and therefore joins two different clusters. By (4),
\[
|P_kP_{k+5}|>\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}.
\]
Since \(\sigma=L+\Delta\),
\[
\sigma-\frac{\Delta}{4}=L+\frac{3\Delta}{4}>L.
\]
Thus every nonboundary edge is strictly lengthened. Therefore \(\Phi\) is a squeeze map on every quotient triangle.

#### 5. The lifted realizing map

Define \(f:\Sigma\to\mathbb R^3\) on vertices by
\[
f(X_j)=P_j,\qquad f(Y_j)=P_{j+5},
\]
with indices on the \(P_j\)’s taken modulo \(9\). Extend \(f\) affinely over every triangle \(D_j,E_j\). The definitions agree on common edges, so \(f\) is continuous and PL.

Moreover,
\[
f(G_\beta X_j)=f(Y_{j+4})=P_{j+9}=P_j=f(X_j),
\]
and
\[
f(G_\beta Y_j)=f(X_{j+5})=P_{j+5}=f(Y_j).
\]
Since \(G_\beta\) sends triangles affinely to triangles, these vertex equalities imply
\[
f\circ G_\beta=f
\]
on all of \(\Sigma\). Hence \(f\) descends to the embedded quotient map \(\Phi\).

Therefore, for \(p,q\in\Sigma\),
\[
f(p)=f(q)
\Longleftrightarrow
\Phi([p])=\Phi([q])
\Longleftrightarrow
[p]=[q]
\Longleftrightarrow
p=G_\beta^k(q)\ \text{for some }k\in\mathbb Z.
\]
The squeeze inequalities above show that \(f\) restricts to a squeeze map on every triangle of the \(G_\beta\)-invariant clean triangulation \(\mathcal T\). Thus \(\beta\) is realized.

We have proved that every \(\beta>\sqrt3\) is realized, and the lower bound proved that no \(\beta\le\sqrt3\) is realized. Hence the realized set is exactly
\[
(\sqrt3,\infty),
\]
and its infimum is \(\sqrt3\). \(\square\)

<PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>
1. **Refinement patch.**  
For each original triangle \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), let \(V_\tau\) be the set of all triangulation vertices lying on its boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\). Since \([r_0,r_1]\) is compact and the triangulation is locally finite, \(V_\tau\) is finite. Subdivide \([r_0,r_1]\) at these vertices and cone the resulting subintervals to \(a\). Lemma 1 shows that each new boundary subedge is strictly shortened and each new cevian side is strictly lengthened, so \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every refined triangle. \(G_\beta\)-invariance is preserved because \(G_\beta\) maps boundary vertices to boundary vertices and carries coning subdivisions to coning subdivisions. Local finiteness follows because every compact set meets only finitely many original triangles and each such triangle is subdivided finitely many times. After this subdivision, any two refined triangles meet face-to-face: boundary overlaps have been subdivided at all relevant endpoints; transverse crossings of nonboundary edges would force overlapping interiors; and two collinear nonboundary edges cannot overlap properly because a line meeting both boundary components \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) has unique endpoints on them.

2. **Finite quotient triangulation patch.**  
Let \(K=[0,\beta]\times[0,1]\). Local finiteness implies that \(K\) meets only finitely many refined closed triangles. Every triangle has some deck translate meeting \(K\), so there are finitely many triangle orbits. If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on a refined triangle \(\Delta\), its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), producing arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k q\) with \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f|_\Delta\) is injective. Hence \(\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta=\varnothing\) for \(k\ne0\), because otherwise injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) would force a fixed point of a nontrivial deck transformation. Therefore the quotient is a finite regular clean triangulation of \(M_\beta\). Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc can be subdivided into finitely many subarcs lying in boundary edges of the quotient triangulation, and each such subarc is strictly shortened by affine contraction, so the whole arc is strictly shortened.

3. **Leaf graph patch.**  
For the quotient triangulation, each triangle contributes one closed interval of cevians. Endpoints of such intervals correspond to nonboundary edges, and every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every leaf-graph vertex has valence \(2\). Thus the quotient leaf graph is a finite disjoint union of circles. If \(C\) is a component, let \(U_C\) be the union of triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan in which consecutive non-extreme triangles share nonboundary edges, so all incident triangles also lie in the same \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is closed because the quotient triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, there is only one component, so the quotient leaf graph is \(S^1\). The same open-closed argument upstairs, using local finiteness for closedness, shows that the lifted leaf graph is connected. The projection of triangulations induces a covering of leaf graphs. No nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) fixes a lifted leaf, as checked by comparing its bottom and top endpoints. Therefore the covering has infinite degree; a connected infinite-degree cover of \(S^1\) is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).

4. **Continuity of \(L_s\) patch.**  
Inside each triangle, the endpoints of the cevians vary continuously and \(f\) is affine, so the oriented image segment varies continuously. At a glued endpoint of two cevian intervals, the limiting leaf is the common nonboundary edge, and both adjacent triangles give the same oriented image segment because the orientation is always from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Thus \(s\mapsto L_s\) is a continuous path of oriented affine lines. Since \(G_\beta\) interchanges bottom and top endpoints and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), one has \(L_{s+1}=L_s^{\mathrm{opp}}\), as required for Lemma 2.

5. **Embedded target patch.**  
Each panel \(\Pi_{ij}\) is the convex hull of two parallel horizontal side segments \(S_{ij}(r_0,z_0)\) and \(S_{ij}(r_1,z_1)\); hence it is a planar convex trapezoid, and its two triangular halves meet exactly along the chosen diagonal. In barycentric coordinates, \(H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\}\), and \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) is given by \(\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\). If two adjacent side slices have different scales, they are disjoint; if their scales agree, they meet only at their common endpoint. The listed slice formulas show that for every horizontal level \(z=\zeta\), the intersection of the target surface with that level consists only of the intended disjoint or endpoint-sharing segments. At \(\zeta=0,h,2h\), the horizontal triangles meet the panels exactly along the intended sides, with the extra panel slice at \(\zeta=h\) disjoint because it has larger scale. Since any unintended intersection would occur in some horizontal slice, the nine triangles meet exactly according to the quotient complex. Thus \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

6. **Cross-cluster perturbation patch.**  
For \(U_i\in\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\), write
\[
U_i=p_i+\alpha_i(p_i-o)+z_i e_3,
\qquad |\alpha_i|\le \eta,\quad z_i\in\{0,h,2h\}.
\]
For \(V_k\in\{A_k,M_k,C_k\}\), \(i\ne k\), similarly
\[
V_k=p_k+\alpha_k(p_k-o)+z_k e_3.
\]
Then
\[
V_k-U_i=(p_k-p_i)+\alpha_k(p_k-o)-\alpha_i(p_i-o)+(z_k-z_i)e_3,
\]
so the perturbation from the original equilateral edge has norm at most
\[
2\eta R+2h\le \Delta/8.
\]
Hence
\[
\bigl||U_iV_k|-\sigma\bigr|\le \Delta/8<\Delta/4,
\]
which justifies inequality (4). The remaining boundary-shortening and nonboundary-lengthening inequalities then follow exactly as written.
</PATCHES_REQUIRED_FOR_CORRECTNESS>

parsed.minor_gaps:
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: The face-to-face refinement of the initial clean triangulation is only sketched; local finiteness, preservation of the squeeze property, and elimination of boundary edge-overlap incidences should be written out.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: The passage from the infinite strip triangulation to a finite regular quotient triangulation of the Möbius band uses standard PL-covering facts but needs a few details: finite orbit representatives, no self-identifications of closed cells, and strict shortening of arbitrary boundary arcs.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: The cevian leaf graph argument is plausible but compressed; one should explicitly prove that the quotient leaf graph is a single circle and that the lifted leaf graph is its infinite connected cover, hence homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: The construction of the continuous path of oriented image support lines \(L_s\) needs an explicit continuity check across glued nonboundary edges.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: The embeddedness proof for the nine target triangles in the upper-bound construction needs a more explicit verification that the side panels are planar convex trapezoids and that the horizontal-slice argument excludes all unintended intersections.
- ROUTINE PATCH REQUIRED: The perturbation estimate for cross-cluster target edges should be written explicitly to justify inequality (4).

PATCHES REQUIRED FOR CORRECTNESS:
1. **Refinement patch.**  
For each original triangle \(\tau=\operatorname{conv}\{a,r_0,r_1\}\), let \(V_\tau\) be the set of all triangulation vertices lying on its boundary edge \([r_0,r_1]\). Since \([r_0,r_1]\) is compact and the triangulation is locally finite, \(V_\tau\) is finite. Subdivide \([r_0,r_1]\) at these vertices and cone the resulting subintervals to \(a\). Lemma 1 shows that each new boundary subedge is strictly shortened and each new cevian side is strictly lengthened, so \(f\) is still a squeeze map on every refined triangle. \(G_\beta\)-invariance is preserved because \(G_\beta\) maps boundary vertices to boundary vertices and carries coning subdivisions to coning subdivisions. Local finiteness follows because every compact set meets only finitely many original triangles and each such triangle is subdivided finitely many times. After this subdivision, any two refined triangles meet face-to-face: boundary overlaps have been subdivided at all relevant endpoints; transverse crossings of nonboundary edges would force overlapping interiors; and two collinear nonboundary edges cannot overlap properly because a line meeting both boundary components \(y=0\) and \(y=1\) has unique endpoints on them.

2. **Finite quotient triangulation patch.**  
Let \(K=[0,\beta]\times[0,1]\). Local finiteness implies that \(K\) meets only finitely many refined closed triangles. Every triangle has some deck translate meeting \(K\), so there are finitely many triangle orbits. If \(f|_\Delta\) were noninjective on a refined triangle \(\Delta\), its affine linear part would have rank \(<2\), producing arbitrarily close distinct points \(p,q\in\Delta\) with \(f(p)=f(q)\). The realization condition would force \(p=G_\beta^k q\) with \(k\ne0\), impossible for \(|p-q|<\beta\). Thus \(f|_\Delta\) is injective. Hence \(\Delta\cap G_\beta^k\Delta=\varnothing\) for \(k\ne0\), because otherwise injectivity of \(f|_\Delta\) would force a fixed point of a nontrivial deck transformation. Therefore the quotient is a finite regular clean triangulation of \(M_\beta\). Finally, every nontrivial boundary arc can be subdivided into finitely many subarcs lying in boundary edges of the quotient triangulation, and each such subarc is strictly shortened by affine contraction, so the whole arc is strictly shortened.

3. **Leaf graph patch.**  
For the quotient triangulation, each triangle contributes one closed interval of cevians. Endpoints of such intervals correspond to nonboundary edges, and every nonboundary edge is incident to exactly two triangles, so every leaf-graph vertex has valence \(2\). Thus the quotient leaf graph is a finite disjoint union of circles. If \(C\) is a component, let \(U_C\) be the union of triangles whose cevian intervals lie in \(C\). Across a nonboundary edge, adjacent triangles lie in the same \(U_C\). Around a boundary vertex, the incident triangles form a finite fan in which consecutive non-extreme triangles share nonboundary edges, so all incident triangles also lie in the same \(U_C\). Hence \(U_C\) is open. It is closed because the quotient triangulation is finite. Since \(M_\beta\) is connected, there is only one component, so the quotient leaf graph is \(S^1\). The same open-closed argument upstairs, using local finiteness for closedness, shows that the lifted leaf graph is connected. The projection of triangulations induces a covering of leaf graphs. No nontrivial \(G_\beta^k\) fixes a lifted leaf, as checked by comparing its bottom and top endpoints. Therefore the covering has infinite degree; a connected infinite-degree cover of \(S^1\) is homeomorphic to \(\mathbb R\).

4. **Continuity of \(L_s\) patch.**  
Inside each triangle, the endpoints of the cevians vary continuously and \(f\) is affine, so the oriented image segment varies continuously. At a glued endpoint of two cevian intervals, the limiting leaf is the common nonboundary edge, and both adjacent triangles give the same oriented image segment because the orientation is always from \(y=0\) to \(y=1\). Thus \(s\mapsto L_s\) is a continuous path of oriented affine lines. Since \(G_\beta\) interchanges bottom and top endpoints and \(f\circ G_\beta=f\), one has \(L_{s+1}=L_s^{\mathrm{opp}}\), as required for Lemma 2.

5. **Embedded target patch.**  
Each panel \(\Pi_{ij}\) is the convex hull of two parallel horizontal side segments \(S_{ij}(r_0,z_0)\) and \(S_{ij}(r_1,z_1)\); hence it is a planar convex trapezoid, and its two triangular halves meet exactly along the chosen diagonal. In barycentric coordinates, \(H(r,z)=\{\lambda_i\ge (1-r)/3\}\), and \(S_{ij}(r,z)\) is given by \(\lambda_k=(1-r)/3\). If two adjacent side slices have different scales, they are disjoint; if their scales agree, they meet only at their common endpoint. The listed slice formulas show that for every horizontal level \(z=\zeta\), the intersection of the target surface with that level consists only of the intended disjoint or endpoint-sharing segments. At \(\zeta=0,h,2h\), the horizontal triangles meet the panels exactly along the intended sides, with the extra panel slice at \(\zeta=h\) disjoint because it has larger scale. Since any unintended intersection would occur in some horizontal slice, the nine triangles meet exactly according to the quotient complex. Thus \(\Phi\) is an embedded PL Möbius band.

6. **Cross-cluster perturbation patch.**  
For \(U_i\in\{A_i,M_i,C_i\}\), write
\[
U_i=p_i+\alpha_i(p_i-o)+z_i e_3,
\qquad |\alpha_i|\le \eta,\quad z_i\in\{0,h,2h\}.
\]
For \(V_k\in\{A_k,M_k,C_k\}\), \(i\ne k\), similarly
\[
V_k=p_k+\alpha_k(p_k-o)+z_k e_3.
\]
Then
\[
V_k-U_i=(p_k-p_i)+\alpha_k(p_k-o)-\alpha_i(p_i-o)+(z_k-z_i)e_3,
\]
so the perturbation from the original equilateral edge has norm at most
\[
2\eta R+2h\le \Delta/8.
\]
Hence
\[
\bigl||U_iV_k|-\sigma\bigr|\le \Delta/8<\Delta/4,
\]
which justifies inequality (4). The remaining boundary-shortening and nonboundary-lengthening inequalities then follow exactly as written.
